- **feat(connectors): slice 655 — BambooHR webhook multi-employee fan-out.** Extends the slice-573 BambooHR `subscribe` receiver so a webhook delivery carrying **multiple** changed employees in `employees[]` re-reads (`FetchOne`, minimal-field) + pushes a `hris.worker_lifecycle.v1` record for **each** changed employee — not just the first (resolving slice 573's decisions-log Revisit #3). The shared `PayloadParser` contract changes from `ParseWorkerID(body) (string, bool, error)` to `ParseWorkerIDs(body) ([]string, error)`: the BambooHR parser returns every changed employee's id; the **Rippling parser returns its single affected worker as a one-element slice**, so the Rippling single-worker path is byte-for-byte unchanged (the receiver's fan-out loop is a no-op for it). **Signature verification stays the FIRST step, exactly ONCE per delivery, BEFORE any fan-out** — a forged multi-worker delivery is rejected `401` before _any_ employee is re-read or pushed (test-asserted; P0 anti-criterion). Each per-employee re-read uses the **same minimal `fields` set / slice-491 over-collection guard** as slice 573 — no new field, no full-PII scope. **Dedup unchanged:** each fanned-out record uses `idem.WorkerLifecycleKey` (per `worker_id` + UTC hour), so a webhook fan-out and a later pull for the same worker still collapse in the append-only ledger. **Partial-failure honesty:** if one employee's re-read or push fails mid-fan-out, the healthy employees are still pushed and the delivery returns `502` (so the vendor retries the whole delivery; the already-pushed records dedup-collapse on the retry) — one bad employee never silently drops the delivery or fails the others. The receiver de-duplicates repeated ids within a delivery and bounds the fan-out at `webhook.MaxFanOut` (100 distinct workers). **Invariant #3 honored — push only; ZERO `internal/api/` changes:** the receiver stays source-side in the connector; no migration, no new evidence kind, no `schemaregistry`/`DefaultSeed` change (reuses `hris.worker_lifecycle.v1`). **Type: JUDGMENT** — the parser-contract change shape (single-id → `[]id` + how Rippling stays single), the per-employee partial-failure behavior, and the per-delivery fan-out cap are recorded in `docs/audit-log/655-bamboohr-webhook-fanout-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 573. Slice doc: `docs/issues/655-bamboohr-webhook-multi-employee-fan-out.md`. (closes #655)
- **feat(connectors): slice 573 — HRIS event-driven termination-webhook profile (Rippling + BambooHR).** Adds the `subscribe` profile to both HRIS connectors (`profiles_supported` becomes `[pull, subscribe]`) — a **source-side webhook receiver** that runs **inside the connector process** (`atlas-rippling subscribe` / `atlas-bamboohr subscribe`), narrowing the deprovisioning-evidence freshness from "up to 24h stale" to near-real-time without polling more often. The receiver (new shared package `connectors/hris/webhook`) receives the vendor's termination / status-change webhook delivery, **verifies the per-vendor HMAC-SHA256 signature BEFORE any work** (`X-Rippling-Signature` / `X-BambooHR-Signature`, constant-time compare via the reusable `webhook.HMACVerifier`; an unsigned, forged, or wrong-signature delivery is rejected with `401` and produces no record; an oversized body is rejected with `413` via `http.MaxBytesReader` before it is read), treats the webhook as a **trigger** and re-reads the affected worker's **minimal lifecycle fields** via the same read-only API (new `GetWorker`/`FetchOne` on each vendor's client — the slice-491 over-collection guard is unchanged: SSN / compensation / address / bank / benefits / performance / DOB / personal-contact stay excluded), builds the **same** `hris.worker_lifecycle.v1` record (`workerrecord.Build`, reused unchanged), and emits it via the existing `Push` API. **Invariant #3 honored — the platform-side wire stays push:** the receiver is part of the connector, not the platform; no `internal/api/` endpoint, no migration, no new evidence kind, no `schemaregistry` change. **Dedup against pull:** the webhook-emitted record and a pull-emitted record for the same worker in the same UTC hour derive the **same idempotency key** (`idem.WorkerLifecycleKey`, reused unchanged), so the append-only ledger collapses them — a termination is never double-written via both a webhook and a subsequent poll. The receiver is a bounded long-lived `http.Server` (gosec-G112 timeouts; SIGINT/SIGTERM-aware graceful shutdown; loopback bind by default, front with a reverse proxy for TLS). Honest naming throughout: the profile is **event-driven via the vendor webhook**, explicitly **NOT "continuous monitoring"** (P0-491-6). **Type: JUDGMENT** — the per-vendor signature scheme (+ the live-tenant-confirmation assumption), the trigger-vs-re-read call, the dedup-key reuse, and the receiver lifecycle/bind defaults are recorded in `docs/audit-log/573-hris-termination-webhook-decisions.md`. Spillover: slice **655** (BambooHR multi-employee webhook fan-out). Slice doc: `docs/issues/573-hris-event-driven-termination-webhook-profile.md`. (closes #573)
- **feat(catalog): slice 654 — validate schema `x-default-scf-anchors` against the bundled catalog (+ remap existing dangling anchors).** Adds a fast Go unit guard (`internal/api/schemaregistry/default_anchor_catalog_test.go`, no Postgres) that loads every embedded evidence-kind schema via the same `LoadPlatformSchemas(PlatformSchemasFS())` loader the atlas server boots with, collects each schema's `x-default-scf-anchors` hint, and asserts every anchor resolves to an anchor present in the bundled SCF catalog fixture (`migrations/fixtures/scf-sample.json`, parsed via the canonical `scfimport.Load`). A negative sub-test feeds a deliberately-dangling `ZZZ-99` (plus the historical real-but-absent `MON-02` / `IRO-02`) and asserts the existence helper rejects them, so the guard can never pass vacuously. This is the slice-068 control-bundle anchor-drift guard's sibling on a different surface (schema hints vs. control-bundle anchors); the two do not overlap. **Run as the authoritative scan, the guard surfaced 18 schemas (not the spec's 3) carrying 12 distinct dangling anchor codes** (`IAC-09`, `IAC-10`, `IAC-17`, `IAC-18`, `IAC-22`, `DCH-06`, `MON-02`, `TDA-06`, `TDA-09`, `IRO-02`, `IRO-07`, `IRO-13`) — all the same class, all remapped in this PR to present, semantically-closest anchors (each grep-verified present, de-duplicated, every resulting list non-empty): e.g. `IAC-22`/`IAC-09` → `IAC-07` (User Provisioning & Lifecycle) or dropped where `IAC-21` already co-listed; `IRO-02` → `IRO-09`; `MON-02` → `MON-01`; `IAC-17`/`IAC-18` → `IAC-15` (Account Review); `TDA-09` → `TDA-01`. Five `description` fields that cited a removed anchor by name were updated to name the present target and note the fixture-absence (the pattern slice 535 already used for `monitoring.alert_firing`). **No evidence-record DATA field changed** — only the `x-default-scf-anchors` hint lists + the documenting prose (anti-criterion P0). **No semver bump:** `default_scf_anchors` is a stored column, not part of the `(kind, semver)` schema identity, and no record consumes the author-time hint (AC-4). **OQ #16/#17 consistency:** this guard is the EXISTENCE floor (mechanical: does the anchor resolve to a real catalog entry); the maintainer's manual review remains the ACCURACY checkpoint (semantic: is this the RIGHT anchor) — complementary, and the guard catches the dangling class the manual checkpoint demonstrably missed. **Type: JUDGMENT** — the per-anchor remap targets, the bundled-fixture catalog source (AC-3; catalog expansion is a separate governance call, deliberately not done), and the no-semver-bump call are recorded in `docs/audit-log/654-schema-default-anchor-catalog-validation-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/654-schema-default-anchor-catalog-validation.md`. (closes #654)

- **feat(connectors): slice 535 — monitoring alert-firing-history (Datadog + Grafana).** Adds the firing-history sibling of slice 488's `monitoring.alert_config.v1` config-inventory kind: a new vendor-neutral evidence kind `monitoring.alert_firing.v1` emitted by **both** the Datadog and Grafana connectors — one record per firing event (`source_vendor`, `rule_id`, `state` [alerting/resolved/no_data/pending], `fired_at`, `resolved_at`, and the routing-target **handle**). Slice 488 reads which alerts are _configured_ (SOC 2 CC7.2); this reads what actually _fired_ and resolved (CC7.3/CC7.4 — the entity evaluates events and responds). A shared `connectors/monitoring/firing` normalization layer (`firing.Collect` / state folding / email-handle PII drop / `Firing` + `RawFiring` structs) + a shared `connectors/monitoring/firingrecord` builder (mirroring `alertcfg`/`monrecord`) + a new `idem.AlertFiringKey` keyed on `(vendor, rule_id, fired_at)` so overlapping-window re-reads do not double-write. **Datadog** (`connectors/datadog/internal/firingevents`): bounded pull of monitor-alert events via the read-only Events API (`GET /api/v1/events?sources=monitor_alert`, new `events_read` scope) with a bounded time-windowed page loop (`maxPages=50`×`pageLimit=100` ⇒ 5,000 events, 60s timeout, `--datadog-firing-lookback` default 24h). **Grafana** (`connectors/grafana/internal/alerthistory`): bounded pull of alert-instance state-history via the read-only state-history API (`GET /api/v1/rules/history`, Viewer role) capped at `maxTransitions=5000` (`--grafana-firing-lookback` default 24h). **Profile JUDGMENT: bounded pull, honestly named** — neither vendor source offers a first-class push the connector receives (Datadog Events API + Grafana state-history are both query surfaces), so `profiles_supported=[pull]` is accurate; no "continuous monitoring"/"event-driven" mislabel (P0). **Information Disclosure (DOMINANT) structural guard:** the `firing.RawFiring`/`Firing` structs physically cannot hold the alert message body, the triggering metric values, the secret webhook URL, or recipient PII — a reflection guard (`TestStructuralOverCollectionGuard`) fails the build if a forbidden field is added, and per-vendor drop tests feed a source event carrying all four and assert none reaches a record. **DoS (load-bearing):** bounded per-run window + event/transition cap + run timeout on both vendors. No platform-side wire change (push only — invariant #3); read-only least-privilege source scope per vendor; no source key/token logged. Registered in `DefaultSeed` with schema `monitoring.alert_firing/1.0.0.json`; anchors **MON-01 + IRO-09** (the spec candidate IRO-02 is absent from the bundled SCF fixture, so IRO-09 — the closest present incident anchor — is used; see decisions log D3). **Type: JUDGMENT** — the profile-shape call, the `(vendor, rule_id, fired_at)` dedup key, the anchor substitution, and the firing-events-only structural guard are recorded in `docs/audit-log/535-monitoring-alert-firing-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 488. Slice doc: `docs/issues/535-monitoring-alert-firing-history-event-driven-profile.md`. (closes #535)
- **feat(connectors): slice 652 — Kubernetes admission-webhook + policy-engine evidence.** Adds two evidence kinds to the Kubernetes connector (slice 487) — `k8s.admission_webhook.v1` + `k8s.admission_policy.v1` — the "is hardening enforced beyond namespace PSS labels?" surfaces an auditor asks about once PSS labels (slice 524) are covered. Unlike slice 524 (which reused the held `namespaces` grant), this slice **deliberately adds new read-only ClusterRole rules** — the **flagged scope expansion** it owns. A new `connectors/k8s/internal/admission` collector mirrors the slice-487/524/525 pattern (`admission.CollectWebhooks` / `admission.CollectPolicies` ↔ `pss.Assess`, `admission.WebhookClient` / `admission.PolicyClient` over the shared slice-621 `k8slist.ListAll` paginating reader + the slice-622 `Reader.Probe` CRD-presence probe, `idem.AdmissionWebhookKey` / `idem.AdmissionPolicyKey`, `buildAdmissionWebhookRecord` / `buildAdmissionPolicyRecord`). **Admission webhooks** (`k8s.admission_webhook.v1`): the new `admissionregistration.k8s.io` `validatingwebhookconfigurations` + `mutatingwebhookconfigurations` `get,list` rule (`k8sauth.AdmissionWebhookRule`). Per webhook entry the record carries CONFIG metadata — `webhook_kind` (validating/mutating), config + entry names, `failure_policy` + derived `fail_closed`, `side_effects`, `has_namespace_selector` / `has_object_selector` (selector **presence**, never the match expressions), `target_service` (the dispatch `namespace/name`, never the raw URL), and the deduped+sorted `intercepted_resources` / `intercepted_operations` sets. **Third-party policy engines** (`k8s.admission_policy.v1`): OPA/Gatekeeper (`templates.gatekeeper.sh constrainttemplates`) and Kyverno (`kyverno.io clusterpolicies` + `policies`), each detected by an **API-discovery probe** so an **absent engine is never an error** (the slice-622 pattern). Per policy the record carries `engine`, `policy_name`, `scope` (cluster/namespaced) + `namespace`, `policy_kind`, and `enforcement_action` + derived `enforcing`. **JUDGMENT — THE load-bearing config-metadata-only guard:** the webhook `.clientConfig.caBundle` / TLS client key and the policy Rego/CEL decision-logic body physically **cannot** enter a record. Enforced **structurally**: (1) the `RawWebhook` / `Webhook` / `RawPolicy` / `Policy` structs have no caBundle / TLS / body field; (2) the webhook client does not model `caBundle` / `url` / any TLS field, and the policy client does not model the Gatekeeper template `targets[]` (Rego) or the Kyverno `spec.rules[]` (CEL/JMESPath) — the json decoder discards them; (3) a reflection guard (`TestStruct_ConfigMetadataOnly_NoBodyOrTLSFields`) FAILS THE BUILD on any field name hinting at a caBundle / TLS key / rule body / intercepted payload; (4) `TestReduceWebhook_DropsCABundleAndURL` + `TestPolicyDecode_DropsRegoAndCELBody` feed fixtures with a REAL caBundle + URL + TLS key and a REAL Rego + CEL body, serialize the whole record, and assert none of those markers ever appears. **JUDGMENT — the ClusterRole grant:** `k8sauth.AdmissionWebhookRule()` adds the one webhook get/list rule; `k8sauth.PolicyEngineRules()` adds the optional `templates.gatekeeper.sh` + `kyverno.io` get/list rules; `AdmissionEvidenceClusterRole()` = base + webhook + policy rules. Every new rule stays `get,list`-only, names its resources EXPLICITLY (no wildcard), and never touches `secrets` — pinned by three new least-privilege tests; the base `DocumentedClusterRole()` is unchanged. `permissions --admission` prints the role WITH the new rules. **JUDGMENT — kind shape (D1):** TWO sibling kinds, not one combined kind (webhook configs and policy-engine policies have disjoint field sets — the slice-488→533 sibling-split precedent). **JUDGMENT — Gatekeeper catalog-only (D5):** v0 reads the static, NAMED `constrainttemplates` (which policies are DEFINED) and leaves the Gatekeeper `enforcement_action` unset — reading each constraint's action would require a **wildcard grant** over the dynamic per-template constraint CRDs, deliberately avoided to honor the no-wildcard anti-criterion (Kyverno's resources are static, so its action is read directly). **JUDGMENT — anchors:** both kinds `x-default-scf-anchors=[CFG-02]` — verified present in the bundled SCF catalog (the same configuration-hardening anchor the PSS + workload kinds use); `--webhook-control` / `--policy-control` default to `scf:CFG-02`. **Anti-criteria (P0):** push only — no platform-side wire change (invariant #3); `profiles_supported=[pull]`; config metadata only — never a caBundle / TLS key / Rego/CEL body / intercepted payload (the structural guard); `get,list` only — no write verb, no wildcard, no `secrets`; the `pull` interval is named honestly (NOT continuous monitoring). Both records are **descriptive** (`INCONCLUSIVE`); the evaluator owns the fail-open / coverage call. Both kinds run by default in `run` (no opt-in flag — they read no Secret material), skippable via `--skip-admission-webhooks` / `--skip-admission-policies`. sha256 content-hash per record; hour-truncated `observed_at`; register-per-run; stable `actor_id=connector:k8s:admission-webhook@<version>` / `connector:k8s:admission-policy@<version>`. Schemas (`k8s.admission_webhook/1.0.0.json` + `k8s.admission_policy/1.0.0.json`, `additionalProperties:false`, NO caBundle/body property) registered in `DefaultSeed` (the slice-068 drift bijection passes). Per-run caps (`maxWebhooks=5000`, `maxPolicies=20000`). Neutral fixtures only (`test-cabundle-should-be-dropped` / `test-rego-body-should-be-dropped`; no vendor-shaped PEM/cert literals). New `connectors/k8s/internal/admission` coverage floor (94; measured 94.6%). No migration, no new tenant-scoped table, no RLS change. **Type: JUDGMENT** — the kind shape, Gatekeeper/Kyverno inclusion, the structural config-only guard, the wildcard-free Gatekeeper catalog-only call, the new ClusterRole rules, the anchor choice, and detection-tier are recorded in `docs/audit-log/652-k8s-admission-webhook-policy-engine-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 524. Slice doc: `docs/issues/652-k8s-admission-webhook-policy-engine-evidence.md`. (#652)

- **feat(connectors): slice 525 — Kubernetes Secret-inventory (metadata-only) evidence.** Adds a fifth evidence kind `k8s.secret_inventory.v1` to the Kubernetes connector (slice 487) — and is the **one deliberate exception** to the connector family's no-Secrets rule. Slice 487 withheld all Secret access (the base ClusterRole does not grant `secrets`); this slice adds, **OPT-IN** (`run --collect-secret-inventory`, off by default), a Secret **metadata** inventory: per Secret the `type` (`Opaque` / `kubernetes.io/tls` / `kubernetes.io/service-account-token` / ...), namespace, name, creation timestamp + derived `age_days`, `key_count`, and the **NAMES** of the keys under `.data` (the map keys, e.g. `tls.crt` / `tls.key` / `token`). The auditable question is "how many TLS secrets / SA tokens exist, where, and how old" (rotation + sprawl signals), never the contents. A new `connectors/k8s/internal/secretmeta` collector mirrors the slice-487/523/524 pattern (`secretmeta.Collect` ↔ `pss.Assess`, `secretmeta.Client` ↔ `pss.Client` over the shared slice-621 `k8slist.ListAll` paginating reader, `idem.SecretInventoryKey`, `buildSecretMetaRecord`). **JUDGMENT — THE load-bearing metadata-only guard (Information Disclosure is the PRIMARY threat because the ClusterRole now reaches `secrets`):** a Secret VALUE (`.data` / `.stringData`, raw or base64) physically **cannot** enter a record. Enforced **structurally**, not procedurally: (1) the `RawSecret` / `Inventory` structs have no value field (`KeyNames []string` holds the `.data` MAP KEYS only); (2) the client decode models `.data` as `map[string]json.RawMessage` and `reduce()` reads only the map KEYS — the base64 values are never decoded, never copied into a record-bound field, and `.stringData` is not modeled at all (the json decoder discards it); (3) a reflection guard (`TestStruct_MetadataOnly_NoValueBearingFields`) FAILS THE BUILD if any field name hints at a value / data / content / payload surface (`KeyNames` allow-listed — names, not values); (4) `TestReduce_DropsSecretValues` + `TestClient_NoSecretValueReachesRecord` feed a fixture Secret with REAL `.data` (base64), `.stringData` (plaintext), and a secret-bearing annotation, serialize the whole record, and assert NONE of those markers — base64, the base64-DECODED form, or the plaintext — appears anywhere. **JUDGMENT — the ClusterRole grant (AC-4):** `k8sauth.SecretsRule()` adds EXACTLY one rule — core `secrets` verbs `get,list` — and `SecretInventoryClusterRole()` = base `DocumentedClusterRole()` + that one rule. The base role is UNCHANGED and still excludes `secrets` (the existing least-privilege test still asserts it); two new tests pin that the inventory role adds exactly the one secrets get/list rule, introduces no wildcard, and grants no write verb. The `permissions --secret-inventory` subcommand prints the role WITH the grant (loudly documented as the one access the base connector withheld); the default `permissions` output still withholds it. **JUDGMENT — anchors:** `x-default-scf-anchors=[CRY-01, CRY-09]`. The spec's candidate `IAC-22` is **absent** from the bundled SCF catalog (a dangling anchor fails the slice-068 drift guard); `CRY-01` ("Use of Cryptographic Controls") + `CRY-09` ("Cryptographic Key Management" — the closest real anchor for the secret-material rotation/sprawl signal) are both present and resolve. `--secret-control` defaults to `scf:CRY-01`. **JUDGMENT — opt-in (D5):** unlike the other four kinds (on by default, individually skippable), this kind is off by default and individually enabled — collecting Secret objects is an explicit, auditable operator decision that lines up 1:1 with the explicit ClusterRole change. **Anti-criteria (P0):** push only — no platform-side wire change (invariant #3); metadata only — no Secret values (the structural guard); exactly `secrets` get,list — no write verb, no wildcard; the `pull` profile is named honestly (NOT continuous monitoring). The record is **descriptive** (`INCONCLUSIVE`) — an inventory signal, not a verdict; the evaluator owns any rotation policy call. sha256 content-hash per record; hour-truncated `observed_at`; register-per-run; stable `actor_id=connector:k8s:secretmeta@<version>`; per-Secret `namespace` scope dimension. The schema (`k8s.secret_inventory/1.0.0.json`, `additionalProperties:false`, NO value property) is registered in `DefaultSeed` (the slice-068 drift bijection passes). A per-run cap (`secretmeta.maxSecrets=20000`) bounds the inventory. Neutral fixtures only (obviously-fake `base64("test-secret-value-should-be-dropped")` + plaintext markers; no vendor-shaped literals). New `connectors/k8s/internal/secretmeta` coverage floor (96; measured 98.0%). No migration, no new tenant-scoped table, no RLS change. **Type: JUDGMENT** — the field shape, the structural metadata-only guard, the single-rule ClusterRole grant, the anchor choice (CRY-01/CRY-09 vs the dangling IAC-22), the opt-in decision, and detection-tier are recorded in `docs/audit-log/525-k8s-secret-inventory-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 487. Slice doc: `docs/issues/525-k8s-secret-inventory-metadata-evidence.md`. (#525)
- **feat(connectors): slice 636 — Datadog Cloud-SIEM signal-history (triage outcomes).** Adds a third evidence kind to the Datadog connector, `datadog.siem_signal.v1`, the CC7.3 sibling of slice 533's `datadog.siem_rule.v1`. Slice 533 reads detection-rule **configuration** (SOC 2 CC7.2 — which rules exist); this surface reads what **fired** and how it was **triaged** (SOC 2 CC7.3 incident response — "rules actually fired and were triaged over the audit period, when, and by whom"). Sourced read-only from the Datadog security-signals search API (`GET /api/v2/security_monitoring/signals`, new `security_monitoring_signals_read` scope) by a new `connectors/datadog/internal/siemsignals` collector mirroring the slice-533 thin-HTTP + register-per-run + hour-truncated-`observed_at` pattern. **JUDGMENT — the over-collection field boundary (the load-bearing call):** a record carries triage **metadata only** — `signal_id`, `rule_id` (ties back to the rule's config record), `rule_name`, `severity`, `status` (normalized to `open` / `under_review` / `triaged` / `archived`), the timeline timestamps (`first_seen_at` / `triaged_at` / `last_updated_at`), and the **opaque** `triager_handle`. It NEVER carries the signal **message body**, the matched log/event **samples**, the matched-event **payload**, the raw detection **query**, signal-body **tags/facets**, or a recipient/triager **email** (PII — an email-shaped triager value is **dropped**). The boundary is **structural**: the `RawSignal` / `Signal` structs have no field that can hold the excluded data — a reflection test (`TestStructuralOverCollectionGuard`) pins the field set, the HTTP client decodes only the body-free fields, and a fixture embedding a message, samples, a query, and a `user.email` tag proves none reaches an emitted record. **JUDGMENT — profile shape + honest naming:** a **bounded pull** of the last-N-hours of signals (`--siem-lookback`, default 24h: `filter[from]=now-lookback`) on the operator's schedule — NOT continuous monitoring and NOT event-driven (Datadog's security-signals API is a search/poll surface with no first-class push the connector receives; signal notifications route to the operator's own integrations). `profiles_supported` stays `[pull]`; the platform-side wire stays push (invariant #3 — no proto/migration change). **JUDGMENT — anchors:** `x-default-scf-anchors` = SCF **THR-01** (detection lineage, slice-635 seed) + **IRO-09** (Incident Reporting); IRO-02 was rejected as not-in-the-bundled-catalog. New evidence kind registered in `DefaultSeed` + a draft-2020-12 JSON Schema (`additionalProperties:false`); the slice-068 drift-guard keeps schema ⇔ seed in sync. A bounded page loop (`maxPages=50` × `pageSize=100` + 60s timeout) caps the read with `ErrSignalCapExceeded`. New `siemsignals` coverage 95.0% (floor 90); the new `datadogauth.RequiredSignalScope` method is covered (the slice-533 0%-covered-method ratchet gotcha avoided). **Type: JUDGMENT** — the field boundary, profile shape, and anchor choice are recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/636-datadog-siem-signal-history-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 533. Slice doc: `docs/issues/636-datadog-siem-signal-history-event-driven-profile.md`. (#636)
- **feat(connectors): slice 622 — Kubernetes CNI-native NetworkPolicy (Cilium / Calico) coverage.** Extends the slice-523 `k8s.networkpolicy_coverage.v1` collector (`connectors/k8s/internal/netpol`) so that segmentation enforced through a CNI's own policy CRDs is no longer a false-FAIL. Many production clusters enforce segmentation entirely through `CiliumNetworkPolicy` / `CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy` (`cilium.io`) or Calico `NetworkPolicy` / `GlobalNetworkPolicy` (`crd.projectcalico.org`); under slice 523 such a cluster read as fully unprotected. The collector now detects each CNI CRD by **API discovery** (a new read-only `k8slist.Reader.Probe` — 200 present / 404 absent; **never hard-fails when absent**), reads the present CRD's policy objects (`get,list`, via the shared slice-621 paginating `k8slist.ListAll`), and folds their coverage into the **same** per-namespace default-deny assessment. A cluster-wide CNI policy (`CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy` / Calico `GlobalNetworkPolicy`) with an all-endpoints zero-rule shape folds default-deny into every namespace. **AC-2 — source distinction:** each policy summary gains an optional `source` (`networking.k8s.io` / `cilium.io` / `crd.projectcalico.org`) and the namespace record gains an optional `sources` set, so the evaluator can reason about which enforcement plane covers a namespace. **JUDGMENT — per-CNI default-deny field mapping (the load-bearing call):** Cilium counts default-deny when `endpointSelector` is empty (all endpoints) and the direction's `ingress`/`egress` key is present but empty (zero rules); Calico when `selector` is all-endpoints (`""` / `all()`), `spec.types` includes the direction, and there are zero rules — both **conservative under-credit** (a non-empty selector, an allow rule, or Cilium's `specs[]` form is NOT credited; better a false-FAIL the operator fixes than false-credited segmentation). **AC-3 — least privilege:** the documented ClusterRole gains exactly two read-only rules — `cilium.io: ciliumnetworkpolicies,ciliumclusterwidenetworkpolicies` and `crd.projectcalico.org: networkpolicies,globalnetworkpolicies`, verbs `get,list` only (no write verb, no `secrets`, no wildcard); the `DocumentedClusterRole` least-privilege test, `atlas-k8s permissions` render, and README stay in sync. **AC-4 — over-collection guard (slice-523 verbatim):** CRD SPEC metadata only — the Cilium `endpointSelector` / Calico `selector` are read only for their all-vs-narrow disposition, the `ingress`/`egress` rule arrays are counted as opaque `json.RawMessage`, and `source` carried into a record is an API-group string; a dedicated no-leak test embeds selector labels, peer endpoints, a CIDR, and ports in the CNI specs and asserts none escapes, and the integration allow-list test admits only the two new SPEC-metadata keys. **Schema (D5): additive, no version bump** — `source` (per policy) + `sources` (per namespace) are added as **optional** properties to the existing `k8s.networkpolicy_coverage/1.0.0.json` (records predating CNI support stay valid and read as upstream-only); the kind stays `1.0.0` in `DefaultSeed`. Push-only (invariant #3, no platform-side wire change); `profiles_supported` stays `[pull]`. New `netpol` coverage at 97.2% (floor 93). **Type: JUDGMENT** — the CRD-detection approach, the per-CNI default-deny mappings, the cluster-wide-folding rule, the additive-field-vs-bump call, and the CRD-absence fallback are recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/622-k8s-cni-native-networkpolicy-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 523. Slice doc: `docs/issues/622-k8s-cni-native-networkpolicy-coverage.md`. (#622)
- **feat(connectors): slice 539 — PagerDuty responder-performance metrics.** Adds a fourth evidence kind `pagerduty.response_metrics.v1` to the slice-489 PagerDuty connector — the responder-performance surface 489 deliberately deferred (P0-489-7) because per-responder timing data profiles named individuals. A new `connectors/pagerduty/internal/metrics` collector + read-only HTTP client reads `GET /incidents?since=&until=` over the SAME bounded look-back window the incident-summary + postmortem kinds use (`--lookback-days`, default 90) and emits, **per service**, incident-response performance AGGREGATES: `incident_count` / `acknowledged_count` / `resolved_count`, and MTTA (time-to-first-acknowledge) + MTTR (time-to-resolve) each as `mean` + `p50` + `p90` + `p95` in whole seconds. This proves incidents are acknowledged and resolved within target windows at the PROGRAM level for SOC 2 CC7.4 ("responds to identified security incidents") — NOT a per-engineer scorecard. **JUDGMENT — the aggregation altitude (DOMINANT Information-Disclosure threat, the load-bearing call):** metrics are SERVICE-grained, NEVER per-named-responder; the evidence ledger must not become an individual-performance surveillance store (a privacy + works-council concern in some jurisdictions). The connector NEVER decodes which individual acknowledged or resolved an incident — only the acknowledgment `at` timestamp is read, never the `acknowledger`; the assignees and the resolver are never decoded. The boundary is enforced **structurally**: the record types (`RawAck` / `RawTiming` / `ServiceMetrics`) have NO field that can hold a responder identity (name / email / id-as-person / contact) BY CONSTRUCTION — the only string field is the opaque `ServiceID` (the aggregation grain), and `json.Decode` discards JSON keys with no matching struct field (so a responder identity never enters memory even when the payload carries it). A reflection guard (`TestAggregateOnly_StructuralGuard`) FAILS THE BUILD the moment a responder-identity / free-text field is added. A drop test (`TestCollect_DropsResponderIdentity`) feeds a fake API response that deliberately embeds named acknowledgers (`Jane Doe`, `jane.doe@example.com`, `PUSER1`), assignees, and an incident title naming an individual, and asserts NO per-named-responder identity becomes the grain of any emitted metrics record — the grain stays the service id. **JUDGMENT — metric shape (D2):** first-ack drives MTTA (later re-assignment acks don't change responsiveness); nearest-rank percentiles (the result is an actually-observed sample an auditor can reconcile to one incident, not an interpolated value); whole seconds; clock-skew (negative-duration) samples dropped from the aggregate but still counted in `incident_count`. The schema carries `x-default-scf-anchors=[IRO-02, MON-02]` (Incident Handling + Continuous Monitoring — both real, both already used in this repo) and is registered in `DefaultSeed` (the schema-drift bijection passes with the new kind). **Anti-criteria (P0):** push only — no platform-side wire change (invariant #3); read-only PagerDuty token only — NO new credential scope beyond the slice-489 read-only key (the `permissions` subcommand documents it); the token is never logged or placed into a record (`TestClient_TokenNeverLogged`); the `pull` profile is named honestly (NOT continuous monitoring). **Bounded (DoS guard):** bounded look-back window + bounded page loop (`maxPages`) + a hard per-run service-aggregate cap (`metrics.MaxServices`). sha256 content-hash per record; hour-truncated `observed_at`; register-per-run; stable `actor_id=connector:pagerduty:metrics@<version>`; per-service `service` scope dimension; `--metrics-control` (default `scf:IRO-02`). Neutral fixtures only (`test-pd-token` / `test-pd-secret-token-value`; obviously-fake `INC*` / `SVC*` ids; no real-looking PagerDuty tokens). Tests: collector unit (aggregate-by-service / first-ack / percentile nearest-rank + no-mutate / drop-missing-grain / negative-duration-drop / hard-cap), client unit (auth header + query + pagination + page-cap + empty-id-skip + token-never-logged), the structural aggregate-only guard + the responder-identity drop test, the record-builder unit (kind/idempotency/payload allow-list), the `run`-seam push/collect/error tests, and the integration no-per-responder-grain assertion on the metrics path. New `connectors/pagerduty/internal/metrics` coverage floor. **Type: JUDGMENT** — the aggregation altitude, the window/percentile choices, the anchor choice (CC7.4 → IRO-02/MON-02), and detection-tier are recorded in `docs/audit-log/539-pagerduty-responder-metrics-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 489. Slice doc: `docs/issues/539-pagerduty-responder-performance-metrics.md`. (#539)
- **feat(connectors): slice 653 — Kubernetes PSS collector `metadata.continue` cursor pagination.** The `atlas-k8s` Pod Security Admission collector (`connectors/k8s/internal/pss`) still issued a single `?limit=500` namespace-list GET and consumed only the first page, silently truncating the PSS-admission posture of a cluster with more than 500 namespaces. It now routes its namespace list through the shared read-only paginating reader (`connectors/k8s/internal/k8slist`, `ListAll[apiNamespace]`) added in slice 621, following the Kubernetes `metadata.continue` cursor to completion (bounded by `k8slist.MaxListPages` + the run timeout) — the fourth and final atlas-k8s collector to adopt the shared reader, with no fourth copy of the paginating logic. The collector's duplicated `getJSON` / `APIError` / `drain` are deleted; `pss.APIError` is re-exported as a type alias of `k8slist.APIError` so existing callers are unaffected. **No new ClusterRole grant — still `get,list` only on namespaces (`k8sauth.DocumentedClusterRole` unchanged); push-only wire, the `k8s.pod_security_admission.v1` evidence-kind shape, and the PSS label-only over-collection guard are all unchanged.** Parent: slice 621 (and #524). Slice doc: `docs/issues/653-k8s-pss-collector-cursor-pagination.md`. (#653)
- **feat(connectors): slice 621 — Kubernetes connector `metadata.continue` cursor pagination across all reads.** The `atlas-k8s` rbac, workload, and netpol collectors previously issued a single `?limit=500` list GET and consumed only the first page, silently truncating a cluster with more than one page of any listed resource (networkpolicies, namespaces, deployments, rolebindings, clusterroles, ...). They now route every list through a new shared read-only paginating reader (`connectors/k8s/internal/k8slist`, generic `ListAll[T]`) that follows the Kubernetes `metadata.continue` cursor to completion — accumulating items across pages and adding `&continue=<token>` to the same list URL each step. The walk is bounded by a deterministic page cap (`k8slist.MaxListPages`, 1000) so a non-terminating / hostile cursor cannot loop forever, plus the run timeout (the caller's context deadline); a later-page read error discards the partial set rather than reporting a truncated list as complete. **No new ClusterRole grant — pagination is a `limit`+`continue` query-param change, still `get,list` only (`k8sauth.DocumentedClusterRole` unchanged, AC-4); push-only wire and the per-collector over-collection guards are unchanged.** The PSS collector (slice 524) has the same single-page pattern but was left out of scope and filed as spillover #653. Parent: slice 523 (and #487). Slice doc: `docs/issues/621-k8s-connector-cursor-pagination.md`. (#621)
- **feat(connectors): slice 623 — Azure Key-Vault roleAssignments ARM `nextLink` cursor pagination.** The per-vault `Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments` read for RBAC-mode Key Vaults (slice 615) now follows the ARM `nextLink` continuation cursor across pages to completion, so a vault with more than the per-page bound of role assignments is no longer silently truncated. The walk mirrors the slice-634 firewall `nextLink` pattern: the server-issued absolute `nextLink` (carrying an opaque skiptoken) is followed verbatim, and the loop terminates on an empty `nextLink`, on the per-vault record cap (`maxRoleAssignmentsPerVault`, 200), on the run-wide DoS backstop (`maxRoleAssignmentsPerRun`, 2000 — unchanged, P0-623-2), or on a per-vault page cap (`maxRoleAssignmentPages`, 100) that breaks a hostile self-pointing cursor. A later-page read error discards the partially-gathered set and marks the vault INCONCLUSIVE (partial-read honesty). **No new Azure scope (ARM Reader only, P0-623-4), no data-plane access, no schema change to `azure.keyvault_access_config.v1`.** The `AccessEntry` over-collection guard is unchanged — principal id + role NAME metadata only; the ARM decode discards everything else, pinned across the multi-page path by a secret-bearing-field test (P0-623-3). Parent: slice 615 (and #521 / #486 R3). Slice doc: `docs/issues/623-azure-keyvault-roleassignment-cursor-pagination.md`. (#623)
- **feat(connectors): slice 595 — MDM config-profile per-setting enrichment (Jamf + Intune).** Populates the per-profile `settings[]` list (empty at the slice-556 v0) on the shared `endpoint.config_profile.v1` evidence kind. **No new evidence kind, no schema change** — the `settings[]` field was already wired end-to-end through the `cfgprofile.AllowedSettingKeys` allow-list + `IsBannedSettingKey` deny-list; this slice supplies real per-setting values. **Source (JUDGMENT, D1 — the load-bearing scope call):** the values are derived from **metadata-grade, already-scoped enforced-state inventory fields**, NEVER the raw configuration-profile payload. Jamf extends the config-profile read to also request the posture sections `OPERATING_SYSTEM` / `DISK_ENCRYPTION` / `SECURITY` (the SAME read-only "Read Computers" role the posture `run` already holds — **NO new scope**) and projects a synthetic **Enforced Configuration Summary** profile carrying `disk_encryption_enforced` / `gatekeeper_enabled` / `screen_lock_enforced` / `device_supervised` / `device_managed`; Intune adds `isEncrypted` + `complianceState` to the device `$select` (covered by the SAME `DeviceManagementManagedDevices.Read.All` permission — **NO new scope**) yielding `disk_encryption_enforced` + `device_compliant`, plus each profile's allow-listed `profile_assignment_state` (compliant / nonCompliant / conflict / error) from the `deviceConfigurationStates` expansion. **Allow-list extension (JUDGMENT, D2):** four new compliance-relevant hardening keys added to `cfgprofile.AllowedSettingKeys` — `device_compliant`, `device_supervised`, `device_managed`, `profile_assignment_state` — each a non-secret boolean/enum summary; never a secret-bearing key. **Value sanitization (D3):** every value is a normalized non-secret summary (`"true"`/`"false"` or a state enum) computed from an enforced-state field — the connector never copies a raw payload value into a setting value. **Secret-redaction guard (LOAD-BEARING, P0-556, unchanged + extended):** structural at three layers — (1) the vendor-client structs carry no field for a payload blob / PSK / shared secret / private key / SCEP challenge (a leak is a compile error); (2) no requested API surface carries the raw payload (Jamf `CONFIGURATION_PROFILES` is metadata-only, the posture sections are enforced-state summaries; Intune neither the device `$select` nor the `deviceConfigurationStates` expansion carries the raw setting payload); (3) the allow-list + deny-list drop any non-allow-listed or credential-substring key at BOTH the normalizer (`cfgprofile.normalizeSettings`) AND the record builder (`cfgrecord.Build`). New per-vendor client tests feed a payload carrying an allowed hardening key alongside fake Wi-Fi-password / VPN-shared-secret / private-key / payload-blob siblings and assert no secret value reaches any setting; `cfgprofile` gains a value-sanitization secret-drop test (allowed key next to `wifi_password` → only the allowed key survives) + an enrichment-key allow-list test; the existing `cfgrecord` recursive secret-walk + `cfgprofile.TestNormalize_DropsSecretBearingSettings` + `TestAllowListHasNoBannedKey` self-consistency guards stay green. Read-only credential unchanged (slice-490 remote-wipe guard intact); push-only wire (invariant #3); sha256 content-hash per record; bounded one-page read per run; neutral fixtures only. schemaregistry kind↔schema drift/bijection unchanged. **Type: JUDGMENT** — the source-field choice (no new scope), the allow-list keys + compliance rationale, the value-shape, and detection-tier are recorded in `docs/audit-log/595-mdm-config-profile-settings-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 556. Slice doc: `docs/issues/595-mdm-config-profile-per-setting-enrichment.md`. (#595)

- **feat(connectors): slice 524 — Kubernetes Pod-Security-Standards admission-config evidence.** Adds a fourth evidence kind to the Kubernetes connector (slice 487): `k8s.pod_security_admission.v1` — the **enforced** side of workload hardening, complementing the **actual**-posture `k8s.workload_security_context.v1` kind. A new `connectors/k8s/internal/pss` collector reads, read-only via the Kubernetes API (`get,list` on **core namespaces** — a rule the base connector already holds, so **NO new ClusterRole rule** is required), the per-namespace Pod-Security-Standards admission configuration: which namespaces carry the `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce`, `/audit`, and `/warn` labels and at which level (`privileged` / `baseline` / `restricted`), plus the optional pinned `*-version` label per mode. The recurring "is hardening **enforced at admission**, not just configured per-workload" auditor ask previously needed a manual upload. **Verdict (JUDGMENT, D2):** the connector verdicts the per-namespace admission posture from the `enforce` label — `PASS` when the namespace ENFORCES a hardened level (`baseline` or `restricted`); `FAIL` when no `enforce` label is set (unenforced — **recorded honestly**, not dropped) or when `enforce` is only `privileged`; `audit`/`warn` modes are reported but do not drive the verdict (they observe/warn, they do not block admission); the platform evaluator owns the final (control, namespace) call. **Label-filter / over-collection guard (structural, the locked pattern, LOAD-BEARING):** the namespace object carries arbitrary labels AND annotations — the PSS HTTP client reads **only** the six `pod-security.kubernetes.io/*` label keys off `metadata.labels` and never decodes `metadata.annotations` at all (`metadata.annotations`, `spec`, and `status` have no matching struct field, so Go's JSON decoder discards them); the `RawNamespace` / `Admission` structs carry **no field** for a pod spec, secret, container env, or any other namespace label/annotation, and a reflection guard (`TestStruct_PSSLabelsOnly_NoOverCollectionFields`) FAILS THE BUILD the moment such a field is added. The client test (`TestClient_OnlyPSSLabelsReachRecord`) serves a namespace carrying unrelated labels (a team label, a secret-looking label value) AND annotations (a kubectl last-applied blob with embedded secret material, an owner-email PII annotation) alongside the PSS labels, and asserts only the PSS labels reach a record; the integration over-collection test allow-lists the PSS-only payload keys. **Anti-criteria (P0):** push only — no platform-side wire change (invariant #3); read-only namespace `get,list` only — **NO new ClusterRole rule**, no write verb, no `secrets`, no wildcard (the `DocumentedClusterRole` least-privilege test + `atlas-k8s permissions` render + README stay in sync, the namespaces rule's `Gates` string now documents the PSS kind); the token is never logged or placed into a record; the `pull` profile is named honestly (NOT continuous monitoring). **Bounded (DoS guard):** a bounded namespace page read (`limit=500`) + a per-run namespace cap (`maxNamespaces=5000`). Schema `k8s.pod_security_admission/1.0.0.json` carries `x-default-scf-anchors=["CFG-02"]` (Secure Baseline Configurations) and is wired into `schemaregistry.DefaultSeed` (drift bijection passes with the new kind). sha256 content-hash per record; hour-truncated `observed_at`; register-per-run; stable `actor_id=connector:k8s:pss@<version>`; new `idem.PSSAdmissionKey` (distinct from the netpol key via the kind prefix); per-namespace `namespace` scope dimension (one record per namespace); `--pss-control` (default `scf:CFG-02`) + `--skip-pss`. Scope discipline (D3): namespace PSS **labels only** — does NOT read the cluster's `AdmissionConfiguration` file (out of API reach), validating/mutating webhooks, or third-party policy engines (OPA/Gatekeeper, Kyverno) — those are documented follow-ons (spillover band 652–659). Neutral fixtures only (obviously-fake namespace names; no real-looking kubeconfig/tokens). New `connectors/k8s/internal/pss` coverage floor. **Type: JUDGMENT** — the evidence-kind shape, the label-filter/over-collection-guard mechanism, the scope-minimum (no new ClusterRole rule), the anchor choice (CFG-02), and detection-tier are recorded in `docs/audit-log/524-k8s-pss-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 487. Slice doc: `docs/issues/524-k8s-pod-security-standards-admission-evidence.md`. (#524)
- **feat(connectors): slice 534 — Grafana SAML/RBAC config evidence.** Adds a second evidence kind `grafana.access_config.v1` to the slice-488 Grafana connector — the authn/authz configuration surface 488 deliberately deferred (P0-488-7) because SSO settings _embed_ secrets (SAML private key, OAuth client secret, LDAP bind password). A new `connectors/grafana/internal/ssoconfig` collector + read-only HTTP client reads `GET /api/v1/sso-settings`, `GET /api/teams/search`, and `GET /api/access-control/assignments` and emits, per Grafana instance, the auditor CONFIG + COUNTS ONLY: whether SSO is enabled (`sso_enabled`), the per-provider posture (`provider_type` + `enabled` + org-role mapping RULE names), aggregate team counts (`team_count` + `total_team_memberships`), and the RBAC role-assignment COUNTS rolled up by scope (`user_role_assignments` / `team_role_assignments` / `builtin_role_assignments`). This proves SSO is enforced and access is role-based for SOC 2 CC6.1/CC6.2/CC6.3. **JUDGMENT — sibling kind, NOT the monitoring kind:** this is an IAM surface, not a monitoring surface, so it gets its own kind rather than widening `monitoring.alert_config.v1`. **JUDGMENT — the over-collection / secret + PII boundary (DOMINANT, load-bearing):** the connector NEVER collects a SAML private key, an OAuth client secret, an LDAP bind password, a signing certificate, or any individual user / team-member / role-assignment identity (name / email / login). The boundary is enforced **structurally**: the record types (`RawProvider` / `RawTeamStats` / `RawRoleStats` / `Provider` / `AccessConfig`) have NO field that can hold a secret or an identity BY CONSTRUCTION, the decode structs in `client.go` omit every secret/identity JSON key so `json.Decode` discards them (the SAML `settings` blob and team-member rows are never materialized), and a reflection guard (`TestStructuralOverCollectionGuard`) FAILS THE BUILD the moment a secret/identity-named field is added. A drop test (`TestClient_NeverLeaksSecretOrPII`) feeds a fake API response that deliberately embeds a SAML private key, OAuth client secret, LDAP bind password, signing cert, and a user email/login/name, and asserts none reaches a record. The schema carries `x-default-scf-anchors=[IAC-06, IAC-22]` (Authenticator Management + Least Privilege) and is registered in `DefaultSeed` (the schema-drift bijection passes with the new kind). **JUDGMENT — auth-scope minimum + Admin warning (this slice owns it):** reading SSO settings needs MORE than 488's Viewer role; the precise additional read-only fixed-role permissions (`fixed:settings:reader` = `settings:read` on `settings:auth.*`; `fixed:roles:reader` = `roles:read` + `users.roles:read` + `teams.roles:read`) are documented in `grafanaauth.RequiredAccessConfigScopes()` and surfaced by the `permissions` subcommand, which WARNS against granting Admin "to be safe". **Anti-criteria (P0):** push only — no platform-side wire change (invariant #3); read-only API only — NO write/admin scope; the token is never logged or placed into a record; `profiles_supported=[pull]` named honestly (NOT continuous monitoring). **Bounded (DoS guard):** per-endpoint decode cap (`maxItems`) + bounded body read + run timeout. sha256 content-hash per record; hour-truncated `observed_at`; register-per-run; stable `actor_id=connector:grafana:ssoconfig@<version>`. Neutral fixtures only (obviously-fake `FAKE-*` keys/secrets; no real-looking Grafana tokens or SAML keys). New shared-package lines (`grafanaauth.RequiredAccessConfigScopes` + permission constants) ship fully tested — `grafanaauth` holds its 98% ratchet (measures 100%). Tests: collector unit (normalize/dedup/clamp/drop-empty/error-propagation), client unit (the secret+PII drop test + collect-shape + role-mappings-captured + bounded-cap + HTTP-error), the structural over-collection guard, the record-builder unit (kind/idempotency/payload allow-list/omit-empty-providers-and-mappings), the `run`-seam push/collect/error tests, and the schemaregistry drift/bijection test. New `connectors/grafana/internal/ssoconfig` coverage floor. **Type: JUDGMENT** — the sibling-kind decision, the over-collection/secret+PII-drop mechanism, the auth-scope-minimum + Admin warning, the anchor choice (IAC-06/IAC-22 → CC6.x), and detection-tier are recorded in `docs/audit-log/534-grafana-sso-rbac-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 488. Slice doc: `docs/issues/534-grafana-saml-rbac-config-evidence.md`. (#534)

- **feat(catalog): slice 646 — map the finer SCF THR controls into the framework crosswalks.** The slice-641 follow-on: slice 641 imported `THR-02..THR-10` as `scf_anchors` rows but only `THR-01` (from slice 635) carried framework crosswalk edges, so the finer controls resolved in the catalog yet no framework requirement pointed at them. This slice authors the requirement → SCF-anchor STRM edges (invariant #7) mapping SOC 2 / ISO 27001 / NIST CSF requirements onto the finer THR controls. **Edges added (JUDGMENT — relationship + strength per edge):** SOC 2 `CC7.2 → THR-04` (Threat Hunting) `intersects_with/0.6`; ISO 27001 `A.5.7 → THR-03` (Threat Intelligence Feeds) `subset_of/0.7`, `A.8.16 → THR-04` (Threat Hunting) `intersects_with/0.6`, `A.8.8 → THR-02` (Indicators of Exposure) `subset_of/0.6`; NIST CSF `ID.RA-02 → THR-03` (Threat Intelligence Feeds) `equal/0.9` — the dedicated upgrade of the slice-480 LOW-confidence MON-08 placeholder, `DE.AE-07 → THR-01` (Threat Intelligence Program) `intersects_with/0.7`, `ID.RA-03 → THR-09` (Threat Catalog) `subset_of/0.7` + `ID.RA-03 → THR-10` (Threat Analysis) `subset_of/0.6`. All are ADDITIVE second anchors (invariant #1 — N anchors per requirement); existing edges are untouched. **Rejected candidates (honest non-mappings):** `THR-05`/`THR-06` (Insider Threat Program / Awareness) and `THR-07` (Vulnerability Disclosure Program) carry NO edges — none of the five bundled crosswalks has a dedicated insider-threat or coordinated-disclosure requirement, and mapping general security-awareness or internal vuln-management requirements onto them would over-state the relationship; `CC9.2 → THR-07` was rejected (vendor-risk ≠ inbound vuln disclosure). PCI DSS + HIPAA gain no THR edges (their threat/vuln/awareness controls already anchor correctly to VPM/RSK/IRO and have no honest dedicated THR match) — a finer pass when a framework with insider-threat/VDP concepts lands is filed as spillover. **Pure crosswalk-data slice — no migration, no schema/evidence-kind change.** Invariant #7/#1 honored — all new edges are requirement → SCF anchor (the b227 guard `TestImport_NoDirectRequirementToRequirementTableExists` stays green). Tests: a new integration test (`internal/api/soc2import/thr_finer_crosswalk_integration_test.go`) asserts every authored edge resolves through a real `fw_to_scf_edges` row and spot-checks the load-bearing STRM relationships (`ID.RA-02 → THR-03` is `equal`, `CC7.2 → THR-04` is `intersects_with`); the CSF loader's 1:1 Subcategory-edge assertion is updated to allow the multi-anchor requirements (len()-style, exact total asserted); the slice-635/641 THR tests, the scfseed + scfimport + full soc2import integration suites, and the schemaregistry drift/bijection test all stay green; the integration-enrolment audit stays OK. Neutral crosswalk data only. **Type: JUDGMENT** — each edge's relationship/strength rationale, the rejected candidates, the per-framework coverage vs spillover, and detection-tier are recorded in `docs/audit-log/646-thr-crosswalk-edges-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 641. Slice doc: `docs/issues/646-thr-domain-crosswalk-finer-edges.md`. (#646)
- **feat(connectors): slice 538 — PagerDuty postmortem/retrospective evidence.** Adds a third evidence kind `pagerduty.postmortem_summary.v1` to the slice-489 PagerDuty connector — the postmortem / retrospective surface 489 deliberately deferred (P0-489-7) because a postmortem is dense free-text routinely embedding customer data, responder PII, and root-cause prose. A new `connectors/pagerduty/internal/postmortems` collector + read-only HTTP client reads `GET /postmortems?since=&until=` over the SAME bounded look-back window the incident-summary kind uses (`--lookback-days`, default 90) and emits, per postmortem, the auditor META-FACTS ONLY: the opaque `postmortem_id`, the linked `incident_id`, the review `status` (normalized enum `not_started`/`in_progress`/`in_review`/`published`), the `created_at` / optional `published_at` timestamps, and the corrective-action ROLLUP (`action_item_count` + `action_items_completed` + `action_items_open`). This proves incidents are reviewed and corrective actions tracked for SOC 2 CC7.5 ("recover from identified security incidents") and the slice-372 IR continuous-improvement loop — NOT the story. **JUDGMENT — the over-collection / redaction boundary (DOMINANT, load-bearing):** the connector NEVER collects the postmortem narrative body, timeline free-text, root-cause prose, an action item's operator-authored title/description, customer data, or responder PII. Action-item titles are operator-authored free-text (they routinely name the affected customer/system/root-cause) and are therefore EXCLUDED — only the item's completion state feeds the count. The boundary is enforced **structurally**: the record types (`RawPostmortem` / `Postmortem` / `RawActionItem`) have NO field that can hold the narrative or a title BY CONSTRUCTION, `json.Decode` discards JSON keys with no matching struct field (so the narrative never enters memory even when the payload carries it), and a reflection guard (`TestMetadataOnly_StructuralGuard`) FAILS THE BUILD the moment a free-text/PII-named field is added (proven: adding a `Body` field trips it). A drop test (`TestClient_ListPostmortems_DropsNarrative`) feeds a fake API response that deliberately embeds a narrative body, root-cause prose, responder PII (`123-45-6789`, `jane@acme.example`), and action-item titles, and asserts none reaches a record. The schema carries `x-default-scf-anchors=[IRO-13, IRO-09]` (Root-Cause Analysis + Incident Reporting) and is registered in `DefaultSeed` (the schema-drift bijection passes with the new kind). **Anti-criteria (P0):** push only — no platform-side wire change (invariant #3); read-only PagerDuty token only — NO new credential scope beyond the slice-489 read-only key (the `permissions` subcommand documents the `/postmortems` GET); the token is never logged or placed into a record; the `pull` profile is named honestly (NOT continuous monitoring). **Bounded (DoS guard):** bounded look-back window + bounded page loop (`maxPages`) + a hard per-run record cap (`postmortems.MaxRecords`); a paginated/pathological source stops at the cap rather than reading forever (`TestClient_ListPostmortems_StopsAtRunCap`). sha256 content-hash per record; hour-truncated `observed_at`; register-per-run; stable `actor_id=connector:pagerduty:postmortems@<version>`. Neutral fixtures only (obviously-fake `PM-FAKE-*` / `INC-FAKE-*` ids; no real-looking PagerDuty tokens). Tests: collector unit (normalize/rollup/drop-blank/hard-cap), client unit (narrative-drop + pagination + run-cap + auth header), the structural metadata-only guard, the record-builder unit (kind/idempotency/payload allow-list/omit-published-when-unpublished), the `run`-seam push/collect/error tests, the integration no-PII/no-free-text assertion on the postmortem path, and the schemaregistry drift/bijection test. New `connectors/pagerduty/internal/postmortems` coverage floor. **Type: JUDGMENT** — the evidence-kind shape, the redaction/over-collection-boundary mechanism (HOW the narrative can't leak), the anchor choice (CC7.5 → IRO-13/IRO-09), and detection-tier are recorded in `docs/audit-log/538-pagerduty-postmortem-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 489. Slice doc: `docs/issues/538-pagerduty-postmortem-retrospective-evidence.md`. (#538)
- **feat(catalog): slice 641 — import the full SCF Threat-Management (THR) domain.** The slice-635 follow-on: slice 635 seeded a single domain-head anchor `THR-01` ("Threat Intelligence Program") so the `datadog.siem_rule.v1` advisory detection anchor would resolve; slice 641 imports the rest of the canonical SCF Threat-Management domain into the bundled sample catalog (`migrations/fixtures/scf-sample.json`), mirroring how other families carry their representative control set. Adds `THR-02` (Indicators of Exposure), `THR-03` (Threat Intelligence Feeds), `THR-04` (Threat Hunting), `THR-05` (Insider Threat Program), `THR-06` (Insider Threat Awareness), `THR-07` (Vulnerability Disclosure Program), `THR-09` (Threat Catalog), and `THR-10` (Threat Analysis) — eight new `scf_anchors` rows (family `THR`), all verbatim SCF identifiers. **THR-01 reconciliation:** the slice-635 one-line placeholder description is reconciled to align with the canonical SCF THR-01 control text (the program now influences "system and security architectures, the selection of security solutions, monitoring, threat hunting, and response and recovery activities"), staying in the catalog's established one-line house style. **Crosswalk re-evaluation (JUDGMENT):** with the full domain present, the slice-635 STRM edges (SOC 2 CC7.2 intersects_with/0.7 + CC7.3 intersects_with/0.6; ISO 27001 A.5.7 equal/1.0 + A.8.16 intersects_with/0.6) are re-evaluated and KEPT AS-IS — the SIEM-rule evidence family anchors at the program-level `THR-01` by deliberate slice-533/635 design; the program → requirement edges remain `intersects_with` (a threat-intel program partially informs CC7.2's operational anomaly-monitoring, it is not identical to it — `equal` would over-state it), and A.5.7 → THR-01 `equal/1.0` is confirmed by the full domain context, not contradicted. **NO change to the `datadog.siem_rule.v1` evidence-kind shape or the schemaregistry drift/bijection guard (AC):** the schema's anchor list stays `[MON-01, THR-01]`; only catalog-fixture data changes — pure fixture slice, no migration. Invariant #7/#1 honored — THR anchors are canonical SCF controls; crosswalk edges are requirement → SCF anchor (the b227 requirement → requirement guard `TestImport_NoDirectRequirementToRequirementTableExists` stays green). **Verbatim-text caveat (flagged for maintainer):** the per-control THR descriptions are a careful canonical reconstruction aligned with SCF intent — the SCF's authoritative per-control prose ships in the downloadable SCF workbook, not retrievable in-repo or from public web pages; the identifiers/titles are verbatim-canonical, the one-line descriptions follow the catalog's existing house-style paraphrase (matching every other sample anchor, none of which reproduce verbatim SCF prose). Tests: a new integration test (`internal/api/soc2import/thr_domain_integration_test.go`) asserts every imported THR anchor resolves through the production `GetSCFAnchorBySCFID` path with family `THR` and that the domain is multi-control (not collapsed to the head); the slice-635 THR-01 resolution + crosswalk tests, the scfseed + scfimport + soc2import integration suites, and the schemaregistry drift/bijection test all stay green; the integration-enrolment audit stays OK. Neutral catalog data only. **Type: JUDGMENT** — the anchor granularity/coverage, the THR-01 reconciliation, the crosswalk-strength re-evaluation, the verbatim-text uncertainty, and detection-tier are recorded in `docs/audit-log/641-scf-thr-domain-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 635. Slice doc: `docs/issues/641-full-scf-thr-domain-import.md`. (#641)
- **feat(connectors): slice 571 — HRIS manager-hierarchy evidence.** Adds a second evidence kind `hris.manager_hierarchy.v1` to the existing Rippling + BambooHR HRIS connectors — the manager reporting tree DERIVED from the slice-491 worker roster's `manager_assignment_id` (each worker already carries their direct manager's opaque worker id). New shared `connectors/hris/hierarchy` builder turns the flat set of (worker → manager) edges into one hierarchy-edge record per worker carrying: the opaque `worker_assignment_id` + optional `manager_assignment_id`, the worker's `depth` in the tree (root = 0), an `orphaned_report` flag (the manager is terminated OR absent from the roster — a broken access-review approver chain), and a `cycle_member` flag. A new shared `connectors/hris/hierarchyrecord` builds the pushable record. Both connectors' `run` subcommand derives the tree from the SAME bounded roster read (no new source read, no new scope — the `permissions` subcommand documents that the hierarchy is derived from the same read) and pushes one edge record per worker after the lifecycle records, sharing the roster's hour-truncated `observed_at`. The schema carries `x-default-scf-anchors=[IAC-22, IAC-09]` (access-review / least-privilege routing; the lifecycle kind's `HRS-04` personnel anchor is deliberately dropped) and is registered in `DefaultSeed` (the schema-drift bijection passes with the new kind). Push only (invariant #3); `profiles_supported=[pull]`; stable `actor_id=connector:<vendor>:hierarchy@<version>`; register-per-run; honest 24h operator-scheduled interval. **JUDGMENT — sibling kind, NOT enrichment:** the hierarchy fields are DERIVED from the whole roster (a worker's depth depends on every ancestor; the orphan flag depends on the manager's terminated status), a different altitude from the per-worker source FACTS the lifecycle record carries — so it gets its own kind with a distinct idempotency-key prefix rather than bloating the lifecycle record (mirroring the slice-488→533 / 490→555 sibling splits). **PII / over-collection guard (the slice-491 identity boundary, LOAD-BEARING):** OPAQUE assignment ids ONLY — NEVER a manager's (or any worker's) name, email, phone, address, or any personal-contact / sensitive-PII detail. The `hierarchy.Edge` struct has NO field capable of holding any of those (a leak is a compile error); a reflection-based structural guard `TestEdge_HasNoPIIField` makes the absence executable, mirroring + extending slice-491's `TestRawWorker_HasNoSensitivePIIField`, and the record-level `TestBuild_PayloadCarriesHierarchyFactsOnly` pins the emitted-payload allow-list. **Bounded + cycle-terminating:** the tree derives from the already-bounded per-run roster; a manager cycle (`A→B→A`, a self-manager `A→A`, or a longer ring) is detected via an iterative three-colour walk and flagged `cycle_member` with `depth=-1` rather than looping forever — asserted with watchdog-timed termination tests (`TestBuild_TwoCycleTerminates` / `SelfManagerCycleTerminates` / `LongCycleTerminates`). Neutral fixtures only (obviously-fake opaque ids; no real-looking HRIS API tokens). Tests: hierarchy builder unit (depth/orphan/cycle/order/empty) + hierarchyrecord builder unit (kind/idempotency/scope/payload-allow-list/flag-carry) + the structural PII guard + the cycle-termination + orphaned-report (terminated manager) cases + the schemaregistry drift/bijection test + both connectors' `run`-seam push tests (incl. a hierarchy-specific push-error wrap). New `connectors/hris/hierarchy` + `connectors/hris/hierarchyrecord` coverage floors; the shared `connectors/hris/idem` floor holds with the new `ManagerHierarchyKey` covered. **Type: JUDGMENT** — the sibling-kind-vs-enrichment call, the PII over-collection guard, the cycle/orphan handling, the anchor choice, and detection-tier are recorded in `docs/audit-log/571-hris-manager-hierarchy-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 491. Slice doc: `docs/issues/571-hris-manager-hierarchy-evidence.md`. (#571)
- **feat(catalog): slice 635 — seed the THR detection anchor for the SIEM-rule kind.** Closes the slice-533 D2 catalog gap: `datadog.siem_rule.v1` ships `x-default-scf-anchors=[MON-01, THR-01]`, but `THR-01` (the SCF Threat-Management / threat-intelligence domain) did NOT exist in the bundled SCF sample catalog (`migrations/fixtures/scf-sample.json`) — it carried the MON domain (incl. `MON-08` "Anomalous Behavior Detection") but no THR-domain anchor, so the connector's advisory detection anchor pointed at no catalog row. **JUDGMENT — path (a), seed (not remap):** the connector binds `scf:THR-01` in six places (schema, `--siem-control` default, README, three tests); THR-01 ("Threat Intelligence Program") is the correct canonical SCF anchor for the threat-detection half of the SIEM-rule evidence question, so seeding it keeps the stated mapping intact and adds a real THR-domain anchor the catalog lacked — strictly preferable to remapping the schema's advisory list to a weaker already-seeded anchor (`MON-08`). Seeds the `THR-01` anchor (family `THR`) into the sample catalog and adds its STRM crosswalk edges: SOC 2 `CC7.2` (intersects_with/0.7) + `CC7.3` (intersects_with/0.6); ISO 27001 `A.5.7` "Threat intelligence" (now **equal/1.0** — upgraded from the slice-438 LOW-CONFIDENCE `MON-08` intersects_with/0.5 placeholder, since THR-01 is the dedicated threat-intel anchor) + `A.8.16` "Monitoring activities" (intersects_with/0.6). **NO change to the `datadog.siem_rule.v1` evidence-kind shape or the drift/bijection guard (AC-3):** the schema's anchor list stays `[MON-01, THR-01]`; only the catalog fixture + crosswalk data change. Invariant #7 honored — the crosswalk is requirement → SCF anchor with STRM-typed edges. Tests: a new integration test (`internal/api/soc2import/thr_anchor_integration_test.go`) asserts THR-01 resolves through the production `GetSCFAnchorBySCFID` path AND that SOC 2 CC7.2/CC7.3 + ISO A.5.7/A.8.16 each resolve to THR-01 through real `fw_to_scf_edges`; the scfseed + scfimport + soc2import integration suites and the schemaregistry drift/bijection test stay green. Neutral data only. **Type: JUDGMENT** — path-choice, canonical-anchor reasoning (THR-01 vs MON-08), and the STRM crosswalk are recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/635-thr-anchor-seed-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 533. Slice doc: `docs/issues/635-thr-anchor-catalog-seed-for-siem-rule-kind.md`. (#635)
- **feat(connectors): slice 533 — Datadog Cloud-SIEM detection-rule evidence.** Adds a second evidence kind `datadog.siem_rule.v1` to the existing `atlas-datadog` connector binary — the Cloud-SIEM / Security-Monitoring detection-rule surface slice 488 deferred (P0-488-7). A new `connectors/datadog/internal/siemrules` collector + Datadog client reads, read-only, the cursor-paginated `GET /api/v2/security_monitoring/rules` (`security_monitoring_rules_read` scope) and captures per rule: name, detection class (log / signal_correlation / threshold), enabled state, highest case severity, and the notification-target HANDLES. **JUDGMENT — sibling kind, NOT reuse (slice-488 D1):** a detection rule carries a severity + a detection-class field the operational `monitoring.alert_config.v1` shape lacks, so it gets its own kind rather than widening the shared monitoring kind into a lowest-common-denominator blob — exactly the split slice-488 D1 reserved. The schema carries `x-default-scf-anchors=[MON-01, THR-01]` and is registered in `DefaultSeed` (the schema-drift bijection passes with the new kind). Push only (invariant #3); `profiles_supported=[pull]`; stable `actor_id=connector:datadog:siemrules@<version>`; register-per-run; hour-truncated `observed_at`; honest 24h operator-scheduled interval. The `run` subcommand threads the SIEM kind alongside the monitor kind (`--siem-control` flag + a seam). **Anti-criteria (P0):** read-only `security_monitoring_rules_read` suffices — no write/admin scope, no new platform-side wire surface (P0); RULE configuration only — NEVER firing signals, the detection query's raw log samples, matched-event payloads, secret webhook URLs, integration tokens, recipient PII, or the raw detection query: the collector record structs (`RawRule` / `Rule`) have NO field capable of holding any of those (a reflection-based structural over-collection test, `TestStructuralOverCollectionGuard`, pins the field set, mirroring the slice-488 / 614 pattern). Bounded cursor loop + hard per-run page cap (`maxPages=50` × `pageSize=100` ⇒ 5,000 rules) + 60s run timeout (DoS guard): an unbounded source stops with `ErrRuleCapExceeded` rather than reading forever. Neutral fixtures only (obviously-fake rule ids/handles; no real-looking Datadog API/app keys). Tests: collector + Datadog-client unit (faked surface, incl. a multi-page cursor case + a cap-termination case), the structural over-collection guard, the schemaregistry drift/bijection test, `run`-seam push/error tests, and the in-package bufconn integration round-trip (descending into notification targets for the no-PII/no-secret allow-list). New `connectors/datadog/internal/siemrules` coverage floor. **Type: JUDGMENT** — the sibling-kind-vs-reuse call, the scope-minimum + over-collection-guard mechanism, the stable-field choices, and the anchor choice are recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/533-datadog-siem-rules-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 488. Slice doc: `docs/issues/533-datadog-cloud-siem-rule-evidence.md`. (#533)
- **feat(connectors): slice 614 — Azure Firewall rule-collection evidence.** Adds a sixth evidence kind `azure.firewall_rules.v1` to the existing `atlas-azure` connector binary — the deferred Azure-Firewall sibling that slice 520 (NSG) named as out-of-scope. A new `connectors/azure/internal/firewall` collector + ARM client reads, read-only, the `Microsoft.Network/firewallPolicies` list and each policy's `ruleCollectionGroups` (network + application rule collections + the rule-collection-group priority ordering), the centralized policy-based L3-L7 boundary-protection control point that complements the per-subnet/per-NIC NSG kind. The schema carries `x-default-scf-anchors=[NET-04, NET-01]` — the SAME boundary-protection anchors as the NSG kind — and is registered in `DefaultSeed` (the schema-drift bijection passes with the new kind). Push only (invariant #3); `profiles_supported=[pull]`; stable `actor_id=connector:azure:firewall@<version>`; register-per-run; hour-truncated `observed_at`; honest 24h operator-scheduled interval. The `run` subcommand threads the firewall kind exactly as 520 did for NSG (`--firewall-control` flag + `--skip-firewall` + a seam). **Anti-criteria (P0):** ARM **Reader** suffices — no Network Contributor, no rule mutation, no new Azure scope (P0-614-1/3); RULE configuration only — NEVER flow logs, packet captures, traffic contents, NAT-rule secrets, threat-intel feeds, or route tables (P0-614-2): the collector record structs have NO field capable of holding any of those (a reflection-based structural over-collection test pins it, mirroring slice 520/615). Bounded ARM page + per-run cap on rule-collection groups (DoS guard); cursor pagination for a huge estate is the deliberate follow-on (spillover slice 634). Neutral fixtures only (obviously-fake `00000000-...` ids). Tests: collector + ARM-client unit (faked surface), the structural over-collection guard, the schemaregistry drift/bijection test, `run`-seam push/skip/error tests, and the in-package bufconn integration round-trip (descending into the rule-collection-group tree for the no-PII/no-secret allow-list). New `connectors/azure/internal/firewall` coverage floor. **Type: JUDGMENT** — the evidence-kind shape, the scope-minimum + over-collection-guard mechanism, and the stable-field choices are recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/614-azure-firewall-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 520. Slice doc: `docs/issues/614-azure-firewall-rule-collection-evidence.md`. (#614)
- **feat(connectors): slice 634 — Azure Firewall rule-collection-group cursor pagination.** Adds ARM `nextLink` cursor pagination to the slice-614 Azure-Firewall reads — the deliberate unbounded-read follow-on slice 614 deferred (its decisions-log D5). The firewall ARM client (`connectors/azure/internal/firewall`) now follows the ARM `nextLink` continuation on BOTH the `firewallPolicies` list AND each policy's `ruleCollectionGroups` list, so a large estate's full rule set is collected instead of only the first bounded page. The `nextLink` is followed verbatim as the absolute, opaque-skiptoken-carrying URL the server returns; every page read — first page and every continuation — is a **GET** (the slice-614 GET-only client-test assertion stays green). **Over-collection guard UNCHANGED (P0-634-2 / the slice-614 structural pin):** this changes only HOW MANY pages are read, not WHAT fields — no new struct field, no schema change, no new evidence kind; the reflection-based structural over-collection test and the schemaregistry drift/bijection test both stay green. RULE configuration only — still NEVER flow logs, packet captures, traffic contents, NAT-rule secrets, threat-intel feeds, or route tables. **DoS backstops kept + added:** the run-wide `maxRuleCollectionGroupsPerRun` (2000) and per-policy `maxRuleCollectionGroupsPerPolicy` (200) record caps are retained as the DoS backstop (paginate UP TO the cap, then stop and report honestly — cap-hit is not an error); two page caps (`maxFirewallPolicyPages`, `maxRuleCollectionGroupPages` = 100 each) are added so a pathological / self-pointing `nextLink` terminates rather than looping forever. **Anti-criteria (P0):** no widening beyond ARM **Reader** (P0-634-1); the per-run cap is NOT removed (P0-634-2). Neutral fixtures only (obviously-fake `00000000-...` ids + a fake `$skiptoken` continuation against the 127.0.0.1 httptest server, no real tokens/GUIDs). Tests: a multi-page cursor-walk case for the policy list AND for the per-policy group list (page1 `nextLink` → page2 none → all records collected across pages, all GET) + a `nextLink`-loop termination case (a self-pointing cursor stops at the per-policy page cap, reports what it gathered, no read error). `connectors/azure/internal/firewall` coverage holds (measured 93.3%, floor 90). **Type: STANDARD.** Decisions + detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/634-azure-firewall-pagination-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 614. Slice doc: `docs/issues/634-azure-firewall-rulecollectiongroup-pagination.md`. (#634)
- **feat(oscal): slice 620 — map an unmapped vendor claim to an SCF anchor.** Adds the operator mapping flow that slice 589's vendor-claims view deferred (its decisions-log D8): an `grc_engineer` maps a slice-512 UNMAPPED claim (`scf_anchor_id IS NULL`, shown by the "Unmapped to SCF" badge) to a canonical SCF anchor. New write endpoint `PATCH /v1/oscal/component-claims/{id}/scf-anchor` with body `{scf_anchor_id}` (the SCF code, e.g. `IAC-06`): approver/admin-gated, tenant-scoped under RLS, validates the anchor resolves against the bundled `scf_anchors` catalog (422 on an unknown code; 404 on an unknown/cross-tenant claim), sets the claim's `scf_anchor_id`, and appends an append-only mapping-audit row. The audit reuses the slice-589 `imported_component_claim_dispositions` table, generalized into a claim **event log** (`event_kind` discriminator + nullable `from_scf_anchor_id`/`to_scf_anchor_id`; the disposition `to_status` CHECK now constrains only `'disposition'` events) — same append-only RLS-scoped pattern, clean anchor-transition semantics. **Anti-criteria (P0):** requirement → SCF anchor only (invariant #7) — no claim → claim mapping; mapping NEVER fabricates control coverage — the claim stays a claim (`is_vendor_claim`/`claim_status` untouched) and nothing writes to `control_evaluations` (an integration test asserts the table stays empty after a mapping, mirroring the slice-619 boundary discipline). The web vendor-claims detail view gains a searchable SCF-anchor picker on each unmapped claim; on success the badge clears and the mapped anchor shows. New BFF route `PATCH /api/oscal/component-claims/[id]/scf-anchor` + `mapClaimScfAnchor` client; the picker reuses the existing `GET /v1/anchors` catalog read (no new read endpoint). Tests: Go unit (handler validators/branches) + integration (PATCH under RLS, audit-row write, 422/404/403/cross-tenant-denied, no-fabricated-coverage) + Playwright e2e (the map flow, BFF-GET route-mocked, hermetic) + web vitest (the new BFF route). New web per-file coverage floor for the BFF route. **Type: JUDGMENT** — the anchor-picker UX, the mapping-write validation shape, the audit-row reuse, and the no-fabricated-coverage guard are recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/620-map-claim-scf-anchor-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 589. Slice doc: `docs/issues/620-map-unmapped-vendor-claim-to-scf-anchor.md`. (#620)
- **feat(connectors): slice 615 — Azure Key-Vault RBAC role-assignment enumeration.** Closes the slice-521 gap (521 D3/D5): for a Key Vault in **Azure RBAC authorization** mode (`enableRbacAuthorization=true`) the `connectors/azure/internal/keyvault` collector now issues a SECOND read-only ARM read — `Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments` scoped to the vault resource id — and merges each into the existing `access_entries` list as a `principal_type=rbac_role_assignment` entry (principal object id + resolved role definition **name**, e.g. `Key Vault Reader`, `Key Vault Secrets User`). This makes the least-privilege read **symmetric** across both vault access models (legacy access-policy vaults were already enumerated in full), completing the IAC-21 privileged-account-management signal for RBAC vaults. **Read shape (JUDGMENT, D1):** the second read lives inside `Client.ListVaults` so the `keyvault.API` seam and the `Inspect` collector are byte-for-byte unchanged — an RBAC vault's principals come from roleAssignments exactly as a legacy vault's come from `accessPolicies`; a legacy vault triggers NO second read.
