Role **names** are resolved via a bounded, per-run-cached `roleDefinitions` lookup (Reader can read role definitions); a failed/empty lookup falls back to the bare role-definition guid so the evidence still names the role unambiguously. **NO schema change (AC-2):** `azure.keyvault_access_config.v1` already carries the discriminated `access_entries[].principal_type` enum (`access_policy | rbac_role_assignment`) + `role_name` (the drift bijection passes unchanged). **Over-collection guard (the slice-521 structural pin, extended to the new path, D2):** roleAssignments + roleDefinitions are wholly **management-plane** METADATA (principal id + role name) — the `keyvault.AccessEntry` struct still has **no field** capable of holding a secret/key/certificate value, so the RBAC path cannot over-collect by construction; the reflection structural test covers the type and a new `TestRBACRoleAssignment_MetadataOnly_PinsNewPath` pins the path. The connector reads ARM **Reader** only (P0-615-3) and NEVER the Key-Vault data plane (`vault.azure.net`, P0-615-2) — the client test asserts no roleAssignments call is ever issued for a legacy vault and the data plane is never touched. **DoS guard (threat-model D):** the per-vault read is a single bounded page truncated to `maxRoleAssignmentsPerVault` (200), the run-wide total is capped at `maxRoleAssignmentsPerRun` (2000), and distinct role-definition lookups are capped at `maxRoleDefinitionLookupsPerRun` (100); a per-vault read error marks that vault INCONCLUSIVE (fail-soft) without failing the run. Cursor pagination for a huge estate is a documented follow-on (spillover slice 623), NOT implemented here. Push-only (invariant #3); `profiles_supported=[pull]`; stable `actor_id` `connector:azure:keyvault@<version>`; hour-truncated `observed_at`; the `keyvault` coverage floor (90) holds (measured 91.5%). **Type: JUDGMENT** — the read-shape choice, the role-name-resolution scope-minimum, and the over-collection-guard mechanism recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/615-azure-keyvault-rbac-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 521. Slice doc: `docs/issues/615-azure-keyvault-rbac-role-assignment-enumeration.md`. (#615)
- **feat(connectors): slice 523 — Kubernetes NetworkPolicy coverage evidence.** Adds a third evidence kind to the Kubernetes connector (slice 487): `k8s.networkpolicy_coverage.v1`. A new `connectors/k8s/internal/netpol` collector reads, read-only via the Kubernetes API (`get,list` on `networking.k8s.io/v1` networkpolicies + core namespaces), the per-namespace NetworkPolicy segmentation posture: how many NetworkPolicies exist, a per-policy SPEC summary (name, the directions it governs, whether its `podSelector` is empty i.e. selects every pod, and a **count** of its ingress/egress rule blocks), and the derived **default-deny** assessment per direction. The recurring SOC 2 CC6.6 / ISO A.8 "prove network segmentation between workloads" demand previously needed a manual upload. **Coverage assessment (JUDGMENT):** a namespace is default-deny in a direction when at least one all-pods (empty-`podSelector`) policy governs that direction with **zero** allow rules — the Kubernetes canonical default-deny shape; `PASS` when default-deny holds for at least one direction, `FAIL` when none (unprotected or per-pod-allow-only), `INCONCLUSIVE` on a per-namespace read error; the platform evaluator owns the final (control, namespace) call. **Over-collection guard (structural, the locked pattern):** NetworkPolicy SPEC metadata **only** — the netpol HTTP client **counts** ingress/egress rule blocks but decodes them as opaque `json.RawMessage`, so the peer / CIDR / namespaceSelector / port contents inside a rule block are never materialized into Go memory; the `RawPolicy` / `Coverage` structs carry **no field** for a peer or selector value, and the connector reads **no** pod contents / env / Secret / ConfigMap / log / exec / traffic. The netpol client test serves a policy embedding a podSelector label, an ingress peer namespaceSelector label, a CIDR, and a port and asserts none escapes; the integration over-collection test allow-lists top-level **and** nested per-policy keys. The connector's documented read-only ClusterRole gains exactly one rule — `networking.k8s.io: networkpolicies` verbs `get,list` (no write verb, no `secrets`, no wildcard); the `DocumentedClusterRole` least-privilege test + `atlas-k8s permissions` render + README stay in sync. Schema `k8s.networkpolicy_coverage/1.0.0.json` carries `x-default-scf-anchors` `["NET-04", "NET-01"]` (boundary-protection + network-security) and is wired into `schemaregistry.DefaultSeed` (drift bijection passes). Push-only (invariant #3); `profiles_supported` stays `[pull]`; stable `actor_id` `connector:k8s:netpol@<version>`; hour-truncated `observed_at`; new `idem.NetpolCoverageKey`; per-namespace `namespace` scope dimension (one record per namespace); `--netpol-control` (default `scf:NET-04`) + `--skip-netpol`. New `connectors/k8s/internal/netpol` coverage floor (93). **Type: JUDGMENT** — the evidence-kind shape, the scf-anchor pair, the default-deny coverage-assessment call, and the SPEC-metadata-only over-collection scope-minimum are recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/523-k8s-networkpolicy-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 487. Slice doc: `docs/issues/523-k8s-networkpolicy-evidence.md`. (#523)
- **feat(oscal): slice 619 — accepted vendor claim → vendor-attested SSP control-implementation.** When an operator has ACCEPTED a vendor's control-implementation claim (slice 512 import + slice 589 disposition), the OSCAL SSP export now surfaces it as an OSCAL `by-component` control-implementation statement — clearly attributed to a dedicated vendor `SystemComponent` (distinct from `this-system`, flagged `vendor-attested` / `operator-credited`), leading with an unmistakable `[VENDOR-ATTESTED — … NOT platform-verified evidence]` label, carrying the vendor's statement verbatim plus the accept-provenance (`accepted-by`, `accepted-at`, `claim-id`, `disposition=accepted`). **THE CONSTITUTIONAL BOUNDARY (P0-619, inherits P0-512-1 / invariant #2):** a vendor claim is an ASSERTION the operator credited, NOT platform-verified evidence, so it can NEVER satisfy a control or contribute to coverage. This is enforced structurally: accepted claims travel in a SEPARATE proto field (`SspInput.vendor_attested_implementations`), are NEVER merged into `control_implementations`, carry **no `evaluation-result` prop**, and the export writes NOTHING — it is one read-only `SELECT` (`ListAcceptedVendorClaimsForExport`, `claim_status='accepted'`) over `imported_component_claims`. The load-bearing integration test (`internal/oscal/vendor_attested_ssp_integration_test.go`) asserts the `control_evaluations` row count is IDENTICAL before and after the export (no fabricated coverage), that the accepted claim appears vendor-attested with provenance, and that REJECTED / needs_info claims do NOT appear. Rejected/needs_info/asserted claims are excluded entirely. **Unmapped (NULL `scf_anchor`) accepted claims are still exported** (raw `control_id` as the OSCAL token) — hiding a credited operator decision would defeat the transparency this surface exists for (D3); slice 620 can later enrich the `scf-id`. No new HTTP route (so no `openapi.yaml` change) and no migration — a pure additive read inside the existing `Export` → `Aggregate` → bridge `SerializeSSP` pipeline; sqlc + Go proto + Python bridge stubs (`grpcio-tools==1.80.0`, `GRPC_GENERATED_VERSION='1.80.0'`) regenerated, no drift. **Type: JUDGMENT** — the by-component shape, the separate-field boundary, the no-evaluation-result guard, the tenant-wide (not period-frozen) read, and the export-unmapped decision are recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/619-accepted-vendor-claim-to-ssp-evidence-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 589. Slice doc: `docs/issues/619-accepted-vendor-claim-to-ssp-evidence.md`. (#619)
- **feat(connectors): slice 521 — Azure Key-Vault access-policy / RBAC evidence.** Adds a fifth evidence kind to the Azure connector (slices 486 / 519 / 520): `azure.keyvault_access_config.v1`. A new `connectors/azure/internal/keyvault` collector reads, read-only via Azure Resource Manager (ARM **Reader** role — **no** new Azure scope, P0-521-3), each Key Vault's **management-plane** posture: the access model (legacy access policies vs Azure RBAC authorization), purge-protection + soft-delete state, public-network-access posture, network-ACL default action, and the principals entitled to the vault (legacy access-policy entries with their granted keys/secrets/certificates permission **verbs**, plus the RBAC-mode flag). **Over-collection guard (the slice-520 gold standard, load-bearing here because Key Vault holds secrets):** the connector reads the ARM management plane **only** (vault list + access policies) and **never** the Key-Vault **data plane** (`vault.azure.net` secret/key/certificate get); it requires **no** Key-Vault data-plane role (`Key Vault Secrets User`, `Key Vault Crypto User`, etc. — the over-privilege trap, P0-521-1) and the collector struct has **no field** for any secret/key/certificate value (P0-521-2). A structural reflection test fails the build if any struct field name hints at secret material; the integration over-collection test descends into the access-entry list applying a per-kind allow-list. Schema `azure.keyvault_access_config/1.0.0.json` carries `x-default-scf-anchors` `["IAC-21", "CRY-09"]` (privileged-account-management + cryptographic-key-management) and is wired into `schemaregistry.DefaultSeed` (drift bijection passes). Push-only (invariant #3); `profiles_supported=[pull]`; stable `actor_id` `connector:azure:keyvault@<version>`; hour-truncated `observed_at`; new `idem.KeyVaultAccessKey`; `--keyvault-control` (default `scf:CRY-09`) + `--skip-keyvault`. New `connectors/azure/internal/keyvault` coverage floor (90). **Type: JUDGMENT** — the evidence-kind shape, the scf-anchor pair, and the management-plane-only / no-secret-value scope-minimum are recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/521-azure-keyvault-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 486. Slice doc: `docs/issues/521-azure-keyvault-access-policy-evidence.md`. (#521)
- **fix(evidence): slice 474 — align the ingest content-hash so the ledger-wide verify walk validates production records.** Slice 464's `atlas evidence verify` walk recomputes each record's canonical hash and compares it to the stored `hash`, but a freshly-ingested production record always reported a mismatch: ingest hashes the FULL `EvidenceRecord` proto **including the wire `scope`** (the EVIDENCE_SDK receipt-hash contract clients reproduce), while the ledger never persisted that wire scope, so the verify reconstructed a scope-free record and recomputed a different hash (slice 464 decisions log D1). This slice persists the canonical (sorted) wire scope at ingest in a new additive, nullable `scope_canonical JSONB` column (migration `20260608040000_evidence_scope_canonical.sql`); the verify walk rehydrates it before recomputing. The stored hash semantics are **unchanged** — only the verify input is now faithful, so a record pushed via the real `Process` verifies clean with no baseline-stamp workaround (AC-1) and any tampered hash-contributing column (payload **or** scope) is still detected (AC-2). **Append-only invariant #2 respected:** the migration is purely additive and rewrites no existing evidence row; pre-slice-474 rows carry `scope_canonical = NULL` and the verify walk falls back to the slice-464 scope-free reconstruction for them (no backfill, no evidence mutation). The shared canonical-scope codec lives in `internal/canonjson` (`MarshalCanonicalScope` / `UnmarshalCanonicalScope`) so ingest and verify agree by construction. **Type: JUDGMENT** — the canonical-hash input choice (persist scope vs change hash form, weighed against the EVIDENCE_SDK wire contract) and the existing-records reconciliation are recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/474-ingest-hash-align-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 464. Slice doc: `docs/issues/474-ingest-hash-ledger-reconstructable.md`. (#474)

- **feat(control): slice 608 — per-tenant control-bundle upload gate policy (`bundle_gate_mode`).** Slice 574 wired the control-bundle upload test-gate into `POST /v1/controls:upload-bundle` with a single GLOBAL v0 policy (hard-block a bundle whose `tests/` are red; allow-with-warning a bundle that ships no tests) and deferred a per-tenant flag (574 D-POLICY-3). This slice ships that per-tenant policy as ONE column on the existing `tenants` identity row (migration `20260608030000_tenant_bundle_gate_mode.sql`): a single `bundle_gate_mode TEXT` enum with a DB CHECK over `('strict','advisory','mandatory_tests')`, **defaulting to `strict`** so an existing/unset tenant keeps the slice-574 behaviour with NO backfill (the resolver treats an absent tenants row as `strict` too). **`strict`** = the slice-574 default (red tests hard-block; no-tests allowed with a warning). **`advisory`** = a red bundle is ACCEPTED with the per-case report attached as `gate_warning` + a new `gate_test_report` field (AC-3) — for tenants authoring iteratively. **`mandatory_tests`** = a bundle that ships NO `tests/` is REJECTED (AC-4); red tests still hard-block (a stricter posture, never looser). The gate (`internal/api/controls/gate.go`) takes the resolved mode as a pure input; the handler resolves it via the new read-only `control.Store.BundleGateMode` (RLS-scoped, invariant #2). **Settings surface (AC-5):** the existing admin-gated `PATCH /v1/tenants/{id}` (slice 144) now also accepts `bundle_gate_mode` (alongside or instead of `name`), writing a `tenant_gate_policy_update` me_audit_log row (CHECK extended in the same migration). **Tenant isolation (AC-6):** the policy read is RLS-scoped, proven at the Store level (tenant A advisory does not leak to tenant B strict) plus HTTP-level default/advisory/mandatory integration tests against real Postgres. No new sqlc table; `GetTenantBundleGateMode` + `UpdateTenantBundleGateMode` queries added and dbx regenerated (sqlc v1.31.1, no drift). A web settings toggle is deferred to spillover slice 613. **Type: JUDGMENT** — policy-values / strict default / resolution precedence / settings-surface call recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/608-per-tenant-bundle-gate-policy-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 574. Slice doc: `docs/issues/608-per-tenant-bundle-test-gate-policy.md`. (#608)
- **feat(web): slice 613 — Settings control for the per-tenant control-bundle gate policy.** Slice 608 made the per-tenant control-bundle upload gate configurable (`tenants.bundle_gate_mode` ∈ `strict | advisory | mandatory_tests`, default `strict`) and exposed it on the admin-gated `PATCH /v1/tenants/{id}`, but deferred the web control (608 D5). This slice surfaces it: the Settings → Tenant section (admin/super_admin only, the same authority that gates the PATCH) gains a `<select>` of the three modes with a one-line explanation of each mode's effect (AC-3: strict = block red tests, allow no-tests; advisory = accept red with a warning; mandatory_tests = reject a no-tests bundle). Changing the control and Saving PATCHes `/v1/tenants/{id}` with `{bundle_gate_mode}` via the existing slice-144 BFF passthrough route (a verbatim body forwarder — no BFF or backend change), and the control reflects the persisted value the PATCH response returns. **READ-PATH JUDGMENT (D1):** `main` exposes no GET that returns a single tenant's `bundle_gate_mode` to the web layer (`/v1/me/tenants` is a JWT-claim directory of id/name/current; the admin tenants list omits the column), and adding one would be a backend change outside this web-only slice — so the control pre-selects the documented default (`strict`, which is exactly what the gate enforces for an unchanged tenant) and reflects the authoritative value on save, surfacing the pending-vs-persisted distinction honestly. The mode set + labels + AC-3 explanations + a fail-safe-toward-strict parser live in the new pure helper `app/(authed)/settings/gate-mode.ts` (vitest: 24 cases, 100% coverage, floored at 98%); a hermetic Playwright spec (`e2e/settings-gate-policy.spec.ts`) route-mocks the `/api/me/tenants` GET (deterministic current tenant) and the PATCH response (known mode) — it does NOT rely on the shared-DB seed for state (b219 lesson) — asserting the control renders the default, swaps explanations on change, PATCHes the chosen mode, and reflects the persisted value. **Anti-criteria (P0):** WEB-ONLY — no `internal/` Go change, no new backend endpoint or BFF route (608 owns the API). **Type: code** (JUDGMENT-light — the read-path call recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/613-web-bundle-gate-policy-toggle-decisions.md`). Parent: slice 608. Slice doc: `docs/issues/613-web-bundle-gate-policy-toggle.md`. (#613)
- **feat(connectors): slice 519 — Azure AKS managed-cluster configuration evidence.** Adds a third evidence surface to the slice-486 Azure connector: `azure.aks_cluster_config.v1`, the AKS (Azure Kubernetes Service) managed-cluster hardening posture read read-only via Azure Resource Manager (the SAME ARM **Reader** role the storage kind uses — no new Azure scope, P0-519-2). A new `connectors/azure/internal/aks` collector + ARM client lists `Microsoft.ContainerService/managedClusters` and emits, per cluster, the compliance-relevant management-plane settings: `rbac_enabled`, `network_policy`, `private_cluster`, `authorized_ip_ranges`, `managed_identity`, `local_accounts_disabled`, `oidc_issuer_enabled`, `kubernetes_version`, and `node_pool_count`. The kind ships a JSON Schema with `x-default-scf-anchors: ["CFG-02", "NET-04"]` (cloud-config + network controls), wired into `schemaregistry.DefaultSeed` (the schema-drift bijection passes with the new kind). **Over-collection guard (structural):** the `aks.ClusterConfig` / `aks.RawCluster` structs carry CONFIGURATION flags ONLY — there is no field for admin kubeconfig, cluster-admin credentials, service-principal secrets, workload/pod manifests, or container contents — and the ARM client issues a `GET` to the managed-cluster **list** surface ONLY, NEVER `listClusterAdminCredential` (the privilege-escalating admin-kubeconfig API, P0-519-1 / P0-519-3). A reflection test over both structs fails the build on any secret/credential/kubeconfig/workload field name; the client test asserts the read is GET-only with no `credential` path; the integration no-PII test enforces a per-kind payload allow-list. New `run` subcommand flags `--aks-control` (default `scf:CFG-02`) + `--skip-aks`; `register` now announces three `supported_kinds`. NO platform API route, NO migration — evidence flows through the slice-013 ledger via the single `IngestEvidence` push RPC (invariant #3); `profiles_supported` stays `[pull]` with an honestly-named operator-scheduled interval. New package enrolled in `cmd/scripts/coverage-thresholds.json` at a 90% floor (measured 94.0%). **Type: JUDGMENT** — evidence-kind shape, field set, SCF anchors, scope minimum, and the structural over-collection boundary recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/519-azure-aks-config-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 486. Slice doc: `docs/issues/519-azure-aks-workload-config-evidence.md`. (#519)
- **feat(connectors): slice 520 — Azure NSG / firewall rule evidence.** Adds a fourth evidence surface to the slice-486 Azure connector: `azure.nsg_rules.v1`, the Network Security Group (NSG) firewall-rule posture read read-only via Azure Resource Manager (the SAME ARM **Reader** role the storage + AKS kinds use — no new Azure scope, P0-520-1). A new `connectors/azure/internal/nsg` collector + ARM client lists `Microsoft.Network/networkSecurityGroups` and emits, per NSG, the operator-authored inbound/outbound security rules (`name`, `direction`, `access` allow/deny, `protocol`, source/destination address-prefix, source/destination port range, `priority`) plus summary counts of subnet / NIC associations — the minimum that demonstrates the network-segmentation control (e.g. "no NSG allows 0.0.0.0/0 inbound on management ports 22/3389"). The ARM default security rules (platform boilerplate) are deliberately excluded. The kind ships a JSON Schema with `x-default-scf-anchors: ["NET-04", "NET-01"]` (Boundary Protection + Network Security), wired into `schemaregistry.DefaultSeed` (the schema-drift bijection passes with the new kind). **Over-collection guard (structural):** the `nsg.GroupConfig` / `nsg.SecurityRule` / `nsg.RawGroup` structs carry RULE metadata ONLY — there is no field for flow logs, packet captures, traffic contents, secrets, or PII — and the ARM client issues a `GET` to the NSG **list** surface ONLY, never a mutate (P0-520-2 / P0-520-3). A reflection test over the structs fails the build on any flow-log/packet/traffic-content/secret field name; the client test asserts the read is GET-only; the integration no-PII test enforces a per-kind payload allow-list that descends into the NSG rule list. The connector verdicts the deterministic segmentation posture: `FAIL` when a rule allows inbound from the whole Internet to a management port (SSH 22 / RDP 3389), `INCONCLUSIVE` on a per-NSG read error, `PASS` otherwise — descriptive only; the evaluator owns the binding decision. New `run` subcommand flags `--nsg-control` (default `scf:NET-04`) + `--skip-nsg`; `register` now announces four `supported_kinds`. NO platform API route, NO migration — evidence flows through the slice-013 ledger via the single `IngestEvidence` push RPC (invariant #3); `profiles_supported` stays `[pull]` with an honestly-named operator-scheduled interval. New package enrolled in `cmd/scripts/coverage-thresholds.json` at a 90% floor (measured 92.0%). **Type: JUDGMENT** — evidence-kind shape, rule field set, SCF anchors, scope minimum, and the structural over-collection boundary recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/520-azure-nsg-firewall-evidence-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 486; sibling of slice 519. Slice doc: `docs/issues/520-azure-nsg-firewall-evidence.md`. (#520)

- **feat(oscal): slice 589 — vendor-claim read API + operator accept/reject/needs-info disposition.** Slice 512 lands a vendor's OSCAL component-definition as vendor-attributed CLAIMS (`imported_component_claims`, `is_vendor_claim=TRUE`, `claim_status='asserted'`) but ships no read surface and no operator action. This slice closes that gap. **Read API:** `GET /v1/oscal/component-definitions` (a tenant's imported component-definitions, most-recent-first) + `GET /v1/oscal/component-definitions/{id}` (one import's components + their vendor claims, flattened with the SCF-anchor mapping + an `unmapped` flag for the slice-512 `scf_anchor_id IS NULL` claims) — mirroring the slice-599 `oscalprovenance` read pattern: a pure SELECT over persisted Postgres rows, tenant-scoped via RLS + a defense-in-depth `tenant_id` predicate + a handler-level read gate (admin | grc_engineer | control_owner), and **never touching the compliance-trestle bridge** (the integration suite runs bridge-free). The `{id}` read is kind-pinned to `'component_definition'` so a profile/catalog id 404s. **Disposition write:** `POST /v1/oscal/component-claims/{id}:accept` / `:reject` / `:needs-info` (the colon-verb shape mirrors the slice-025 walkthroughs `/{id}:finalize` precedent) — gated tighter on `grc_engineer` (IsApprover) | IsAdmin (the slice-021 exceptions precedent: adjudicating a vendor's assertion is a write, not a view). **THE LOAD-BEARING BOUNDARY (canvas invariant #2 / P0-512-1):** a vendor claim is an ASSERTION, not platform-verified evidence — the disposition writes ONLY the claim's `claim_status` + `dispositioned_by`/`dispositioned_at`/`disposition_note` metadata and an append-only `imported_component_claim_dispositions` audit row; it NEVER writes to `control_evaluations` or the evidence ledger, and the slice-512 `is_vendor_claim = TRUE` CHECK is left untouched (the integration test asserts `control_evaluations` stays empty and `is_vendor_claim` stays true after an accept). Accepting a claim records that the operator CREDITS the vendor's assertion; it does not satisfy a control. Migration `20260608050000_oscal_component_claim_disposition.sql` widens the slice-512 `claim_status` CHECK to add `needs_info` and adds the audit table (paired `.down.sql`, verified reversible). **Web:** a `/oscal/component-definitions` list + per-import claims view (nav "Vendor Claims") with per-claim accept/reject/needs-info, an explicit vendor-claim-is-assertion disclaimer, and the unmapped flag; the Playwright spec is hermetic (route-mocks the BFF GET/POST, no shared-DB seed — the b219 lesson). sqlc queries added + dbx regenerated (v1.31.1, no drift); `routes.go` + `docs/openapi.yaml` regenerated (no drift); new package `internal/api/oscalcomponents` enrolled in `scripts/integration-shards.txt` (shard A) at a 78% coverage floor (measured 80.6%). **Type: JUDGMENT** — disposition states, the claim-is-assertion boundary, read-API shape, and UI framing recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/589-component-claim-read-and-disposition-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 512. Slice doc: `docs/issues/589-component-claim-read-and-operator-disposition.md`. Spillovers: #619 (accepted-claim → SSP evidence), #620 (map-unmapped-claim UX). (#589)
- **feat(oscal): slice 599 — OSCAL resolved-chain provenance read API.** Slice 578 records the resolved import chain as provenance in the `imported_catalog_audit_log.detail` JSON of the `profile_imported` success row — a `chain` array of `{role, sha256, bytes}` entries (entry profile + intermediate profiles + catalogs) plus a `chain_depth` count — but that provenance was WRITTEN with no read surface: it was only queryable by reading the raw audit-log JSON. This slice adds the read path so an operator or auditor can prove, for an imported profile baseline, the exact ordered chain of documents (and their sha256 hashes) that resolved it — the "diligence the diligence tool" provenance story for chained imports. **Route:** `GET /v1/oscal/imported-profiles/{id}/provenance` returns `{baseline_id, profile_title, source_label, oscal_version, source_sha256, imported_at, chain_depth, chain[]}` where each chain entry is `{role, sha256, bytes}` in document order (AC-1). A single-level slice-511 import returns its two-element `[entry-profile, catalog]` chain without special-casing (AC-2). **Tenant-scoped (AC-3):** the read rides RLS plus an explicit `tenant_id` predicate on a new sqlc query (`GetProfileImportProvenance`) that joins `imported_catalogs` (confirming `kind = 'profile'`) to its `profile_imported` audit row; a cross-tenant or unknown id returns 404 (the integration suite asserts tenant A cannot read tenant B's provenance, and that tenant B can read its own). The read is a pure SELECT over the existing append-only audit-log table — **no migration, no write path** (constitutional invariant #2) — and **never touches the compliance-trestle bridge** (the provenance is already persisted in Postgres), so the read path is testable with seeded DB rows alone and runs in CI without the Python bridge. New read package `internal/api/oscalprovenance` (Store + Handler mirroring the slice-064 `controldetail` read pattern: per-request tenant-GUC transaction, handler-level defense-in-depth role guard admitting admin + grc_engineer + control_owner — the OPA middleware is the primary gate). Enrolled in `scripts/integration-shards.txt` (Leg A) with a 88% coverage floor. **Type: code.** Parent: slice 578 (the write). Slice doc: `docs/issues/599-oscal-resolved-chain-provenance-read-surface.md`.
- **feat(control): slice 574 — control-bundle upload test-gate ("tests must pass to upload").** Slice 496 shipped the control-bundle test runner (`atlas-cli controls test`) but deliberately did NOT wire it into the upload path (P0-496-5). This slice closes that loop: `POST /v1/controls:upload-bundle` now runs a bundle's declared `tests/` cases through the SAME slice-496 runner (`bundletest`) before persisting, and — under the strict v0 policy — REJECTS the upload with a `400` + the per-case test report when any case fails or errors, so a control whose evidence query is provably wrong against its own fixtures never reaches the catalog. `ParseTarball` now captures the archive's top-level `tests/*.yaml` bytes into `control.Bundle.TestFiles`; a new in-memory `bundletest.RunFromFiles` evaluates them without writing the archive to disk. SQL-language fixtures run on the upload path (the handler holds a tenant tx via the new read-only `control.Store.WithReadOnlyTenantTx`, which is always rolled back — constitutional invariant #2: the gate evaluates, it never mutates evidence). A bundle with no `tests/` is accepted with a `gate_warning` (no-tests policy, matching the slice-496 CLI); per-tenant `require_bundle_tests_pass` config is deferred to slice 608. The CLI `controls upload` renders the gate's per-case report on a `400` so the author sees which fixture failed. JUDGMENT slice — gate-policy rationale (hard-block vs warn; no-tests handling; SQL fixtures) recorded in `docs/audit-log/574-control-bundle-test-upload-gate-decisions.md`. (#574)

- **feat(connectors): slice 590 — MDM software-inventory cursor pagination (Jamf + Intune).** Slice 555 added installed-software inventory evidence (`endpoint.software_inventory.v1`) to the Jamf + Intune MDM connectors but read only the **first bounded page** per MDM (Jamf `page-size=200`, Intune `$top=200`), so a fleet larger than one page — or, for Intune, an app catalog larger than one page of `detectedApps` — was silently truncated. This slice adds cursor pagination to the software read on both connectors so a large-fleet / large-catalog adopter gets a COMPLETE inventory, bounded so there is no unbounded loop (P0-590 / threat-model D). **Jamf** walks the `page` / `page-size` / `totalCount` offset cursor (`connectors/jamf/internal/devices/swclient.go`), incrementing `page` until `gathered >= totalCount` or an empty page (the defensive terminator for an older Jamf that omits `totalCount`) or the max-page cap. **Intune** follows the `@odata.nextLink` opaque skiptoken cursor (`connectors/intune/internal/devices/swclient.go`) — each subsequent page is the server's absolute `@odata.nextLink` requested verbatim via a new `getJSONAbsolute` client helper (the existing `getJSON` refactored to share `doGetJSON`) — until `nextLink` is absent or the cap; walking every page is REQUIRED because slice 555 inverts the app-centric `detectedApps` graph into a device-centric shape, so a device's apps that fall on later pages would otherwise be dropped (the exact bug 555 left open). Both connectors share a `maxSoftwarePages = 50` cap (at `page-size`/`$top` = 200 this bounds a run to 10,000 source records) so a non-converging `totalCount` or a non-terminating `nextLink` chain cannot drive an unbounded read. **The evidence kind `endpoint.software_inventory.v1`, the slice-555 over-collection allow-list (Jamf `GENERAL + APPLICATIONS` sections only; Intune `$select=id,displayName,version` + `$expand=managedDevices($select=id)`), the per-device `MaxSoftwarePerDevice = 500` ceiling, and the record builder / normalizer are all REUSED UNCHANGED — no schemaregistry change, no new kind, no migration; the decode structs gained ONLY the page-cursor fields (`totalCount`, `@odata.nextLink`), neither of which is collected evidence.** The schema-drift bijection (`internal/api/schemaregistry`, `internal/control`) passes unchanged, and the slice-555 over-collection tests still pass. A new multi-page cursor test on each connector drives a stateful TWO-page fixture, asserts the result is the UNION of both pages (Intune including a device whose apps span both pages and a page-2-only device), and asserts the loop terminates (exactly two pages served). Honest interval unchanged (`pull`, operator-scheduled, not continuous monitoring). **Type: STANDARD (JUDGMENT-light)** — the page size (reuse `pageLimit = 200`), cursor-state shape / termination, and max-page cap recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/590-mdm-swinv-pagination-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 555. Slice doc: `docs/issues/590-mdm-software-inventory-cursor-pagination.md`.
- **feat(web): slice 594 — Slack + webhook per-kind notification settings toggles.** Slice 583 widened the backend per-`(event, channel)` filter to admit the slice-543 `slack` + `webhook` delivery channels (migration `20260608020000_userprefs_slack_webhook_channels.sql` + `internal/auth/userprefs.Channels`), and the generic `GET/PATCH /v1/me/preferences` endpoint already accepts + returns those new cells — but the web Settings → Notifications matrix only rendered `in_app` + `email` columns, so a user could set a Slack/webhook per-kind opt-out only via the API. This slice adds the `slack` + `webhook` columns to that per-event matrix, mirroring the existing `in_app` / `email` columns exactly: each `NotificationRow` now renders four channel checkboxes, toggling a cell PATCHes `/v1/me/preferences` with the `{event:{slack|webhook:bool}}` shape (no new BFF route — the existing slice-108 preferences proxy is a verbatim passthrough that already carries the channels 583 widened), and a cell with no stored row renders enabled (the same slice-108 default-on-missing-row read the email column already uses). The per-kind slack/webhook cell reuses the slice-585 `configured` PRESENCE signal: when the operator has not configured a channel (`configured === false`), that channel's per-kind cells render **disabled** (setting a per-kind opt-out is meaningless if the channel can never deliver), mirroring the master toggle's disabled state; the `configured` reads share the `ChannelMasterToggle` query keys so TanStack Query dedupes (no extra round-trip), and a missing/undefined `configured` keeps the cell interactive (backward-tolerant). The master opt-in remains the OUTER runtime gate (the 583 filter); the per-kind grid mirrors the email column's independent editability (a master-OFF state does NOT disable the per-kind cell). **Anti-criteria (P0):** does NOT expose or accept a user-typed Slack/webhook URL or token — the matrix is a boolean opt-out grid only; the channel target stays operator-configured env (slice-543 P0-543-2 / SSRF boundary, unchanged); does NOT change the master opt-in toggle rows (slice 584 owns those); WEB-ONLY (no `internal/notify` or `internal/api/me` Go change). vitest covers the new column wire shape (`web/lib/api/me.test.ts`: `get/patchMyPreferences` round-trip slack + webhook cells verbatim); the Playwright settings spec asserts the slack/webhook columns render on every event row and that toggling a slack cell PATCHes `/v1/me/preferences`. **Type: code.** Parent: slice 583 (the backend filter); composes with slice 584 (the master toggle rows) + slice 108 (the per-event matrix this extends). Slice doc: `docs/issues/594-slack-webhook-per-kind-settings-ui.md`.
- **feat(oscal): slice 578 — chained OSCAL profile-over-profile resolution.** Slice 511 resolved an OSCAL profile against a supplied catalog, bounding the import chain to a single level (profile → catalog) by construction. This slice lifts that bound to a **bounded chain** (profile → profile → … → catalog): a profile whose `import.href` names ANOTHER supplied profile is resolved recursively until the chain bottoms out at a supplied catalog. **The two load-bearing safety properties are enforced Go-side and pure (no bridge, no Postgres) in `internal/oscal/profileimport/chain.go` so they are unit-testable without compliance-trestle (D1):** (1) **cycle detection** — a profile that imports itself directly (A → A) or transitively (A → B → A) is a structured error, never an infinite loop or fetch (P0-578-2); a depth-first walk tracks the profile keys on the current path and rejects on revisit, popping on exit so a legal diamond is not a false cycle; (2) the **chain-depth bound** `MaxChainDepth = 8` (D2 — the FedRAMP baselines are depth 1, an overlay is depth 2; 8 gives generous headroom while bounding a pathological/malicious chain) rejects a too-deep chain (AC-3). The slice-511 no-external-dereference guard carries forward **at every chain link** (P0-578-1 / P0-511-1): every `import.href` is matched ONLY against supplied documents, an external scheme is rejected before matching, and an href that names no supplied document is a structured error with no fetch. **Resolution stays delegated to compliance-trestle** (D1): once the graph is proven safe the bridge lays every supplied profile + catalog into its isolated sandbox, rewrites every href to a `trestle://` path, and `ProfileResolver` descends the chain natively — no parallel resolver. **Wire shape (D4):** the existing `ImportProfile` RPC gains an additive `repeated SuppliedProfile profiles = 4` field (no new RPC; a slice-511 single-level caller sends an empty list and gets identical behavior); Go bindings via `buf generate`, Python stubs via `uv run --with 'grpcio-tools==1.80.0' bash oscal-bridge/scripts/gen_proto.sh` (verified `GRPC_GENERATED_VERSION = '1.80.0'`). **Persistence reuses slice 511's shape** (`imported_catalogs.kind = 'profile'`, requirement → SCF anchor, transactional all-or-nothing) — no new table, no migration. The **resolved-chain provenance** (D6) is recorded in the `profile_imported` success-audit `detail` JSON: a `chain` array of `{role, sha256, bytes}` for the entry profile + every intermediate profile + every catalog, plus a `chain_depth` count, so an auditor can prove the exact chain material. The `atlas-oscal import-profile` CLI gains a repeatable `--profile` flag for intermediate profiles. The load-bearing properties are proven at BOTH tiers: pure-Go table tests (chained resolve validates, A→B→A rejected, depth-exceeded rejected, deep external href rejected, diamond is not a cycle) AND bridge-gated integration tests against a real Postgres + the real Python bridge (A→B→catalog resolves end-to-end through trestle; A→B→A rejected without loop with a rejection-audit row). **Type: JUDGMENT** — the depth bound, cycle-detection policy, delegation split, and wire shape recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/578-oscal-chained-profile-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 511. Slice doc: `docs/issues/578-oscal-chained-profile-resolution.md`. Spillover: `docs/issues/599-oscal-resolved-chain-provenance-read-surface.md`.
- **feat(connectors): slice 556 — MDM configuration-profile detail evidence (Jamf + Intune).** Slices 490 (posture summary) and 555 (software inventory) reported WHETHER a managed device is compliant and WHAT software it runs; this slice adds a third, scoped sibling kind `endpoint.config_profile.v1` reporting WHICH configuration / compliance profiles are deployed to a managed device and what compliance-relevant settings they enforce — evidence for configuration-management controls (auditors increasingly ask for the baseline itself: "show the screen-lock policy that is enforced", "show the disk-encryption configuration profile"), `x-default-scf-anchors = [CFG-02, CFG-04]` (Secure Baseline Configurations / Configuration Change Control). It is a SEPARATE kind, not an extension of posture or software-inventory, because it answers a different control question at a finer grain than the posture verdict. **THE LOAD-BEARING SECRET-REDACTION BOUNDARY (P0-556 / threat-model I): configuration profiles routinely embed SECRETS — Wi-Fi PSKs, VPN shared secrets, certificate private keys, API tokens, SCEP challenges, and arbitrary password / `<data>` / `PayloadContent` payload values — and NONE of these ever enters an evidence record.** Enforcement is structural at THREE layers (the slice-490/555 gold standard), so a leak is a compile error, a not-requested field, AND a dropped key: (1) the shared `cfgprofile.Setting` / `RawSetting` + the per-vendor `RawConfigSetting` Go types carry only an allow-listed `key` + a non-secret summary `value` — there is NO field anywhere in the type graph for a raw payload blob, a password, a private key, or a shared secret, so a leak would be a compile error; (2) the vendor clients request profile METADATA only at the API boundary — Jamf asks for the `GENERAL + CONFIGURATION_PROFILES` inventory sections (never `USER_AND_LOCATION` / GPS `location` / `APPLICATIONS`, and the section returns no raw payload blob), Intune `$select`s the device `id` and `$expand`s `deviceConfigurationStates($select=id,displayName,state)` (never `deviceName` / `userPrincipalName` / the raw setting payload), both read-only GETs; (3) the settings allow-list (`cfgprofile.AllowedSettingKeys`: `passcode_required`, `disk_encryption_enforced`, `firewall_enabled`, `screen_lock_enforced`, …) is the ONLY set of keys that may enter a record, with a belt-and-braces deny-list (`IsBannedSettingKey` — `password` / `secret` / `psk` / `privatekey` / `token` / `challenge` / `payloadcontent` / `certificate` / `data` / `pin` / …) re-applied at BOTH the normalizer and the record builder so even a hand-built `Device` cannot smuggle a secret. The required secret-redaction test `TestBuild_PayloadCarriesProfileFieldsOnly_NoSecrets` hand-builds a profile carrying `wifi_password` / `vpn_shared_secret` / `certificate_private_key` / `PayloadContent` and asserts only allow-listed keys reach the payload AND a recursive walk over the whole record finds none of the secret VALUES; per-connector client tests assert the request never asks for the banned sections/props. A new shared `connectors/mdm/{cfgprofile,cfgrecord}` package family mirrors the slice-490 `{devposture,devrecord}` / slice-555 `{swinventory,swrecord}` split (one normalizer + one record builder across both vendors), the `idem` package gains a kind-namespaced `ConfigProfileKey` (so a device's posture + software + config-profile records never collide on the same key for a given device+hour — proven by `TestConfigProfileKey_NamespacedFromSiblings`), and each connector gains a `run-config-profiles` subcommand (pull profile; honest 24h operator-scheduled cadence — NOT continuous monitoring). The per-device profile list is stable-sorted and bounded (`MaxProfilesPerDevice = 200`, `MaxSettingsPerProfile = 64`). Read-only credential is **identical** to `run` (no new MDM scope; no write/management permission — the remote-wipe guard from slice 490 is unchanged); records are `service` + required `--environment` scoped; `Result = INCONCLUSIVE` (the platform evaluator owns the pass/fail). Wired into `schemaregistry.DefaultSeed()` + the bundled JSON Schema (`endpoint.config_profile/1.0.0.json`); the schema-drift bijection passes. New floored packages `connectors/mdm/cfgprofile` (94) + `connectors/mdm/cfgrecord` (96). No migration — evidence flows through the slice-013 ledger. **v0 emits per-profile METADATA only** (the Jamf CONFIGURATION_PROFILES section + Intune deviceConfigurationStates are metadata-only; the `settings[]` array is wired end-to-end through the allow-list guard and tested via fixtures but empty for both vendors at v0) — per-setting enrichment through the SAME allow-list is spillover slice 595. **Type: JUDGMENT** — the separate-sibling-kind call, the per-device-roll-up shape, the configuration-profile field set, the SCF anchors, and THE secret-redaction allow-list / structural boundary recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/556-mdm-config-profile-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 490; sibling of slice 555. Slice doc: `docs/issues/556-mdm-config-profile-detail-evidence.md`.
- **feat(connectors): slice 555 — MDM software-inventory evidence (Jamf + Intune).** Slice 490 shipped the Jamf + Intune MDM connectors with a single posture-summary kind (`endpoint.device_posture.v1`) and **deliberately excluded the installed-application inventory** (Jamf `APPLICATIONS` section / Intune `detectedApps`) as the load-bearing over-collection guard (P0-490-3): a full app inventory is high-volume, PII-adjacent data the posture-summary control question does not need. This slice adds that inventory back as a **separate, scoped** evidence kind `endpoint.software_inventory.v1`, emitted by the SAME two connectors via the SAME locked pattern, because it answers a **different** control question — patch-/vulnerability-management and asset inventory ("are managed endpoints running patched, authorized software?"), `x-default-scf-anchors = [VPM-04, AST-03]`. **The load-bearing guard (P0-555 / threat-model I): each software item carries the app NAME + optional VERSION + optional bundle/package IDENTIFIER + optional INSTALL DATE only — NEVER file paths, per-user app-usage telemetry, license keys, device contents, browsing data, or owner personal contact detail.** Enforcement is structural at three layers (the slice-490 gold standard): (1) the shared `swinventory.SoftwareItem` + the per-vendor `RawSoftwareItem` Go types have NO field for a path / usage / license key — a leak would be a compile error; (2) the vendor clients request ONLY the software-relevant data at the API boundary — Jamf asks for the `GENERAL + APPLICATIONS` inventory sections (never `USER_AND_LOCATION` / GPS `location`), Intune `$select`s `id,displayName,version` on `detectedApps` with `managedDevices($select=id)` (never `sizeInByte`, `deviceName`, or `userPrincipalName`), both read-only GETs; (3) the record builder allow-lists every payload + per-item key. A new shared `connectors/mdm/{swinventory,swrecord}` package family mirrors the slice-490 `{devposture,devrecord}` split (one normalizer + one record builder across both vendors), the `idem` package gains a kind-namespaced `SoftwareInventoryKey` (so a device's posture record and its software record never collide on the same key for a given device+hour), and each connector gains a `run-software` subcommand (pull profile; honest 24h operator-scheduled cadence — NOT continuous monitoring). The per-device software list is stable-sorted and bounded (`MaxSoftwarePerDevice = 500`, a defensive over-collection ceiling). Read-only credential is **identical** to `run` (no new MDM scope; no write/management permission — the remote-wipe guard from slice 490 is unchanged); records are `service` + required `--environment` scoped; `Result = INCONCLUSIVE` (the platform evaluator owns known-vulnerable-version detection). Wired into `schemaregistry.DefaultSeed()` + the bundled JSON Schema (`endpoint.software_inventory/1.0.0.json`); the slice-068 schema-drift bijection passes. New floored packages `connectors/mdm/swinventory` (94) + `connectors/mdm/swrecord` (91). No migration — evidence flows through the slice-013 ledger. The over-collection guard is proven by `TestBuild_PayloadAndItemsCarrySoftwareFieldsOnly` (allow-listed keys + banned-substring assertion) plus per-connector client tests asserting the request never asks for the banned sections/props. **Type: JUDGMENT** — the separate-sibling-kind-vs-posture-extension call, the per-device-roll-up-vs-per-app-record shape, the software-field allow-list, the SCF anchors, and the over-collection-exclusion boundary recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/555-mdm-software-inventory-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 490. Slice doc: `docs/issues/555-mdm-software-inventory-evidence.md`.
- **feat(notify): slice 583 — per-kind filtering for Slack + webhook channels.** Slice 542 added per-notification-kind filtering for the EMAIL channel (on top of the master opt-in, a user mutes individual kinds via the slice-108 `user_notification_preferences` per-event `email` channel column); slice 543 shipped Slack + webhook with the MASTER opt-in only. This slice generalizes the slice-542 filter to those two channels so a user can mute specific kinds per channel. **(1) Channel-pref shape (D1):** reuses the slice-108 per-`(event, channel)` matrix — migration `20260608020000_userprefs_slack_webhook_channels.sql` widens the `user_notification_preferences_channel_check` CHECK from `('in_app', 'email')` to `('in_app', 'email', 'slack', 'webhook')` (monotonic widening, inserts no rows), and `internal/auth/userprefs.Channels` extends in the same PR (whitelist-move-together). **No new sqlc query / no dbx change** — the existing `ListUserNotificationPreferences` already returns `(event, channel, enabled)` rows; the filter just reads a different channel discriminator. **(2) Default semantics (D2):** identical to 542/566 — `master AND per-kind`, default-on-missing-row. The master channel opt-in (default opted-OUT) is the OUTER gate; a kind is suppressed for a channel ONLY by an explicit `enabled=false` row. Absent row / unmapped kind = deliver. Backward-compatible: no silent suppression of a previously-delivered kind. **(3) 542-generalization (D3):** the filter (kind→event map, per-kind decision, count-map narrowing, per-channel pref projection) is lifted from `internal/notify/email/kindfilter.go` into the shared `internal/notify` package as channel-agnostic functions (`KindToEvent` / `EnabledForKind` / `FilterCountsByChannelPref` / `ChannelPrefMap`); Slack + webhook consume them via a one-line dbx→`ChannelPrefRow` adapter, and the email package's helpers become thin delegates so email's wire output stays byte-identical (slice 543 D1) AND there is exactly ONE filter implementation. The existing slice-542 email unit tests pass unchanged. **(4) Scheduler path (D4):** the filter is applied INSIDE each channel's `DeliverDigest`, so the slice-582 fan-out scheduler honors it for free (no scheduler edit). **Anti-criteria (P0):** does NOT change recipient/target or tenant scoping (operates purely on the already-RLS-scoped in-memory count map, slice 542 P0-542-2); per-channel isolation (an EMAIL opt-out does NOT mute a kind on Slack/webhook — proven by test); the slice-543 SSRF/secret boundaries are untouched (this filters WHICH kinds deliver, not how). Four per-channel semantics (kind-on→deliver, kind-off→mute, no-row→default-on, email-unchanged) proven at the Go integration tier (real Postgres + RLS) per channel. Does NOT add a settings-UI column for the slack/webhook per-kind toggles (the new cells are settable via the generic `/v1/me/preferences` API; the dedicated UI is spillover slice 594) and does NOT edit `internal/api/me` (585's territory). **Type: JUDGMENT** — channel-pref shape, default-on semantics, and the shared-filter factoring recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/583-slack-webhook-per-kind-filtering-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 543. Slice doc: `docs/issues/583-slack-webhook-per-kind-filtering.md`.

- **feat(web): slice 585 — disabled "not configured" state for notification-channel toggles.** Slice 584 added the Slack + webhook master opt-in toggles (and slice 445 the email toggle) to Settings → Notifications, but all three rendered **unconditionally interactive** regardless of whether the OPERATOR had configured the channel's delivery target (SMTP env for email; `ATLAS_SLACK_*` for Slack; `ATLAS_WEBHOOK_*` for webhook). A user who opted into an unconfigured channel got a silent no-op (the opt-in persisted but delivery was inert server-side via `ErrNotConfigured`). This slice surfaces a `configured` boolean on the `GET`/`PUT /v1/me/{email,slack,webhook}-channel` response and disables the toggle + shows a muted "This channel is not configured by your administrator." note when `configured === false`. **The load-bearing boundary (P0-585 / P0-543-2 / threat-model S — info disclosure): `configured` is a PRESENCE signal only — it is derived from each channel's existing `Config.Enabled()` check (env set?), and the operator-configured target/secret (SMTP host/credentials, Slack/webhook URL/token) NEVER reaches the wire.** A unit test asserts `configured=true` when the env is set and `false` when unset, and that no target value appears in the response body. The webhook is reported `configured=true` only when its env URL is present AND passed the startup SSRF guard (a present-but-internal-target URL fell back to an inert transport, so it is honestly reported as not configured). The BFF proxies pass the new field through unchanged (verified by vitest on all three channel routes); the settings toggles treat a missing/undefined `configured` as configured (backward-tolerant — preserves the prior always-interactive behavior). The OpenAPI RouteSpec list is unchanged (it tracks method/path/tier, not response-body schema — drift guard green). A Playwright spec intercepts the BFF GET to inject `configured=false` and asserts the disabled toggle + note deterministically (the slice's route-level-assertion option, since the live docker-compose channel-env state is non-deterministic). **Type: code.** Parent: slice 584 (the toggle UI); extends slice 543 (the channel routes + operator env config). Slice doc: `docs/issues/585-channel-toggle-unconfigured-disabled-state.md`.
- **feat(oscal): slice 512 — OSCAL component-definition import (vendor control-implementation claims as evidence).** Slice 492 shipped OSCAL _catalog_ import and slice 511 _profile_ import; this slice adds _component-definition_ import — the inbound complement to the platform's own SSP export. A component-definition is the **vendor-side** artifact: a software/service vendor ships it to assert "this product implements control X this way" (per defined-component, a list of `control-implementations` → `implemented-requirements`, each targeting a control id with an implementation statement). The customer ingests each implemented-requirement as **vendor-attributed control-implementation evidence**. A new `ImportComponentDefinition` RPC extends the existing `oscal-bridge` (never a second process — P0-512-7); the bridge deserializes + validates via compliance-trestle's `ComponentDefinition` model. **The load-bearing invariant (P0-512-1 / threat-model E — elevation): a vendor's implemented-requirement is an ASSERTION, not platform-verified evidence — it does NOT auto-satisfy a control or fabricate coverage.** Imported requirements land as vendor-attributed CLAIMS (`is_vendor_claim=TRUE` CHECK-pinned, `claim_status='asserted'`); the persistence shape carries no satisfied/active boolean an import could flip, and the import writes ZERO `control_evaluations` rows (invariant #2 — ingestion never touches the evaluation surface) and ZERO `accepted` claims — surfacing a claim as satisfied requires the existing operator action, never automatic. This keeps the import inside the CLAUDE.md fabricate-coverage boundary even though no LLM is involved. **No external dereference (P0-512-2 / threat-model I):** `ComponentDefinition(**doc)` is a pure pydantic parse that fetches nothing; the bridge reads only in-document prose (requirement description + statement prose) and treats `links` / `source` / back-matter `href` as opaque metadata — proven by a bridge unit test that monkeypatches `socket.connect` to raise while parsing a document carrying external hrefs. Caps bound the expansion surface (threat-model D / AC-3): 16 MiB byte cap (Go-side before the wire + bridge-side), 1,000-component cap, 50,000 total-claim cap. A Go `internal/oscal/componentimport` caller persists the components + claims **transactionally** (a validation failure commits nothing — AC-5 / P0-512-4); each claim maps its target requirement → SCF anchor only (invariant #7 / P0-512-3), reusing slice-492's deterministic crosswalk (unmatched = `scf_anchor_id NULL`, "needs operator mapping", never dropped). The import-run provenance row + append-only audit log reuse the slice-492/511 tables (new `kind='component_definition'` discriminator value, `source='oscal-component-import'`, audit actions `component_definition_imported` / `component_definition_import_rejected`); the components + vendor claims live in two **new sibling tables** `imported_components` + `imported_component_claims` (migration `20260608010000_oscal_component_definitions.sql`, additive; four-policy FORCE RLS on both), because a component-definition is a vendor-CLAIM hierarchy, not a control set — keeping a claim distinguishable from a control row is what P0-512-1 depends on. `tenant`-scoped + `grc_engineer`-gated (P0-512-6); the bundled SCF spine (`scf_anchors`) is never touched (P0-512-5). CLI `atlas-oscal import-component-definition <file> [--json]` (AC-9). The Go-side reconciliation + no-auto-satisfy logic is testable WITHOUT the bridge (pure-Go `helpers_test.go`); the end-to-end import + the no-auto-satisfy invariant + tenant isolation + malformed-rollback are proven at the Go integration tier (real Postgres + real bridge, self-skipping when the compliance-trestle bridge subprocess is unavailable — slice-030 D2), and the parse + no-deref + cap enforcement at the bridge unit tier (pytest). **Type: JUDGMENT** — the sibling-tables-vs-`kind`-reuse model call, the schema-enforced CLAIM-not-satisfaction shape, the SCF reconciliation, the statement-flattening, and the no-external-deref design recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/512-oscal-component-definition-import-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 492; sibling of slice 511. Slice doc: `docs/issues/512-oscal-component-definition-import.md`.
- **feat(notify): slice 582 — notification-channel digest scheduler (fan-out driver).** Slices 445 (email) and 543 (Slack/webhook) each shipped the delivery SUBSTRATE — a per-user opt-in + a `DeliverDigest(ctx, userID, recipientUserID)` primitive that builds, claims, and delivers ONE user's minimum-disclosure digest — but neither shipped the DRIVER that walks all opted-in users on a schedule and calls `DeliverDigest` for each; the primitives were invoked only by tests. This slice adds that recurring driver: a new `internal/notify/scheduler` package — an **in-process daily tick-loop** (mirroring the slice-076 metrics + slice-510 backup schedulers — **no external cron** for the single-VM self-host target; `DefaultInterval` `24h`, overridable via `ATLAS_DIGEST_INTERVAL`) wired into `cmd/atlas/main.go` startup. **(1) Enumeration (D2):** three new sqlc queries (`ListEmailOptInUsers` / `ListSlackOptInUsers` / `ListWebhookOptInUsers`, each `SELECT tenant_id, user_id ... WHERE enabled = true`) run through the migrator (BYPASSRLS) pool — the ONE deliberate cross-tenant read, returning ONLY the `(tenant, user)` keys (no notification content, no PII). `enabled = false` / no-row are excluded by the `WHERE`, so **default opted-OUT** (P0-445-7 / P0-543-3) is honored at the SQL layer. **(2) Tenant isolation (canvas invariant #6):** each per-user delivery runs the EXISTING `DeliverDigest` under that user's OWN tenant context (`tenancy.WithTenant`), so every read inside the sink is RLS-scoped — Tenant A's notifications can never reach Tenant B's user (proven by `TestSweepOnce_TwoTenantsNoCross`). The slice-029 `recipient_user_id` passed is the user's UUID string, the contract the sinks already use. **(3) Idempotency (D3):** the driver adds NO new idempotency surface — it reuses each sink's claim-before-send (the per-UTC-day `digest_key` UNIQUE), so a second sweep the same day double-CALLS `DeliverDigest` but does NOT double-SEND (`TestSweepOnce_IdempotentNoDoubleSend`). **(4) Scope discipline (P0):** a delivery driver, NOT a producer — it reads the opt-in tables and drives the existing minimum-disclosure builders unchanged; it never writes a notification and never widens per-channel disclosure. Per-user + per-channel **try/log/continue** — one failing delivery or a channel enumeration error does not abort the sweep for the rest. The driver depends on a 1-method `DigestDeliverer` interface + an `OptInLister` func; thin per-channel adapters (`channels.go`) bind email/slack/webhook without the driver importing each `DeliveryResult` type. **(5) main.go mounting (D5):** only channels whose config is present are mounted (email when SMTP configured, Slack/webhook when a URL is set + passes the startup SSRF guard); no configured channel → scheduler not started. **Honest-interval discipline:** the digest period is named explicitly (per-UTC-day / daily), NOT marketed as "continuous monitoring". **No migration** — the existing slice-445/543 opt-in tables suffice. New floored package `internal/notify/scheduler` (floor 69, merged unit+integration 71.43%) + integration-shard enrolment (leg B2). **Type: JUDGMENT** — scheduling shape, enumeration mechanism, idempotency reuse, and channel-binding recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/582-digest-scheduler-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 543. Slice doc: `docs/issues/582-notification-channel-digest-scheduler.md`.
- **feat(notify): slice 543 — Slack + generic-webhook notification delivery channels.** Generalizes the slice-445 email delivery substrate (per-user opt-in + notification-store-as-source + claim-before-send idempotency + minimum-disclosure digest) to two more delivery sinks: a **Slack** incoming-webhook channel (Block Kit summary) and a **generic webhook** channel (flat JSON to PagerDuty / a SIEM / an internal bot). The shared cross-channel pieces live in a new `internal/notify` package (the `Summary` minimum-disclosure value object, closed type-label map, deep-link builder, the `Secret` redaction type, and the SSRF guard); each channel is a sibling package (`internal/notify/slack`, `internal/notify/webhook`) mirroring email's `Channel` orchestration — email's behavior is byte-identical (its tests pass unchanged). **JUDGMENT** (`docs/audit-log/543-additional-channels-decisions.md`): sibling-packages-over-plugin-registry (D1), per-channel disclosure shape (Slack mrkdwn-escaped Block Kit / flat webhook JSON, both counts + deep-link only), and secret/SSRF handling. **Security (threat-model load-bearing):** payloads carry summary counts + a deep-link only, never notification details/evidence (P0-543-1); the channel target (Slack URL / webhook URL) is **operator-configured env, never user-controlled free-text and never notification-derived** (P0-543-2); the webhook URL is **SSRF-validated at startup** — https-only, internal addresses (loopback / RFC1918 / link-local incl. `169.254.169.254` / ULA / CGNAT) rejected; Slack token + webhook bearer + HMAC signing secret are a `Secret` type that **redacts in every String()/format/JSON path and is scrubbed from any surfaced error** (P0-543-5); per-channel context-correct escaping; per-user opt-in **default opted-OUT** (P0-543-3); delivery sinks only — they never produce notifications (P0-543-4). New migration `20260608000000_slack_webhook_channels.sql` adds two opt-in tables + a `channel_delivery_log` ledger (four-policy FORCE RLS, per-channel idempotency UNIQUE). New routes `GET/PUT /v1/me/{slack,webhook}-channel`. Cross-tenant isolation + minimum-disclosure + SSRF-deny + secret-redaction + idempotency proven in unit + integration tests. Slice doc: `docs/issues/543-additional-notification-channels-slack-webhook.md`.
- **feat(web): slice 584 — Slack + webhook notification-channel settings toggles.** Slice 543 shipped the backend per-user opt-in routes for the two new delivery channels (`GET/PUT /v1/me/slack-channel` + `/v1/me/webhook-channel`, the slice-445 `{enabled}` shape) but never surfaced them in the web settings page, so a user could only flip them via the API. This slice adds a master opt-in **toggle row** for each channel to Settings → Notifications, mirroring the slice-445 email-delivery toggle exactly: two BFF proxies (`web/app/api/me/{slack,webhook}-channel/route.ts`) carry the session-cookie bearer server-side and pass the `{enabled}` body through unchanged; `web/lib/api/me.ts` gains `get/setSlackChannelOptIn` + `get/setWebhookChannelOptIn` over a shared `ChannelOptIn` helper; and `web/app/(authed)/settings/page.tsx` renders both rows via a generalized `ChannelMasterToggle` component (same fail-closed display — defaults off while loading/on-error — and disabled-while-pending shape as the email toggle). Each row carries the same minimum-disclosure copy ("summary counts + a deep-link only; never notification details"). **Security (SSRF boundary, P0-543-2):** the UI is a **boolean opt-in toggle only** — it does **not** expose or accept a user-typed Slack URL / webhook URL / token; the channel target is operator-configured env, validated server-side at startup (slice 543), and is never carried on the BFF wire. Default is opted-OUT (P0-543-3): a user with no opt-in row reads the toggle unchecked. vitest covers both new BFF routes (401-no-cookie / GET passthrough / PUT-forwards-`{enabled}`-with-bearer / upstream-path assertions); the Playwright settings spec gains AC-15 asserting both rows render opted-out by default and expose no free-text target input. Does NOT add new backend endpoints (slice 543 owns them) and does NOT let a user configure another user's opt-in (the routes bind tenant + user from the credential). Parent: slice 543. Slice doc: `docs/issues/584-notification-channel-settings-ui.md`.
- **feat(oscal): slice 511 — OSCAL profile import + resolution (resolve direction of invariant #8).** Slice 492 shipped OSCAL _catalog_ import; this slice adds _profile_ import — resolving an OSCAL profile's `import` / `merge` / `modify` directives against a supplied catalog into a concrete control baseline (the FedRAMP Low/Moderate/High shape: each is a profile over the NIST 800-53 catalog), then persisting that resolved set as a provenance-labeled imported baseline mapped requirement → SCF anchor (invariant #7). A new `ImportProfile` RPC extends the existing `oscal-bridge` (never a second process — P0-511-6); the bridge resolves via compliance-trestle's `ProfileResolver` (import-control selection, `merge`, `set-parameters` value substitution, and `alters`) **inside an isolated, ephemeral trestle workspace whose only catalogs are the ones the caller supplied**. **The load-bearing security property (P0-511-1 / threat-model I — information disclosure): the resolver NEVER dereferences an external `import.href`.** Every href is rewritten to a sandboxed `trestle://` path pointing at a supplied catalog before resolution; an `https://` / `sftp://` / `file://` / bare-path href is rejected as a structured error **with no fetch** (the external-href gate runs _before_ any positional catalog match, so a single-catalog import cannot smuggle an external reference through), and an href that maps to no supplied catalog errors the same way. Caps bound the expansion-attack surface (threat-model D / AC-3): a 16 MiB byte cap on the profile and on each supplied catalog (Go-side before the wire + bridge-side), a 10,000 resolved-control cap, a ≤16 supplied-catalog cap, and import-chain depth bounded to a single profile→catalog level by construction (a supplied document that is itself a profile fails the catalog parse). A Go `internal/oscal/profileimport` caller persists the resolved baseline **transactionally** (a resolution/validation failure commits nothing — AC-5 / P0-511-3) into the slice-492 tables, distinguished by a new `imported_catalogs.kind` discriminator (`'catalog'` | `'profile'`) + a `profile_title` provenance column (migration `20260608000000_oscal_imported_profiles.sql`, purely additive), `tenant`-scoped + `grc_engineer`-gated (P0-511-5), with an append-only audit row (`profile_imported` / `profile_import_rejected` — AC-7). The bundled SCF spine (`scf_anchors`) is never touched (P0-511-4) and SCF reconciliation reuses slice-492's deterministic crosswalk. CLI `atlas-oscal import-profile <profile-file> --catalog <file>... [--json]` (AC-8). The Go-side reconciliation logic is testable WITHOUT the bridge (pure-Go `helpers_test.go`); the end-to-end resolve + the no-external-fetch guard + cap enforcement are proven at both the bridge unit tier (pytest, monkeypatched socket asserts no network) and the Go integration tier (real Postgres + real bridge, self-skipping when the compliance-trestle bridge subprocess is unavailable — slice-030 D2). Component-definition import remains a separate follow-on (slice 512). **Type: JUDGMENT** — profile-resolution semantics (delegated to compliance-trestle vs done in Go), the sandboxed-no-deref design, the SCF reconciliation, and the persistence-shape call (`kind` discriminator vs a sibling table) recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/511-oscal-profile-import-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 492. Slice doc: `docs/issues/511-oscal-profile-import.md`.

- **feat(control): slice 496 — control-bundle test runner (fixture evidence → expected pass/fail).** Canvas §4.4 lists "Tests — fixture evidence + expected pass/fail" as a first-class part of every control bundle and slice 009 promised a tests directory, but there was no runner: a control author writing a Rego/SQL/JSON-path evidence query could not verify it short of uploading the bundle and eyeballing live state. This slice ships the runner — the control-as-code analogue of "tests before code". **(1) Fixture format (AC-1, JUDGMENT call):** a `tests/` directory beside `control.yaml` holds `*.yaml` files of test cases; each case declares a `name`, fixture evidence `records` (`result` + optional `observed_at` + optional `payload`), and an `expected_state` (pass|fail|na|inconclusive). `observed_at` defaults to the case's evaluation instant so a simple case is in-window without freshness reasoning; unknown YAML keys are a loud parse error. **(2) Runner reuses the LIVE engine (AC-2/AC-4, anti-criterion P0-496-1):** a new DB-free seam `eval.EvaluateFixture` runs the SAME freshness-window filter + per-language dispatch (rego|sql|jsonpath) + result-precedence rollup + no_evidence override that the live `eval.computeRow` uses — there is no parallel "test interpreter" that could drift, so a bundle that passes its tests behaves identically when evaluated for real. Rego + JSON-path evaluate fully in-process; SQL runs through the same read-only-subtransaction sandbox when a tx is supplied. **(3) No live ledger (invariant #2 / P0-496-2 / AC-9):** fixtures are presented in-memory; the runner performs no writes and touches no tenant evidence — the Rego/JSON-path path needs no Postgres at all, and a SQL query with no DB connection reports a typed `ErrFixtureSQLNeedsDB` rather than a false pass (fail-loud). **(4) CLI (AC-5/P0-496-6):** `atlas-cli controls test <bundle-dir>` reports per-case PASS/FAIL/ERROR (text + `--json`) and **exits non-zero on any failure or error** so it slots into CI for community-contributed bundles; a bundle with no tests is a stderr warning, not a failure. **(5) Threat model (AC-8/D):** the per-query timeout the live evaluator enforces applies; a cancelled context or broken query surfaces as a test error, never a hang. **Faithful-semantics note (recorded, not "fixed"):** records that EXIST but aged out of the freshness window are `stale` (not `no_evidence`), so the no_evidence override does NOT fire — the query runs over an empty in-window set and returns its default; the runner reproduces this exactly. New package `internal/control/bundletest` (coverage floor 85, Leg-A integration-shard enrolment for the SQL end-to-end test). Does NOT add a control-authoring UI, fixture record-and-replay, or a hard upload-time test gate (P0-496-5; all follow-ons). **Type: JUDGMENT** — fixture-format shape, in-memory evidence-set stubbing, and result rollup recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/496-control-bundle-test-runner-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/496-control-bundle-test-runner.md`.

- **feat(connectors): slice 491 — Rippling + BambooHR HRIS joiner/mover/leaver connectors.** Two read-only HRIS connectors (`connectors/rippling/`, `connectors/bamboohr/`) bring the **authoritative worker roster + joiner/mover/leaver lifecycle** into the evidence ledger — the spine of the access-review (slice 374) + deprovisioning controls (SOC 2 CC6.1/CC6.2/CC6.3). Both follow the locked connector pattern (register-per-run, stable `actor_id`, hour-truncated `observed_at`, scope minimums, vendor-native read-only auth) and emit ONE shared evidence kind, `hris.worker_lifecycle.v1`, built once in a shared `connectors/hris/{worker,workerrecord,idem}` package (the slice-490 MDM two-connector precedent) with a `source_hris` discriminator. **The load-bearing P0 (the HRIS holds the most sensitive PII the platform will ever touch):** the connectors collect **worker-lifecycle facts ONLY** — worker id, employment status, start/end dates, title, department, the opaque manager assignment id, and work email (the only contact field, for the access-review join) — and **structurally exclude** SSN / national id, compensation / salary, home address, bank / payment, benefits / health, performance, date of birth, personal phone, and protected-class data at three layers: (1) the API request asks for only the minimal field set (Rippling `fields=` selector; BambooHR a custom report scoped to the lifecycle fields, never the full-PII directory/employee endpoints), (2) the raw + normalized structs have no field for any excluded value so a leak is a compile error, and (3) tests assert no excluded-PII key/substring reaches an emitted payload AND the request never asks for a banned field (AC-10), plus the credential-never-logged guard (AC-11). Auth is a read-only minimal-field HRIS credential, redacted on every format path; the `permissions` subcommand prints the documented minimum with a no-full-PII / no-write warning. Pull-only profile with an honest interval (NOT "continuous monitoring"); push-only platform wire (invariant #3); no migration (evidence flows through the existing ledger). Schema `hris.worker_lifecycle.v1` lands in the schema-registry tree with `x-default-scf-anchors = [IAC-22, IAC-09, HRS-04]` (mirroring `okta.user_lifecycle.v1`) and is wired into `DefaultSeed`. **Type: JUDGMENT** — the shared-vs-per-connector kind, the included-vs-excluded field set (the PII boundary), the worker-identity choice, the anchors, the scope minimum, and per-HRIS least-privilege scope recorded in `docs/audit-log/491-hris-connectors-decisions.md`. Follow-ons: manager-hierarchy evidence (slice 571), event-driven termination-webhook profile (slice 573). Slice doc: `docs/issues/491-hris-connectors-rippling-bamboohr.md`.

- **feat(catalog): slice 516 — full HIPAA Security Rule coverage incl. §164.314 + §164.316 (catalog-only; no new loader code).** Slice 481 shipped a curated high-signal subset of **31** HIPAA Security Rule standards + implementation specifications spanning the three safeguard categories; this slice completes coverage of the remaining **36** — the rest of Administrative (§164.308), Physical (§164.310), and Technical (§164.312) safeguards PLUS the two sections 481 deferred, **Organizational Requirements (§164.314)** and **Policies/Procedures/Documentation (§164.316)** — so a HIPAA covered entity or business associate can run a **full Security Rule self-assessment** out of security-atlas, not only the high-signal subset. Like 481 (and mirroring slice 514's CSF extension), it is **pure data + tests + a decisions log** with **zero loader changes** — the slice-438 framework-agnostic importer (`internal/api/soc2import`) already imports the HIPAA crosswalk by `(framework_slug, framework_version)`. **(1) Data (AC-1):** `data/crosswalks/hipaa-security-rule.yaml` is extended to **all 67** standards + implementation specifications across the five Subpart C sections — Administrative (30), Physical (12), Technical (12), Organizational (8), Documentation (5) — each mapped to **one** SCF anchor via an explicit STRM-typed edge (`relationship_type` + `strength` on every row — 16 `equal`, 24 `subset_of`, 27 `intersects_with`; strengths [0.55, 0.9]). **Every one of the 30 distinct anchors referenced resolves against the seeded 53-anchor SCF sample catalog** (`migrations/fixtures/scf-sample.json` — a non-existent anchor rolls the whole import back, proven by `TestHIPAAImport_RejectsEdgeToNonexistentAnchor`), and every edge is requirement → SCF anchor, never requirement → requirement (invariant #7); the new §164.314 organizational rows resolve to Third-Party Management anchors, NOT HIPAA-to-HIPAA edges. **(2) D7 re-map (AC-2):** the slice-481 ≤0.65 low-confidence rows are re-mapped/re-confirmed — §164.308(a)(8) Evaluation is lifted from CPL-03 @ 0.65 to **CPL-01 (Compliance Management) @ 0.75**, a closer covering anchor within the palette; §164.312(c)(1) Integrity and §164.310(b) Workstation Use are re-confirmed at their slice-481 anchors with the residual gap documented because no finer covering anchor exists in the 53-anchor sample fixture (decisions-log D5 — the honest, palette-bound call). **(3) Five-framework graph proof preserved (AC-4):** the integration suite's invariant-#1 proof still holds — the single shared SCF anchor `IAC-01` satisfies HIPAA §164.312(a)(1) alongside SOC 2 `CC6.1` + ISO `A.5.15` + PCI `8.2.1` + CSF `PR.AA-01` through the one anchor row (no per-framework duplication — invariant #1). **(4) Idempotent import (AC-3):** re-importing the extended file is a no-op (67 requirements + 67 edges unchanged). **(5) Regulatory-weight confidentiality (AC-3/AC-5):** `TestHIPAAImport_AnchorsReadCarriesNoTenantScopedData` (column-exhaustive allow-list) stays green and unmodified — no tenant-scoped column added to the catalog `/anchors` read; ePHI sensitivity makes this the load-bearing assertion and it covers the new §164.314/§164.316 rows by construction. **Tests:** the slice-481 unit count assertion (`[25,35]`) is retargeted to full coverage, `TestLoad_HIPAASpansAllThreeSafeguardCategories` is renamed to `TestLoad_HIPAASpansAllSecurityRuleSections` (now allowing §164.314 + §164.316), and a new `TestLoad_HIPAAFullCoverage` asserts the exact 67-row count + per-section distribution (AC-1); the integration suite imports all 67 rows, proves idempotency + the five-framework shared anchor + rollback, and the existing SOC 2 / ISO / PCI / CSF suites pass **unmodified** (AC-4). No migration (`framework_requirements` + `fw_to_scf_edges` exist since slices 007/438); no new floored package or integration-shard entry (extends the already-enrolled `internal/api/soc2import`). Does **not** add a HIPAA-specific loader, does **not** ship the covered-entity workflow / BAA tracking / required-vs-addressable decision flow / breach risk-assessment / HIPAA FrameworkScope ePHI example (all the deferred phase-3 slice 517 — canvas §10.3), and does **not** copy verbatim regulatory text beyond identifiers/short titles — HIPAA is a US-government public-domain work but the `community_draft` source-attribution discipline still applies. **Type: JUDGMENT** — STRM type + strength per new requirement, the anchor-palette constraint, the D7 re-map, and the 21 residual ≤0.65 low-confidence flags recorded with confidence + detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/516-hipaa-full-coverage-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 481. Slice doc: `docs/issues/516-hipaa-security-rule-full-coverage.md`.
- **feat(notify): slice 566 — per-kind email opt-out for `audit_note.reply` + `evidence.staleness`.** Slice 542 layered the slice-108 per-event `email` channel on top of the slice-445 master email opt-in, but the notification-`type` taxonomy and the slice-108 `event` taxonomy are NOT 1:1 (542 D3): two kinds the slice-445 digest renders — `audit_note.reply` (audit-note replies) and `evidence.staleness` (stale-evidence digests) — had **no slice-108 event row at all**, so they were UNMAPPED → included-when-master-on with **no per-kind opt-out surface**. An opted-in user could mute control-drift / policy-ack / risk-review / audit-period emails individually but could **not** mute audit-note-reply or stale-evidence emails short of turning the master switch off.
