This slice gives those two kinds a per-kind opt-out by extending the slice-108 event taxonomy. **(1) Whitelist-move-together (the slice-108 discipline):** the two event keys `audit_note_reply` + `evidence_staleness` are added in BOTH places that must move atomically — the `user_notification_preferences_event_check` CHECK constraint (new migration `20260607040000_userprefs_unmapped_kinds.sql`, sorting after `20260607030000_backup_runs.sql`, with a reversible `.down.sql`) AND the Go whitelist `internal/auth/userprefs.Events`; a value in one but not the other is a latent 500. **(2) Mapping (542 D3 forward note honored):** the `kindToEvent` map in `internal/notify/email/kindfilter.go` gains `audit_note.reply` → `audit_note_reply` and `evidence.staleness` → `evidence_staleness` (dot→underscore normalization, same shape as the existing `control.drift` → `control_drift`), so the digest filter now honors an explicit per-kind opt-out for them. **(3) UI:** the settings-page notification matrix (`web/app/(authed)/settings/page.tsx`) gains the two toggle rows, mirroring the four existing per-event rows; the Playwright settings spec asserts both new rows render. **Backward-compatible default preserved (P0-566-1):** the migration inserts NO rows and the default-on-missing-row semantics (542 D2 / 108 D3) are unchanged — an opted-in user keeps receiving both kinds until they set an explicit per-kind `email=false`; no silent suppression. Recipient resolution, tenant scoping, and the minimum-disclosure body shape are untouched (inherits P0-542-2 / P0-542-3). **Type: JUDGMENT** — the event-key spelling + the taxonomy-extension call recorded with detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/566-per-kind-email-prefs-unmapped-kinds-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 542. Slice doc: `docs/issues/566-per-kind-email-prefs-for-unmapped-notification-kinds.md`.
- **feat(catalog): slice 514 — full NIST CSF 2.0 Subcategory coverage (catalog-only; no new loader code).** Slice 480 shipped a curated high-signal subset of **35** CSF 2.0 Subcategories spanning all six Functions; this slice completes coverage of the remaining **71** so a SaaS operator can run a **full CSF 2.0 self-assessment** out of security-atlas, not only the high-signal subset. Like 480, it is **pure data + tests + a decisions log** with **zero loader changes** (P0-514-1) — the slice-438 framework-agnostic importer (`internal/api/soc2import`) already imports the CSF crosswalk by `(framework_slug, framework_version)`. **(1) Data (AC-1):** `data/crosswalks/nist-csf-2.0.yaml` is extended to **all 106** NIST CSF 2.0 (NIST CSWP 29) Subcategories across the six Functions and 22 Categories — GOVERN (31), IDENTIFY (21), PROTECT (22), DETECT (11), RESPOND (13), RECOVER (8) — each mapped to **one** SCF anchor via an explicit STRM-typed edge (`relationship_type` + `strength` on every row — 13 `equal`, 52 `subset_of`, 41 `intersects_with`; strengths [0.55, 0.9]). **Every one of the 106 anchors referenced resolves against the seeded 53-anchor SCF sample catalog** (`migrations/fixtures/scf-sample.json`, P0-514-4 — a non-existent anchor rolls the whole import back, proven by `TestCSFImport_RejectsEdgeToNonexistentAnchor`), and every edge is requirement → SCF anchor, never requirement → requirement (invariant #7, P0-514-2). **(2) D7 re-map (AC-2):** the five slice-480 low-confidence rows (GV.OV-01, RC.CO-03, RS.AN-03, RC.RP-04, DE.AE-02) are re-confirmed against the catalog; because the **test fixture** still carries the 53-anchor sample (not the operator's full SCF catalog), each is mapped to the closest _covering_ anchor within the palette and its residual gap documented rather than referencing a finer anchor the import cannot resolve — the honest, anchor-palette-bound call (decisions-log D5). **(3) Four-framework graph proof preserved (AC-4):** the integration suite's invariant-#1 proof still holds — the single shared SCF anchor `IAC-01` now satisfies CSF `PR.AA-01` **and** the new `PR.AA-04` alongside SOC 2 `CC6.1` + ISO `A.5.15` + PCI `8.2.1` through the one anchor row (no per-framework duplication — invariant #1, P0-514-3), and the GOVERN no-analog proof scales to all **31** GOVERN edges traversing SCF anchors. **(4) Idempotent import (AC-3):** re-importing the extended file is a no-op (106 requirements + 106 edges unchanged). **Tests:** the slice-480 unit count assertion (`[30,40]`) is retargeted to full coverage and a new `TestLoad_CSFFullSubcategoryCoverage` asserts the exact 106-Subcategory count + per-Function distribution (AC-5); the integration suite imports all 106 rows, proves idempotency + the four-framework shared anchor + the GOVERN no-analog property, and the existing SOC 2 / ISO / PCI / HIPAA suites pass **unmodified** (AC-4). No migration (`framework_requirements` + `fw_to_scf_edges` exist since slices 007/438); no new floored package or integration-shard entry (extends the already-enrolled `internal/api/soc2import`). Does **not** add a CSF-specific loader (P0-514-1), does **not** ship the CSF Profile/Tier maturity assessment (out of catalog scope — slice 515), and does **not** copy verbatim CSF text — CSF 2.0 is a US-government public-domain work but the `community_draft` source-attribution discipline still applies (P0-514-5). **Type: JUDGMENT** — STRM type + strength per new Subcategory, the anchor-palette constraint, and the 28 residual ≤0.65 low-confidence flags recorded with confidence + detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/514-nist-csf-full-coverage-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 480. Slice doc: `docs/issues/514-nist-csf-2-0-full-subcategory-coverage.md`.

- **feat(notify): slice 542 — per-notification-kind email preferences.** Slice 445 shipped a MASTER email opt-in (one toggle: email delivery on/off for the whole user) and deliberately deferred per-kind filtering (445 D7); the slice-108 `user_notification_preferences` table already carried a per-event `email` channel column that 445 never consulted, so an opted-in user received a digest covering ALL their unread kinds with no way to mute a single noisy kind. **This slice makes the 445 digest build CONSULT that existing column** — no new migration. The composition is **master AND per-kind** (D1): the master switch (default OFF) stays the OUTER gate, evaluated first; the per-event `email` pref is the INNER filter, evaluated only after the master gate passes — so a per-kind row can only ever NARROW delivery for an already-opted-in user, never widen it (a master-OR composition would let slice-108's default-`true` per-kind rows silently override the master-off default, regressing P0-445-7). **Default-on-missing-row (D2 — the backward-compatibility call):** an opted-in user with no per-kind row keeps receiving every kind exactly as before; a kind is suppressed only by an EXPLICIT `email=false` row for its mapped event, inheriting slice-108's own default-on policy (108 D3) so the digest and `/v1/me/preferences` agree on what "no row" means. **The type→event mapping is NOT 1:1 (D3):** `control.drift` (dot) maps to slice-108 event `control_drift` (underscore); `audit_note.reply` + `evidence.staleness` have no slice-108 event row and default to included-when-master-on (a future slice adding their event rows MUST add the mapping — documented inline). **Filter location + shape (D4):** the filter is a pure-Go map narrowing applied to the in-memory `counts` map AFTER the RLS-scoped read and AFTER the master gate, BEFORE the empty-digest skip — recipient resolution and tenant scoping are structurally untouched (P0-542-2, threat-model I: the filter can remove a kind's count but never redirect delivery), the total is recomputed so an all-muted digest skips cleanly with no empty email, and the minimum-disclosure body shape is unchanged (P0-542-3). Pure logic in new `internal/notify/email/kindfilter.go` (no DB import; fast table-tests in `kindfilter_test.go` covering master-on+kind-on→send / master-on+kind-off→mute / master-on+no-row→default-on / unmapped→included); two new integration tests (`TestDeliverDigest_PerKindMute_RemovesCountKeepsDelivery` proves a muted kind's count is removed but the same account email still receives the surviving kinds; `TestDeliverDigest_AllKindsMuted_Skips` proves all-muted → skip). **Type: JUDGMENT** — filter composition semantics + default-on/off-per-kind + the type→event mapping recorded with rationale + detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/542-per-notification-kind-email-prefs-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/542-per-notification-kind-email-preferences.md`.
- **feat(infra): slice 510 — automated backup + scheduled restore-verification (operationalizing the slice-432 runbook).** Slice 432 documented the _manual_ backup/restore procedure and explicitly scoped automation out ("that would be a code slice" — P0-432-4); slice 373's BCP/DR RTO/RPO tiers cannot be met by a runbook a solo operator might forget to run. **This is that code slice.** A new `internal/backup` package + two **in-process tick-loop schedulers** (mirroring the exception-expiry / metrics schedulers — **no external cron** for the single-VM self-host target — D1) turn the documented procedure into a running feature, with **zero new docker-compose services** so the four self-host-bundle CI legs (bundled / external / proxy / migrate) keep their exact topology. **What runs each cycle (defaults: daily):** (1) **AC-1 scheduled backup** — a **pure-Go logical dump** (`pg_dump` is deliberately NOT shelled out: the atlas runtime is `distroless/static` — no shell, no psql, no pg*dump — D2) writes a self-contained schema-+data SQL stream; (2) **AC-2 pluggable backup-target** — one `Target` interface (`Put/Get/List/Delete`), a `LocalTarget` (default volume) + an `S3Target` (off-host durability, integration-tested against MinIO), mirroring the evidence object-storage abstraction (D3); (3) **AC-3 retention/rotation** — operator-configurable keep-N-daily + M-weekly (default 7+4), old backups rotate out so storage never grows unbounded (P0-510-3); (4) **AC-4 the load-bearing half — scheduled restore-verification** — restores the latest backup into an **ephemeral throwaway database**, runs a smoke check (schema lands, a sentinel query returns), then **DROPs the ephemeral DB in a `defer` on every path including failure** (P0-510-2: no standing second copy — an integration test asserts zero `atlas_restore_verify*%`databases remain); (5) **AC-5 integrity** — every backup carries a **sha256** recomputed-and-checked **before** any replay, so a corrupted artifact **fails verification loudly** and is never restored (integration test tampers an artifact → hash-mismatch fail); (6) **AC-6 status + alerting** — every run writes an **append-only`backup_runs`status record**, and a FAILURE raises an **in-app notification composing with the slice-445 email channel**. **The load-bearing security boundary (AC-7 / P0-510-1, enforced at the DB grant layer — not application code):** a backup is a **full cross-tenant copy** that deliberately crosses the RLS boundary (invariant #6), so it is a **deployment-privileged operation** — the backup scheduler runs as the BYPASSRLS migrator role, and the new **deployment-scope`backup_runs`table is granted to`atlas_migrate`ONLY, never`atlas_app`** (the RLS-enforced tenant-reachable role). A tenant-facing handler therefore *cannot* read or trigger a backup: its role has zero privilege on the table — proven by an integration test asserting `permission denied`for`atlas_app`on both SELECT and INSERT. The crown-jewel dump is contained at the deployment tier, integrity-checked before any restore, and the restore lands only in a disposable DB. **Scope discipline (out):** PITR/WAL archiving (logical-dump is the v1 RPO mechanism), Helm/K8s backup operators, cross-region replication, and rewriting the slice-432 runbook / slice-373 BCP/DR plan (this operationalizes + cross-links only — P0-510-4). Self-host config + the restore-verification model documented in`docs/SELF_HOSTING.md`(cross-linking 432 + 373); migration`20260607030000_backup_runs.sql`(+`.down.sql`); new `internal/backup`coverage floor (73) + Leg-A integration-shard enrolment (the package creates/drops databases + dumps the whole DB → must run serially). **Type: JUDGMENT** — the backup-target abstraction, the pure-Go-dump-vs-pg_dump choice, the scheduling + retention defaults, and the ephemeral-DB mechanism recorded with confidence + detection-tier in`docs/audit-log/510-automated-backup-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/510-automated-backup-restore.md`.

- **feat(notify): slice 445 — email/SMTP notification delivery channel (delivery substrate).** A solo security leader lives in their inbox, not the app — so the in-app notification store (slice 029) needed a way to reach email. This slice ships a **delivery sink** (NOT a notification producer — P0-445-5) over a **thin email-provider abstraction** (one `Provider` interface + one stdlib `net/smtp` implementation — no multi-provider plugin system) configured entirely via `ATLAS_SMTP_*` environment variables for the single-VM self-host target (AC-1). An **opt-in per-user email channel** (`internal/notify/email`) reads the opted-in target user's unread notifications **under that notification's own tenant context** (RLS FORCE) and delivers **one digest email** — summary counts grouped by type + a deep-link back into the authenticated app, **minimum-disclosure** (no raw evidence payloads, S3 URLs, or confidential control text — AC-4/AC-7/P0-445-4). Delivery goes **strictly to the user's account email** resolved server-side (no user-controlled recipient field — P0-445-1/AC-3). Two new RLS four-policy tables (`email_channel_optin`, `email_delivery_log`) back the **default-OPTED-OUT** master toggle (P0-445-7) and **idempotent** delivery — a UNIQUE `(tenant_id, recipient_user_id, digest_key)` claim-before-send means a given per-day digest is never double-sent and acts as the 24h per-user rate limit (AC-5); delivery outcome (sent/failed) + attempt count are recorded with an SMTP send timeout and re-attempt-on-next-tick backoff (no hot retry — AC-8). **Load-bearing security guards:** all header fields (subject/recipient) are **CRLF-stripped unconditionally** so notification-derived content can never inject a header or extra recipient (open-relay guard — AC-6/AC-12/P0-445-2), all HTML-body interpolation is `html.EscapeString`-d (AC-14), the type label comes from a closed map (never echoes a raw row value), and SMTP credentials are env-only and never logged (threat-model S). **Cross-tenant email is proven absent** by an integration test that runs each tenant's delivery under its own GUC and asserts Tenant A's notification never reaches Tenant B's user (AC-13/P0-445-3). A new `GET/PUT /v1/me/email-channel` endpoint + BFF route + a minimum-surface Settings → Notifications "Email delivery" toggle expose the opt-in (AC-9). Self-host SMTP env config documented in `deploy/docker/.env.example` (AC-10). Email only — no SMS/Slack/webhook (P0-445-6; filed as follow-ons 541-545). **Type: JUDGMENT** — provider-abstraction shape, minimum-disclosure body, and rate-limit default recorded in `docs/audit-log/445-email-channel-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/445-email-smtp-notification-channel.md`.

- **feat(connectors): slice 489 — PagerDuty connector (incident-response evidence): on-call coverage + bounded-window incident summaries.** A new first-class peer connector — `connectors/pagerduty/` — answers the recurring SOC 2 CC7.3 / CC7.4 / CC7.5 auditor demand ("show your on-call schedule and your incident history") and the slice-372 IR plan, today a manual upload. It follows the locked slice-004 / 486 / 487 / 488 connector pattern verbatim (register-per-run, stable `actor_id`, hour-truncated `observed_at`, scope minimums, vendor-native read-only auth) and emits **two evidence kinds**: `pagerduty.oncall_coverage.v1` (escalation policy, its tiers, and the on-call IDENTITY — opaque id + display name — needed to prove coverage; read-only PagerDuty REST `GET /escalation_policies`) and `pagerduty.incident_summary.v1` (incident id / number / urgency / status / service / created+resolved timestamps over a bounded look-back window; read-only `GET /incidents?since=&until=`). Each record is pushed to the platform's single `IngestEvidence` / `Push` API with a sha256 content-hash and stable optional fields — **no platform-side wire change** (invariant #3, P0-489-1); the connector registers `profiles_supported=[pull]` and names its interval honestly (operator-scheduled, recommended 24h — **not** "continuous monitoring", P0-489-6). **The load-bearing over-collection guard (P0-489-3 / threat-model I):** the connector collects coverage facts + incident-SUMMARY metadata only — on-call identity (id + display name) and incident id/urgency/status/service/timestamps — and **never** a responder's personal phone number or personal email, the incident's free-text title/body/notes (which can embed customer data), or postmortem text. The boundary is enforced **by construction** (the `oncall.RawTarget` type has no phone/email field and the connector never fetches `/users`; the `incidents.RawIncident` type has no title/body/notes field; the clients decode only the listed fields, so unwanted PII / free-text never enters memory) plus tests: a record-shape test asserts no PII-shaped or free-text substring reaches an emitted payload (AC-10), and another asserts the PagerDuty token is never logged (AC-11). The read-only REST API token is read only from the environment (never a CLI flag), redacted on every credential format path, and never placed into a record (P0-489-4); the connector requires a **read-only least-privilege token only**, surfaced by the `permissions` subcommand and warned against write/admin (P0-489-2). It is **OSS, in-tree, read-only API** (P0-489-5) and faked in tests (no live PagerDuty in CI — a thin read-only HTTP client `pdhttp`, no heavy vendor SDK; the in-package bufconn integration test exercises the full collect → build → `Push` → receipt round-trip for both kinds and same-hour idempotency). Two evidence-kind JSON Schemas land in the schema-registry tree with `x-default-scf-anchors` set (`IRO-04`/`IRO-07` for coverage, `IRO-02`/`IRO-09` for incidents — flagged for maintainer recheck per OQ #9) and are registered in `DefaultSeed`; no migration. The incident look-back window defaults to 90 days (`--lookback-days`, bounded — threat-model D). Postmortem/retrospective evidence, responder-performance metrics, and an event-driven webhook profile are filed as follow-on slices 538–540 (P0-489-7). **Type: JUDGMENT** — the two evidence-kind shapes, the on-call-identity-vs-PII boundary, the look-back window, the scope minimums, and the `x-default-scf-anchors` recorded with confidence + detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/489-pagerduty-connector-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/489-pagerduty-connector.md`.
- **feat(connectors): slice 490 — Jamf + Intune MDM connectors (managed-device endpoint posture).** Two new first-class peer connectors — `connectors/jamf/` and `connectors/intune/` — answer the recurring SOC 2 CC6.7 / CC6.8 and ISO A.8 auditor demand ("prove laptops are disk-encrypted, have a screen-lock policy, and are managed"), today a manual MDM export. Both follow the locked slice-004 / 486 / 487 / 488 / 489 connector pattern verbatim (register-per-run, stable `actor_id`, hour-truncated `observed_at`, scope minimums, vendor-native read-only auth) and emit **one shared evidence kind** — `endpoint.device_posture.v1` — because the two MDMs' device-posture field set is identical at the posture-summary altitude (a `source_mdm` discriminator preserves provenance; the shape is normalized once in `connectors/mdm/devposture` and built once in `connectors/mdm/devrecord`). **Jamf** collects managed-Mac posture (FileVault disk-encryption, screen-lock/passcode compliance, OS version, managed/supervised/enrollment state, the device→owner assignment identity) via the read-only Jamf Pro API (`GET /api/v1/computers-inventory`, posture-relevant inventory sections only). **Intune** collects managed-device compliance posture (BitLocker disk-encryption, `complianceState`, OS version, management/enrollment state, the device→owner assignment identity) via the read-only Microsoft Graph device-management API (`GET /deviceManagement/managedDevices`, explicit posture `$select`). Each record is pushed to the platform's single `IngestEvidence` / `Push` API with a sha256 content-hash and stable optional fields — **no platform-side wire change** (invariant #3, P0-490-1); each registers `profiles_supported=[pull]` and names its interval honestly (operator-scheduled, recommended 24h — **not** "continuous monitoring", P0-490-6). **The load-bearing over-collection guard (P0-490-3 / threat-model I):** both connectors collect posture/compliance **summary + the device→owner ASSIGNMENT identity only** (opaque assigned-user id + optional display name) — and **never** device geolocation, the installed-app inventory (Jamf `APPLICATIONS` / Intune `detectedApps`), device contents, browsing data, or owner personal contact detail (phone / personal email / address). The guard is enforced **by construction** at three layers: the `devposture.RawDevice` + per-vendor raw types have no field for geolocation / apps / contact detail (a leak is a compile error); the vendor clients request ONLY the posture-relevant data at the API boundary (Jamf never requests `APPLICATIONS` or the GPS `location` section; Intune's `$select` never includes `detectedApps` / `phoneNumber` / `emailAddress`); and the record builder allow-lists payload keys. Tests: `TestEmittedRecords_NoGeoOrAppsOrPII` (both connectors) asserts only allow-listed keys + no over-collection substring reach a payload (AC-10), the client tests assert the request itself never asks for the excluded sections/props, and `TestCredential_NeverLogged` asserts the MDM credential is never logged (AC-11). **The load-bearing remote-wipe guard (P0-490-2 / threat-model E):** each connector requires a **read-only device-read credential only** — Jamf an API client bound to a read-only API role (Read Computers / Read Mobile Devices); Intune an Entra app granted only `DeviceManagementManagedDevices.Read.All` — surfaced by each `permissions` subcommand with an explicit warning that a write/management MDM credential is a **remote-wipe** risk and must never be used (the auth-package tests hold `RequiredRole`/`RequiredPermission` to read-only). Source credentials are read only from the environment (never a CLI flag), redacted on every credential format path, and never placed into a record (P0-490-4). Both are **OSS, in-tree, API-based** — the on-device agent is the customer's own MDM, not a security-atlas agent (P0-490-5, consistent with the osquery/Fleet/read-only-API posture) — and faked in tests (no live Jamf/Intune in CI — thin read-only HTTP clients handling the OAuth client_credentials token exchange + the bounded read; per-connector in-package bufconn integration tests exercise the full collect → build → `Push` → receipt round-trip + same-hour idempotency). The shared `endpoint.device_posture.v1` JSON Schema lands in the schema-registry tree with `x-default-scf-anchors=[END-04, CFG-02]` (Endpoint Security + Secure Baseline Configurations — consistent with `osquery.host_posture.v1`, flagged for maintainer recheck per OQ #9) and is registered in `DefaultSeed`; **no migration** (evidence flows through the existing ledger). v0 reads the first bounded page per MDM (bounded page size + run timeout — threat-model D). Software-inventory evidence, configuration-profile detail evidence, and an event-driven webhook profile are filed as follow-on slices 555–557 (P0-490-7). **Type: JUDGMENT** — the shared-vs-per-connector evidence-kind decision, the posture field set, the device→owner assignment-identity boundary, the `x-default-scf-anchors`, the scope minimums, and the per-MDM read-only auth scope recorded with confidence + detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/490-mdm-connectors-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/490-mdm-connectors-jamf-intune.md`.
- **feat(catalog): slice 482 — per-requirement coverage-strength rollup + confidence-band visualization (the canvas §3.2 headline promise).** Canvas §3.2 promises the platform can compute _coverage strength_ per requirement through the STRM-edge `strength` field ("if your evidence covers SCF:IAC-22 with strength 1.0, and ISO27001:A.9.4.2 → SCF:IAC-22 with strength 0.8, the ISO requirement is covered at 0.8, and the UI surfaces the gap"). The raw ingredients existed — slice 438's per-edge `strength`, slice 012's control-state evaluation, slice 016's freshness, and the `GET /v1/requirements/{id}/coverage` endpoint that already returns each anchor's `strength` — but the rollup and its visualization did not; slice 438 (P0-438-6) and slice 447 explicitly deferred coverage-strength visualization to this follow-on (roadmap §10.2). **(1) Backend read-model (AC-1/AC-2/AC-3):** `GET /v1/requirements/{id}/coverage` gains two **additive** fields — `coverage_strength` (numeric 0.0–1.0) + `confidence_band` (string) — computed **server-side** (no client-supplied strength — P0-482-4) from each anchor's edge `strength` combined with the tenant's evaluated coverage state. The formula (the JUDGMENT call) is **best-satisfying-path**: `coverage_strength = MAX over anchors of (edge_strength × anchor_coverage)`, where `anchor_coverage` is the best 30-day effectiveness pass rate over the tenant's RLS-scoped controls on that anchor, gated by FrameworkScope — the same per-control number slice 256 ships on `/v1/controls/{id}/coverage`, aggregated requirement-side. The single-anchor case reduces **exactly** to the canvas worked example (0.8 × 1.0 = 0.8 → "strong"). The existing `requirement` / `anchors` / `controls` payload fields are **unchanged** (additive only — P0-482-5); the per-anchor `strength` stays (AC-3). **(2) The load-bearing RLS isolation (threat-model I / P0-482-3 / invariant #6):** because the rollup folds in the tenant's evaluated state, `coverage_strength` is **tenant-confidential derived data**, not global catalog data — it is computed **only** over the RLS-scoped `controls` rows (the same scope the existing `controls[]` array uses, no `WHERE tenant_id` clause; RLS does the filtering at the DB layer). A requirement the tenant has no controls for resolves to the **uncovered** band (empty, never a foreign tenant's value); a control whose framework is out of FrameworkScope, or that has no effectiveness data, contributes nothing (the slice-256 null contract, lifted to the rollup). An integration test (AC-7) seeds a **strong** (0.8) control for tenant A and proves tenant B sees `0` / `uncovered` for the same requirement — never tenant A's score. **(3) Confidence bands (the JUDGMENT call):** four bands with cut points pattern-matched to the canvas example — `strong` [0.80, 1.0] (0.8 is the floor — the canvas value reads as strongly, not perfectly, covered, with the 0.2 gap shown alongside), `partial` [0.50, 0.80), `weak` (0, 0.50), and `uncovered` (no in-scope evaluated path). A genuine 0.0 score _with_ an in-scope control (0% pass rate) classifies as **weak**, not **uncovered** — "covered but failing" is a real state distinct from "no coverage" (slice 256 P0-256-1, carried up). **(4) Freshness weighting (decisions-log call):** **not** folded in for v1 — the 30-day effectiveness window is already freshness-bounded, so re-applying slice-016 freshness would double-count; a distinct freshness _penalty_ is a deliberate v2 enrichment on the revisit list. **(5) UI (AC-4/AC-5):** the control detail view's coverage table (`web/components/control/coverage-table.tsx`) gains a **Confidence** column — one band badge per requirement row derived from the per-row coverage with thresholds mirroring the backend (`web/components/control/confidence-band.ts`, a styled badge — no new charting dependency), carrying `data-band` for value-driven assertions; the footer documents the thresholds. A new requirements-coverage **BFF route** (`web/app/api/requirements/[id]/coverage/route.ts`, httpOnly-bearer pass-through, no client-supplied input) + lib type (`web/lib/api/requirement-coverage.ts`) carry the additive fields for a future requirement-detail page. **Scope discipline (P0s):** read + visualize **only** — does NOT ship the crosswalk-review/conflict editing UI (P0-482-1, separate slice), does NOT change how `strength` is stored or how the 438 loader writes edges (P0-482-2), and does NOT add a cross-framework comparison matrix (P0-482-6) — both filed as follow-on docs slices 536 + 537. **Tests:** fast pure-Go unit branches for the formula + band thresholds (`helpers_test.go`, no DB — AC-8, slice-353 Q-2) run on every CI run; integration tests (AC-6 mixed-strength edges + mixed evidence → expected band; AC-7 the RLS cross-tenant isolation proof) run on the already-enrolled `internal/api/ucfcoverage` shard; vitest covers the band classifier + the BFF mapping; a Playwright assertion (commented per the slice-082 seed-harness convention) covers the band rendering. New web per-file coverage floors added per the slice-347 ratchet; raises the `internal/api/ucfcoverage` Go floor's headroom (no migration — reads existing edges + state). **Type: JUDGMENT** — the rollup formula, the no-freshness-weighting choice, and the band thresholds + labels recorded with confidence + detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/482-coverage-strength-rollup-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/482-coverage-strength-rollup-visualization.md`.
- **feat(web): slice 527 — admin user-assign dialog gets user + tenant dropdowns (replaces raw-UUID text inputs).** A maintainer UX follow-on to slice 479: the `/admin/users` assign-to-tenant dialog took the **user id** and **tenant id** as raw UUID `Input`s — correct but operator-hostile (copy/paste two UUIDs, no way to see who/which you're choosing, no guard against a typo'd-but-well-formed UUID). This slice replaces both with **dropdowns**. **User dropdown (AC-1):** populated from the user list the page **already loads** (`GET /api/admin/users`) — no second fetch; option label = display-name + email, value = user id; de-duplicated by user id so the super-admin cross-tenant list (N membership rows per person) renders one option per person. **Tenant dropdown (AC-2/AC-4):** for a super-admin, populated from `GET /api/admin/tenants` (the existing slice-143 BFF route — reused, no new endpoint); option label = tenant name, value = tenant id. **Tenant-admin pinning (AC-3 / the load-bearing authz-honesty check, P0-527-1 / P0-479-2):** a tenant-admin's tenant field is **pinned** read-only to their session tenant, NOT a cross-tenant chooser — and the cross-tenant tenant list is fetched **only** when the existing `cross_tenant` signal is true (`enabled: crossTenant`), so a tenant-admin's browser never even receives the other-tenant names (STRIDE-I closed at the _fetch_ boundary, not just render). **No second authority probe** — the field mode reuses the `cross_tenant` flag slice 479 already derives + the `/api/me` session tenant the page already fetches (P0-527-2). **Server stays the sole gate:** the selected ids POST to `/v1/admin/users/assign` exactly as before; the server (478) re-checks authority regardless of what the UI sends — the dropdown narrows, not widens, the input space, and a 403 still renders inline (P0-527-4). The self-assign affordance, the role checkbox group, and the revoke-with-confirm flow are unchanged (P0-527-3). **Primitive choice (decisions-log D1):** a NATIVE `<select>` (new `web/components/ui/select.tsx`, styled to match `Input`), NOT `@radix-ui/react-select` (the project has **no** Radix deps — its form family is `@base-ui/react`) and not base-ui's heavier popup Select — zero new dependencies (slice-277 P0), and a native `<select>` + associated `<label htmlFor>` is keyboard-navigable and screen-reader-labelled with no ARIA wiring to get wrong (AC-8, slice-331/363 a11y lineage). **Tests (AC-9):** vitest for the pure option-mapping + pinning logic (`web/lib/admin/assign-options.ts` — extracted as a node-testable `.ts` module per slice 069 / the slice-363 pattern; new per-file coverage floor added to `web/coverage-thresholds.json` per the slice-347 ratchet); the Playwright `admin-users.spec.ts` updated to drive the dropdowns (super-admin assign + self-assign, the tenant-admin pinned case asserting no chooser **and** the tenants BFF never called, the inline-403 case, the a11y labelling). **Type: JUDGMENT** — the native-`<select>` choice and the tenant-admin pinning UX recorded with confidence + detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/527-admin-user-assign-dropdowns-decisions.md`. A searchable combobox for very large user/tenant lists is filed as follow-on slice 528. Parent: slice 479; consumes slices 478 + 142/143. Slice doc: `docs/issues/527-admin-user-assign-dropdowns.md`.
- **feat(connectors): slice 488 — Datadog + Grafana monitoring connectors, the monitoring/alerting evidence family.** Two new first-class peer connectors — `connectors/datadog/` and `connectors/grafana/` — answer the recurring SOC 2 CC7.2 ("the entity monitors system components") evidence demand ("prove monitors/alerts are configured and route to on-call"), today a manual screenshot upload. Both follow the locked slice-004 / 486 / 487 connector pattern verbatim (register-per-run, stable `actor_id`, hour-truncated `observed_at`, scope minimums, vendor-native read-only auth) and emit **one shared evidence kind** — `monitoring.alert_config.v1` — because the two vendors' alert-configuration field set is identical at the config-inventory altitude (a `source_vendor` discriminator preserves provenance; the shared shape is built once in `connectors/monitoring/monrecord`). **Datadog** collects monitor inventory (name, type, enabled state, notification-target handles parsed from the monitor message) via the read-only Datadog API (`GET /api/v1/monitor`, `monitors_read` scope). **Grafana** collects alert-rule + notification-policy inventory (rule title, type, enabled/not-paused state, folder, contact-point name) via the read-only Grafana provisioning API (Viewer-role service-account token). Each record is pushed to the platform's single `IngestEvidence` / `Push` API with a sha256 content-hash and stable optional fields — **no platform-side wire change** (invariant #3, P0-488-1); each registers `profiles_supported=[pull]` and names its interval honestly (operator-scheduled, recommended 24h — **not** "continuous monitoring", P0-488-6). **The load-bearing over-collection guard (P0-488-3 / threat-model I):** both connectors collect monitor/alert + routing-target **metadata only** — rule name, type, enabled state, and the notification-target **handle / channel / contact-point NAME** — and never the secret webhook URL, integration tokens, recipient email addresses (PII), dashboard JSON, metric time-series, or log results. The guard is enforced by construction (the shared `alertcfg.Target` type has no secret field; the Grafana `ContactPoint` struct has no `settings` field, and the Datadog client never decodes the monitor `query` / `options` blob — so secrets never enter memory as connector data) plus tests: the Datadog message parser **drops `@user@example.com` email-recipient mentions**, and a record-shape test asserts no secret URL / token / recipient PII reaches an emitted payload. Source keys/tokens are read only from the environment (never a CLI flag), redacted on every credential format path, and never logged or placed into a record (P0-488-4); both auths require **read-only least-privilege scopes only** (Datadog `monitors_read`, Grafana `Viewer`), surfaced by each connector's `permissions` subcommand (P0-488-2). Both are **OSS, in-tree, read-only API** (P0-488-5) and faked in tests (no live Datadog/Grafana in CI — thin read-only HTTP clients, no heavy vendor SDKs). The shared `monitoring.alert_config.v1` JSON Schema lands in the schema-registry tree with `x-default-scf-anchors=[MON-01]` (Continuous Monitoring) and is registered in `DefaultSeed`; no migration. Datadog Cloud-SIEM rule evidence, Grafana SAML/RBAC config evidence, and an alert-firing-history event-driven profile are filed as follow-on slices 533–535 (P0-488-7). **Type: JUDGMENT** — the shared-vs-split evidence-kind decision, the `x-default-scf-anchors` choice, and the per-connector scope minimums are recorded with confidence + detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/488-monitoring-connectors-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/488-monitoring-connectors-datadog-grafana.md`.

- **feat(connectors): slice 487 — Kubernetes connector (RBAC + workload security-context), the first non-cloud-IaaS first-class peer.** A new `connectors/k8s/` connector follows the locked slice-004 / slice-486 pattern verbatim (register-per-run, stable `actor_id`, hour-truncated `observed_at`, scope minimums, vendor-native read-only auth) and emits **two evidence kinds**: `k8s.rbac_binding.v1` (Role / ClusterRole + their bindings — who-can-do-what, with the referenced role's rules attached and a connector-side `grants_wildcard` heuristic, read-only Kubernetes API `rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1`) and `k8s.workload_security_context.v1` (per-workload `runAsNonRoot` / `privileged` / `readOnlyRootFilesystem` / `allowPrivilegeEscalation` / host-namespace posture aggregated across containers, read-only `apps/v1`). Each record is pushed to the platform's single `IngestEvidence` / `Push` API with a sha256 content-hash and stable optional fields — **no platform-side wire change** (invariant #3, P0-487-1); the connector registers `profiles_supported=[pull]` and names its interval honestly (operator-scheduled, recommended 24h — **not** "continuous monitoring", P0-487-6). **The load-bearing scope-minimum guard (P0-487-2 / P0-487-3):** the connector requires a least-privilege read-only ClusterRole granting `get`/`list` only on the RBAC kinds + apps workloads + namespaces — **never write verbs, never wildcards, and critically never `get`/`list` on `secrets`** — and it collects RBAC + security-context **configuration only**, never Secret values, ConfigMap values, container env, or logs (the workload client decodes only the security-context fields, so env / volumes / Secret refs are discarded at the JSON boundary). `internal/k8sauth.DocumentedClusterRole()` is the single source of truth that the README, the `atlas-k8s permissions` subcommand, and a unit test all read; the test fails the build if anyone widens the rule set. The cluster token is read only from the environment / the projected in-cluster ServiceAccount mount (never a CLI flag), is redacted on every credential format path, and never enters an evidence record (P0-487-4). The connector is **API-based, not an in-node agent** (P0-487-5), **OSS and in-tree**, and faked in tests (no live cluster in CI — `client-go` is deliberately not pulled in; a thin read-only HTTP client mirrors the slice-486 dependency discipline). **Two evidence-kind JSON Schemas** land in the schema-registry tree with `x-default-scf-anchors` set (`IAC-21`/`IAC-22` for RBAC, `CFG-02`/`END-04` for workloads) and are registered in `DefaultSeed`; no migration. Tests cover the collect → push round-trip against mocked Kubernetes API surfaces, the no-Secret/ConfigMap/env-leak guard, and the no-token-log guard; new per-package coverage floors added. NetworkPolicy, Pod-Security-Standards admission-config, Secret-inventory (metadata-only), and a watch-based event-driven profile are filed as follow-on slices 523–526 (P0-487-7). **Type: JUDGMENT** — the two evidence-kind shapes, their `x-default-scf-anchors`, and the exact minimal ClusterRole recorded with confidence + detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/487-kubernetes-connector-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/487-kubernetes-connector.md`.
- **feat(connectors): slice 486 — Azure connector (Entra ID + Storage), bringing the third major cloud to parity with AWS.** A new `connectors/azure/` connector follows the locked slice-004 / slice-045 pattern verbatim (register-per-run, stable `actor_id`, hour-truncated `observed_at`, scope minimums, vendor-native read-only auth) and emits **two evidence kinds**: `azure.entra_role_assignment.v1` (directory-role / RBAC assignments + service-principal / app-registration inventory, read-only Microsoft Graph — `Directory.Read.All` + `Application.Read.All`) and `azure.storage_account_config.v1` (encryption-at-rest, secure-transfer / minimum-TLS, public-blob-access posture, read-only Azure Resource Manager — ARM **Reader** role). Each record is pushed to the platform's single `IngestEvidence` / `Push` API with a sha256 content-hash and stable optional fields — **no platform-side wire change** (invariant #3, P0-486-1); the connector registers `profiles_supported=[pull]` and names its interval honestly (operator-scheduled, recommended 24h — **not** "continuous monitoring", P0-486-6). **Least-privilege (P0-486-2):** only the two read-only Graph application permissions + the ARM Reader role are required and documented; the README and the `atlas-azure permissions` subcommand both render the canonical set and a unit test rejects any future widening into `*.ReadWrite.*` / `*.Manage` / Owner / Contributor / Global Administrator. **Confidentiality (P0-486-3/4):** the connector collects configuration + role-assignment metadata only — never blob/object contents, Key-Vault secret values, storage access keys, SAS tokens, or user PII beyond the assignment's display name — and the Azure credential (Entra app-registration client-credentials or managed identity) stays source-side, redacted on every format path, never logged and never placed in an evidence record (tests assert both). **Two evidence-kind JSON Schemas** land in the schema-registry tree with `x-default-scf-anchors` set (`IAC-21`/`IAC-22` for Entra, `CRY-04`/`NET-04` for Storage) and are registered in `DefaultSeed`. **Tests:** the Graph + ARM surfaces are interface-faked (no live Azure, no real credentials in CI); the in-package bufconn integration test exercises the full collect → build → `Push` → receipt round-trip for both kinds, asserts same-hour idempotency, no-PII/no-secret payloads, and credential redaction. New per-package coverage floors added; no migration (schemas seed via `DefaultSeed`). Does **not** ship AKS / NSG / Key-Vault / Azure-Policy evidence or an event-driven profile (P0-486-7; follow-ons filed in band 519–524). **Type: JUDGMENT** — the two evidence-kind shapes, their `x-default-scf-anchors`, and the scope minimum recorded with confidence + detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/486-azure-connector-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/486-azure-connector.md`.
- **feat(catalog): slice 481 — HIPAA Security Rule crosswalk loaded as the 5th framework through the generic importer (catalog-only; no new loader code).** Roadmap §10.2 names HIPAA Security Rule as the fourth phase-2 framework alongside ISO 27001:2022 (438/467), PCI DSS v4.0 (447), and NIST CSF 2.0 (480) — completing the §10.2 framework set. This slice ships **only the HIPAA Security Rule → SCF crosswalk catalog** (requirement nodes + STRM edges), exactly like 480, on the slice-438 framework-agnostic importer (`internal/api/soc2import`), so HIPAA ships as **pure data + tests + a decisions log** with **zero loader changes** (P0-481-2). **Catalog, not workflow (P0-481-1):** the slice does **not** ship the HIPAA covered-entity workflow, BAA tracking, the required-vs-addressable decision flow, breach risk-assessment (§164.402), or a HIPAA FrameworkScope ePHI example — all of that is the canvas §10.3 deferred phase-3 covered-entity workflow (filed as spillovers 516–518). **(1) Data (AC-1):** a DRAFT crosswalk at `data/crosswalks/hipaa-security-rule.yaml` (`framework_slug: hipaa_security_rule`, `framework_version: "2013-01-25"`, `source_attribution: community_draft`) ships a curated high-signal subset of **32 standards + implementation specifications spanning all three safeguard categories** — Administrative (§164.308, 18), Physical (§164.310, 5), Technical (§164.312, 9) — each mapped to **one** SCF anchor via an explicit STRM-typed edge (`relationship_type` + `strength` on every row — 12 `equal`, 7 `subset_of`, 13 `intersects_with`; strengths [0.6, 0.9]); every anchor referenced resolves against the seeded SCF catalog (P0-481-4) and every edge is requirement → SCF anchor, never requirement → requirement (invariant #7, P0-481-3). **(2) Five-framework graph proof (AC-4):** the integration suite extends the slices 438/447/480 invariant-#1 proof from four frameworks to five — a single shared SCF anchor (`IAC-01`) resolves to SOC 2 `CC6.1` + ISO `A.5.15` + PCI `8.2.1` + CSF `PR.AA-01` + HIPAA `164.312(a)(1)` through the **one** anchor row, with exactly one `scf_anchors` row for `IAC-01` (one control, N framework satisfactions; no per-framework duplication — invariant #1). **(3) Regulatory-weight confidentiality assertion (AC-5 — the load-bearing check):** because HIPAA governs ePHI, the integration suite explicitly asserts the HIPAA catalog `/anchors` payload carries **no tenant-scoped field** — the read projection is column-exhaustively checked against an allow-list of catalog reference columns (SCF anchor identity + STRM edge facts), and any tenant-scoped column (tenant/owner/lifecycle/freshness/evidence/implementation) fails the test loudly; tenant control-implementation state stays behind the slice-104 `?include=state` RLS opt-in (P0-481-5). **(4) Read path (AC-3):** `GET /v1/requirements/{id}/anchors` serves HIPAA requirement → anchor + STRM type with no new code (the framework-agnostic reverse-traversal route). **Required-vs-addressable** (45 CFR §164.306(d)) is preserved as a public-domain regulatory fact in the per-requirement YAML comments only — no structured loader field, no decision flow (P0-481-1; decisions log D6). **Tests:** pure-Go loader unit branches (parse, three-safeguard-category coverage, access-control→IAC-01, every-mapping-targets-an-anchor, five-framework code namespacing) run on every CI run; the integration suite covers import round-trip + idempotency (AC-6), does-not-disturb SOC 2/ISO/PCI/CSF (AC-2), the five-framework shared-anchor proof, the no-tenant-field confidentiality assertion, and rollback-on-nonexistent-anchor — and the existing SOC 2/ISO/PCI/CSF suites pass **unmodified** (AC-7). No new floored package or integration-shard entry (extends the already-enrolled `internal/api/soc2import`); no migration (`framework_requirements` + `fw_to_scf_edges` exist since slices 007/438). Does **not** bundle pre-built SCF data (P0-481-6) or ship a HIPAA FrameworkScope ePHI example (P0-481-7); the HIPAA Security Rule is a US-government public-domain work but the `community_draft` source-attribution discipline still applies. **Type: JUDGMENT** — STRM type + strength per standard/spec, the curated subset, and the required-vs-addressable handling recorded with confidence + detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/481-hipaa-crosswalk-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/481-hipaa-security-rule-crosswalk-loader.md`.
- **feat(catalog): slice 480 — NIST CSF 2.0 crosswalk loaded as the 4th framework through the generic importer (no new loader code).** Roadmap §10.2 names four phase-2 frameworks; ISO 27001:2022 (slices 438/467) and PCI DSS v4.0 (slice 447) shipped, and this slice adds **NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0** as the third of the four to land. The slice-438 importer (`internal/api/soc2import`) is already framework-agnostic — it loads any `(framework_slug, framework_version)` crosswalk YAML — so CSF ships as **pure data + tests + a decisions log**, with **zero loader changes** (P0-480-1). **(1) Data (AC-1):** a DRAFT crosswalk at `data/crosswalks/nist-csf-2.0.yaml` (`framework_slug: nist_csf`, `framework_version: "2.0"`, `source_attribution: community_draft`) ships a curated high-signal subset of **35 Subcategories spanning all six CSF Functions** (GOVERN, IDENTIFY, PROTECT, DETECT, RESPOND, RECOVER), each mapped to **one** SCF anchor via an explicit STRM-typed edge (`relationship_type` + `strength` on every row — 11 `equal`, 14 `subset_of`, 10 `intersects_with`; strengths [0.6, 0.9]); every anchor referenced resolves against the seeded SCF catalog (P0-480-4) and every edge is requirement → SCF anchor, never requirement → requirement (invariant #7, P0-480-2). **(2) Four-framework graph proof (AC-4):** the integration suite extends slices 438/447's invariant-#1 proof from three frameworks to four — a single shared SCF anchor (`IAC-01`) resolves to SOC 2 `CC6.1` + ISO `A.5.15` + PCI `8.2.1` + CSF `PR.AA-01` through the **one** anchor row, with exactly one `scf_anchors` row for `IAC-01` (one control, N framework satisfactions; no per-framework duplication — invariant #1, P0-480-3). **(3) GOVERN no-analog (AC-5 — the load-bearing differentiator):** CSF's GOVERN Function has no SOC 2 _Function_ counterpart; the suite proves a GOVERN Subcategory (`GV.RR-02`) maps to an SCF governance-family anchor (`GOV-04`) via an STRM edge and that every GOVERN edge traverses an SCF anchor (invariant #7) — demonstrating the graph models a Function with no v1-framework structural analog without assuming framework overlap. **(4) Read path (AC-3):** `GET /v1/requirements/{slug}/anchors` serves CSF requirement → anchor + STRM type with no new code (the slice-007 reverse-traversal route is framework-agnostic) and leaks no tenant control-implementation state — that stays behind the slice-104 `?include=state` RLS opt-in (P0-480-5). **Tests:** pure-Go loader unit branches (parse, six-Function coverage, GOVERN→GOV family, every-mapping-targets-an-anchor, four-framework code namespacing) run on every CI run; the integration suite covers import round-trip + idempotency (AC-6), does-not-disturb SOC 2/ISO/PCI (AC-2), the four-framework shared-anchor proof, the GOVERN no-analog proof, and rollback-on-nonexistent-anchor — and the existing SOC 2/ISO/PCI suites pass **unmodified** (AC-8, proving the loader is unchanged). No new floored package or integration-shard entry (extends the already-enrolled `internal/api/soc2import`); no migration (`framework_requirements` + `fw_to_scf_edges` exist since slices 007/438). Does **not** ship a CSF Tier/Profile assessment workflow (P0-480-6) or bundle pre-built SCF data (P0-480-7); CSF 2.0 is a US-government public-domain work but the `community_draft` source-attribution discipline still applies (P0-480-8). **Type: JUDGMENT** — STRM type + strength per Subcategory, the curated subset, and the GOVERN anchor choice recorded with confidence + detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/480-nist-csf-crosswalk-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/480-nist-csf-2-0-crosswalk-loader.md`.
- **feat(oscal): slice 494 — Assessment-Results export carries the drawn sample evidence + walkthrough attachment refs.** Canvas §8.2 defines the OSCAL Assessment-Results artifact as "sampled evidence records + auditor annotations" and §8.3/§8.5 define a walkthrough as a narrative **plus** attachments exported as observation evidence — but two pieces shipped unwired in the AR exporter (`internal/oscal/aggregate.go`, `assessmentInput()`): the `SampledEvidenceIds: nil` placeholder meant the AR told an auditor "we drew N of M" but **not which N**, and the walkthrough loop had no attachment handling so the captured screen-grabs/transcripts were absent. An AR that omits the sampled records sends the auditor back to a spreadsheet — the gap most likely to break the v1 "the auditor works in our tool" promise. This slice wires both. **(1) Drawn sample (AC-1/AC-2/AC-3):** each `SamplePopulation` now carries its `sampled_evidence_ids[]`, **read from the persisted `sample_evidence` rows** (slice 026 materializes the realized Fisher-Yates draw at draw-time over the frozen population) rather than recomputing at export — the slice's compute-vs-persist JUDGMENT call, resolved to "read the persisted draw" because the seed lives on `samples` not `populations`, the persisted rows are frozen-correct **by construction** (invariant #10), and reading them is cheaper than re-shuffling (slice-028 spirit, D1). A new join query `ListSampledEvidenceForPeriod` (populations → most-recent sample → sample_evidence, ordered by ordinal = shuffle order) carries the draw; the `nil` placeholder + comment are removed; a never-sampled population honestly carries an empty set. **(2) Walkthrough attachments (AC-4/AC-5):** each `Walkthrough` carries its attachment refs (id, filename, content hash, content type, annotation ref, storage URI) via a new `ListWalkthroughAttachmentsForPeriod` query and a new `WalkthroughAttachment` proto message; the Python bridge maps them onto the walkthrough's **`observation.relevant-evidence[]`** (`href` = object-storage URI, content hash/type/annotation as props) — the OSCAL-native, single-hop home that co-locates the evidence with the walkthrough it evidences and round-trips cleanly through compliance-trestle (D2, vs. back-matter resources). The attachment **bytes are never embedded** — referenced by hash + URI only (P0-494-2); the signed bundle is the integrity layer. A per-walkthrough cap of 50 refs with an honest "N additional attachment(s) not shown" overflow note bounds AR size without ever silently dropping evidence (threat-model D, D3). **Invariant #10 (the load-bearing concern):** the draw runs over the frozen population, never live data — proven by an integration test that freezes a period, adds post-`frozen_at` evidence, exports, and asserts the post-freeze record **never** appears in the sampled set (AC-7, P0-494-1). **Tenant isolation (invariant #6):** both new reads run under the same tenant `app.current_tenant` RLS context + transaction as the rest of the aggregate; an integration test proves Tenant A's sampled evidence + attachment hashes never appear in Tenant B's AR (AC-8, P0-494-3). **Reproducibility (§8.3):** re-running `audit/sample.Sample` with the persisted seed against the frozen population yields the exact AR draw (AC-9). **Tests:** fast proto-conversion unit branches (sampled-ids, attachment refs, cap + overflow, filename/annotation helpers) run on every CI run; the load-bearing AC-6/7/8/9 integration tests run through the **real DB read path with a capturing fake bridge** so they execute on every CI integration shard, not only when the optional Python oscal-bridge is present (slice-493 D-test pattern); a separate bridge-gated round-trip test proves wire fidelity (sampled-id props + relevant-evidence survive trestle) and skips when the bridge is unavailable. Extended the bridge pytest's AR test to assert the new sampled-id props + relevant-evidence emission. Does **not** change the AP/SSP/POA&M serializers (P0-494-5), does **not** alter the cosign signing path (P0-494-6), does **not** change the slice-026 sampler determinism contract — it **consumes** the existing draw (P0-494-4), and adds **no** new floored package or integration-shard entry (extends the already-enrolled `internal/oscal`). **Type: JUDGMENT** — the read-persisted-vs-recompute call, the `relevant-evidence`-vs-back-matter OSCAL mapping, the attachment-ref cap, and the additive proto-field shape recorded with confidence + detection-tier in `docs/audit-log/494-ar-sampled-evidence-decisions.md`. Resolves slice 030's `SampledEvidenceIds` stopgap. Slice doc: `docs/issues/494-assessment-results-sampled-evidence-walkthrough-attachments.md`.
- **docs: slice 513 — correct the AI-assist-boundary canonical-adopter reference (`QuestionnaireAnswer` → `mcp_write_proposals`).** Both `CLAUDE.md` ("AI-assist boundary (hard)") and `Plans/canvas/04-evidence-engine.md` §4.6.5 asserted the schema-level `ai_assisted=true ⇒ human_approver` enforcement using `QuestionnaireAnswer` as the canonical example that already carries the `ai_assisted`/`human_approved`/`human_approver` columns. Slice 498's `database-designer` pass (decisions-log D5) established that those columns do **not** exist on `questionnaire_answers` on `main`: the real shipped adopter is `mcp_write_proposals` (slice 173, migration `20260520030000_mcp_write_proposals.sql`), and slice 498 extracted that predicate into the shared reusable `ai_assist_human_approver_guard` IMMUTABLE function + CHECK template (`internal/llm` + `migrations/sql/20260607000000_ai_generations.sql`) that new AI-assist surfaces adopt verbatim. This correction points both references at the real enforcement surface, cross-references slice 498's decisions-log D5, and notes that `questionnaire_answers` gains the columns (adopting the same shared guard) when slice 440 lands the questionnaire answer-suggestion surface. The constitutional boundary itself is unchanged — only the illustrative adopter is corrected. **Docs-only:** no code, no schema, no migration. **Type: AFK** — decisions log at `docs/audit-log/513-ai-adopter-correction-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 498; relates to slice 440. Slice doc: `docs/issues/513-ai-assist-boundary-canonical-adopter-doc-correction.md`.
- **feat(oscal): slice 493 — SSP carries authored control-implementation narratives (not the synthesized placeholder).** Canvas §8.2 specifies the OSCAL SSP is generated from "control implementation narratives," but the exporter shipped a stopgap (slice 030 `D-narrative`): the SSP `ControlImplementation.Statement` was filled twice — first with `c.ApplicabilityExpr` (an explicit placeholder), then overwritten with a templated `"<Title> (control family: <family>). Implementation owned by role <owner>."` string — because the read projection (`ListActiveControls`) exposed `title`/`control_family` but **not** the control bundle's human-authored `description` (slice 009). Every exported SSP therefore had cookie-cutter implementation statements where the narrative that explains _how_ a control is implemented — the single most-read section of an SSP to an auditor — should be. This slice wires the authored narrative through. **(1) Read path (AC-1):** a purpose-built companion query `ListActiveControlsWithDescription` carries the `description` column; `ListActiveControls` is left untouched for its other consumers (pattern-matched to the slice-137 / slice-175 export-projection convention — D-query). **(2) Statement source (AC-2/AC-4):** `ControlImplementation.Statement` is filled **exactly once** with the authored description verbatim; the `ApplicabilityExpr` placeholder write and the double-fill loop are removed. **(3) Fallback (AC-3, P0-493-1):** a control with no authored description (a manual/minimal bundle) falls back to a **clearly-labeled** synthesized summary (`"[Auto-generated summary — no authored implementation narrative on file.] Control \"…\" (family: …); implementation owned by role \"…\". Provide an authored …"`) — **never** an empty statement, and labeled so an auditor is not misled into thinking the boilerplate is authored (D-fallback). No LLM is involved — the statement is the operator's text or a deterministic template, honoring the constitutional AI-assist boundary. **(4) Tenant-scoped + point-in-time consistent (AC-5):** the new read runs under the same `app.current_tenant` RLS context inside the same export transaction as the rest of the aggregate (invariant #6). **Tests:** unit branches at the proto-conversion tier (authored-present verbatim, description-absent labeled fallback, no double-fill) + a real-Postgres integration suite proving the authored description reaches the SSP JSON verbatim (AC-6), the description-less fallback (AC-7), and — load-bearing — **tenant isolation** (Tenant A's control descriptions never appear in Tenant B's SSP, AC-8 / P0-493-2). The content + isolation assertions run through the real DB read path with a capturing fake bridge so they execute on every CI run, not only when the optional Python oscal-bridge is present (D-test); a separate bridge-gated round-trip test covers wire fidelity and skips when the bridge is unavailable (slice-030 D2 precedent). Does **not** change the AP/AR/POA&M serializers (P0-493-3), does **not** alter the cosign signing path (P0-493-5), and does **not** add rich-text rendering or per-component decomposition (scope discipline — follow-on). Resolves slice 030's `D-narrative` stopgap (slice-030 decisions log updated). **Type: JUDGMENT** — fallback wording, companion-query-vs-widened-projection, and bridge-independent test design recorded in `docs/audit-log/493-ssp-narratives-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/493-ssp-control-implementation-narrative.md`.
- **feat(web): slice 479 — admin user-management UI: assign users to tenants + roles (incl. self).** Wires the slice-060 `/admin/users` scaffold (which rendered the role-permission matrix plus a placeholder table and documented a missing-endpoint gap) to the just-merged slice-478 API, giving a super-admin a supported, audited replacement for the hand-DB-surgery that was otherwise required to manage who belongs to which tenant. **User list (AC-1):** binds to `GET /v1/admin/users` via a new BFF route (`web/app/api/admin/users/route.ts`); a super-admin gets the cross-tenant shape (each row tagged with `tenant_id`) and a tenant-admin gets the within-tenant shape. The page derives a `cross_tenant` flag from the response **shape** (every row carrying a non-empty `tenant_id`) rather than a second probe, so the server stays the single source of authority truth. The slice-060 role-permission matrix is retained below the table as the canonical role reference. **Assign (AC-2):** a labelled dialog (user UUID + tenant UUID + a checkbox per canonical role, sourced from the slice-060 `ROLES` constant) POSTs to `/v1/admin/users/assign`; success/error render inline and the list refetches via TanStack Query invalidation. **Revoke (AC-3):** a per-row action behind a confirm dialog → `POST /v1/admin/users/revoke` (the within-tenant shape, which carries no row `tenant_id`, falls back to the session tenant from `/api/me`). **Self-assign (AC-4):** an "Add me to a tenant" affordance for super-admins; on success a notice explains that the current session token predates the new membership, so a full re-login (linked to `/login?from=/admin/users`) is required before the tenant appears in the switcher — we do **NOT** auto-switch (P0-479-3) and do not attempt a token-exchange that would fail on a not-yet-available tenant. **Authz-honest (AC-5 / P0-479-1 / P0-479-2):** authority is enforced **server-side** (478 is the gate); the BFF forwards the bearer and passes the upstream status + error through verbatim. A tenant-admin's within-tenant response shows **no** cross-tenant controls (no Tenant column, no self-assign button); a 403 from any mutation renders as a clear inline message, never a dead button or a silent failure. **Accessible (AC-6):** the assign dialog inputs are `<label htmlFor>`-associated, the role group is a `<fieldset>`/`<legend>`, error alerts carry `aria-live` + a stable id, per the slice-331 / slice-363 a11y lineage. **Scope:** UI over 478's API only — NO backend behavior (478 owns it), NO invitation/email/SCIM UI (P0-479-4), NO change to the membership-bounded switcher for normal users (P0-479-5). **Tests (AC-7):** vitest for both new BFF route handlers + their pure validation helpers (`route.test.ts`, `revoke/route.test.ts` — 24 cases, neutral `test-bearer-479` fixtures, per-file coverage floors added to `web/coverage-thresholds.json` per the slice-347 ratchet); a Playwright e2e (`web/e2e/admin-users.spec.ts`) mirroring `admin-tenants.spec.ts` (BFF mocked via `page.route()` — the 478 cross-tenant write is a BYPASSRLS platform-global row the harness cannot clean up; 478's Go integration tests own the real DB behaviour) covering the super-admin list + assign + self-assign re-auth notice + revoke confirm + the authz-honest tenant-admin case + the inline-403 case + the a11y labelling. **Type: JUDGMENT** — role-picker shape, response-shape-derived super-admin scope, re-login (not token-exchange) for self-assign, BFF-mock e2e, and the within-tenant revoke tenant_id fallback recorded in `docs/audit-log/479-user-mgmt-ui-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 478. Slice doc: `docs/issues/479-admin-user-management-ui.md`.
- **feat(auth): slice 478 — super-admin user↔tenant↔role assignment API (incl. self-assignment).** Closes the deferred slice-060.5 backend gap: there was no admin API to manage who belongs to which tenant. A user's tenant membership is a `users` row keyed by `(idp_issuer, idp_subject)` per tenant (read by the slice-192 resolver's `enumerateMemberships`); capabilities come from `user_roles` (slice-035 enum). The slice-062 `adminusers` package only exposed a within-tenant list/get/patch-roles surface. This slice extends it with the **cross-tenant super-admin** surface — a super-admin can now list users across every tenant, **assign** a user identity to a tenant with role(s), **revoke**, and crucially **self-assign** (add themselves to a tenant they super-administer but are not a member of, so the membership-bounded switcher [P0-192-5] surfaces it after re-auth). **Routes:** `GET /v1/admin/users` now dispatches super-admins to a paginated cross-tenant view (keyset cursor over `(tenant_id, id)`, bounded — no unbounded scan) and everyone else to the unchanged within-tenant list; `POST /v1/admin/users/assign` and `POST /v1/admin/users/revoke` are new. **Authority tiers (P0-478-1):** cross-tenant writes require the verified `atlas:super_admin` JWT claim (enforced server-side by `requireSuperAdmin`, second-legged by the OPA `admin.rego`/default-deny gate — P0-478-4); within-tenant writes are allowed for a tenant-admin and run under RLS; a tenant-admin naming a foreign tenant is denied 403 (tested). Cross-tenant writes use the BYPASSRLS auth pool (the target tenant ≠ the actor's GUC), mirroring `admintenants.Create`; assign is atomic (membership + roles in one tx) and idempotent. **THE load-bearing call — local-auth identity (P0-478-2, slice 478 D1):** local operators have an empty `(idp_issuer, idp_subject)` tuple; copying it into a second-tenant membership would make the resolver over-match _every_ local user across _all_ tenants (the slice-476 hazard). Resolved by minting a **stable synthetic per-user key** (`idp_issuer='urn:atlas:local'`, `idp_subject=<origin users.id>`) written to both the new membership row and (backfill) the origin row — non-empty so the resolver surfaces it, unique per local user so it can never over-match; local login is unchanged (the synthetic issuer is never used for token exchange). The non-over-match is proven by an integration test that assigns two distinct local users to a shared tenant and asserts each resolves to exactly its own two memberships. **Audit (AC-7):** every assign/revoke writes a tenant-scoped `me_audit_log` row (surfaced by the slice-124 aggregator) plus, on the super-admin cross-tenant path, a platform-global `super_admin_audit_log` row + a slice-126 sink fanout; two new action values (`user_tenant_assign`, `user_tenant_revoke`) extend both CHECK constraints (`migrations/sql/20260607010000_user_tenant_assignment_audit.sql`, reversible). **Scope:** backend API + membership/role write + the `available_tenants` refresh path only; NO UI (slice 479), NO invitation/email/SCIM/password flows, NO auto-switch of the actor's tenant (P0-478-5), NO change to OPA role semantics. The slice-192 resolver/switcher is untouched (P0-478-3). **Tests:** pure-Go unit (`helpers_test.go`: assign validation, cursor round-trip, limit clamp, synthetic-key invariant) + a real-Postgres integration suite (cross-tenant assign + idempotency + audit, self-assign reachable in the real resolver end-to-end, local-auth non-over-match, the authz DENY cases, within-tenant RLS-correct write, cross-tenant-vs-scoped list). Neutral `test-*` fixtures only (P0-478-6). **Type: JUDGMENT** — local-auth identity mechanism, the two-tier authority model, revoke membership semantics, and the audit dual-write recorded in `docs/audit-log/478-user-tenant-assignment-decisions.md`. Unblocks slice 479 (the admin user-management UI) and subsumes the mechanism slice 476 needed (demo reachability via self-assign). Slice doc: `docs/issues/478-super-admin-user-tenant-assignment-api.md`.
- **feat(ai): slice 498 — shared `internal/llm` local-inference foundation + `ai_generations` audit record + runtime `ai_assisted ↔ human_approver` DB enforcement.** Builds the AI-assist substrate the four ready v0 surfaces (440 / 441 / 444 / 471) each assumed someone else would build — slice 182 shipped only the board-narrative _documentation_ foundation (tone list, ADR, schema-extension _contract_), no code, and on `main` `internal/llm` did not exist, no Ollama client existed, no shared `ai_generations` table existed, and the only `ai_assisted`/`human_approver` adopter was `mcp_write_proposals` (slice 173). This slice extracts the three load-bearing, security-critical pieces into a dedicated foundation so the surfaces become thin consumers. **(1) `internal/llm` client** — a narrow, provider-agnostic `Client` interface (`Generate(ctx, GenerateRequest) → (GenerateResult, error)`; request = system prompt + pre-assembled context + optional model id + **mandatory** token budget + timeout; result = opaque text + resolved `model_name`/`model_version`/`model_provider`) with a **local Ollama** implementation as the default (Llama 3.1 8B per slice-182 D5, read from `ATLAS_LLM_*` config; never egresses past the configured local endpoint — P0-498-1, no cloud routing = slice 499) and a `StubClient` CI seam (the documented unblock so 440/441/444/471 stub identically without a live Ollama). The client takes **pre-assembled context** — no pgvector / retrieval (P0-498-2 = slice 500). Caps are enforced **before** any inference: an over-cap or timeout-less request is rejected, never an unbounded job (P0-498-6). Model output is opaque text in/out — never executed, eval'd, or interpolated into SQL/shell/path (P0-498-7). **(2) `ai_generations` audit record** (`migrations/sql/20260607000000_ai_generations.sql`) — one tenant-scoped, **append-only** row per generation across every surface (`surface` enum, `prompt_version`, model name/version/provider, full system prompt, assembled `context_inputs` JSONB, `raw_draft`, `surface_subject` linkage, `created_at`); written via **parameterized sqlc**; **four-policy-shaped RLS** on `app.current_tenant` (append-only → SELECT + INSERT policies only under `FORCE`, no UPDATE/DELETE policy + no UPDATE/DELETE grant = immutable snapshot, P0-498-5; same pattern as `evidence_audit_log`/`decisions_audit`/`artifact_access_log`). **(3) Reusable enforcement** — a shipped IMMUTABLE SQL function `ai_assist_human_approver_guard(ai_assisted, human_approved, human_approver)` adopted by a one-line CHECK on each approvable consumer record (so the boundary predicate is authored once going forward), mirrored by a Go guard (`llm.EnforceApproval`) for friendly early rejection; the **DB CHECK is authoritative** — `human_approved=true` with a missing/blank `human_approver` is rejected at the DB layer (P0-498-4). `mcp_write_proposals` keeps its inlined CHECK unchanged (no behavior change — AC-8). **(4) Smoke path** — `Service.GenerateAndRecord` ties stub client → `ai_generations` write → enforcement end-to-end (AC-10, internal only, no user-facing surface = P0-498-3). **Tests:** pure-Go unit (request/cap validation, stub, enforcement guard ±, config, deterministic context render — slice-353 Q-2 `helpers_test.go`) + Ollama transport over `httptest` (success, over-cap-before-IO, backend error, timeout, default-model fallback) + a real-Postgres integration suite proving **cross-tenant isolation** (tenant B sees zero of tenant A's rows — P0-498-8 / AC-12), **append-only** (UPDATE/DELETE denied), and the **DB-layer enforcement** via an ephemeral adopter table that CHECK-rejects the forbidden write (AC-9). Neutral `test-*` fixtures only (P0-498-9). Merged unit+integration coverage measured 86.5% locally; `internal/llm` floored at 80 and enrolled in integration shard Leg B3. **Type: JUDGMENT** — Client shape, `ai_generations` columns, enforcement placement (DB CHECK + reusable fn), CI-stub approach, and the AC-8 adopter-drift resolution recorded in `docs/audit-log/498-llm-foundation-decisions.md`; contract + CI-stubbing + local-model quality caveat in `docs/ai-assist/llm-foundation.md`. Unblocks slices 440 / 441 / 444 / 471; extended by 499 (cloud opt-in + banner) and 500 (pgvector grounding). Slice doc: `docs/issues/498-shared-local-inference-client-foundation.md`.

- **feat(cli): slice 464 — `atlas evidence verify` ledger-wide integrity walk (+ `SELF_HOSTING.md` migrate-prose fix).** Slice 432's restore drill surfaced that `atlas evidence verify` — referenced by the BCP/DR plan, `SELF_HOSTING.md`, and the restore runbook — **did not ship**: only `evidence push` existed. This slice adds the operator-facing verb. `atlas evidence verify` walks the append-only evidence ledger (keyset-paginated, `--page-size`, streaming for large ledgers), recomputes each record's canonical hash from its persisted columns via the **same `canonjson` primitive** the ingest path uses, and reports any record whose stored `hash` no longer matches — a silently-corrupted or tampered record. **READ-ONLY** (canvas invariant #2 — never mutates the ledger). **Role-correct (AC-2):** a tenant-scoped walk (`--tenant <uuid>`) runs as `atlas_app` under RLS (transaction-scoped `app.current_tenant` GUC); the cross-tenant walk (`--all-tenants`, super-admin) uses the documented `SET LOCAL ROLE atlas_service_account` path (BYPASSRLS, reachable only from `atlas_app`), **never a superuser connection**. A new migration grants `atlas_service_account` **SELECT-only** on `evidence_records` so the cross-tenant read works without a write grant. **Exit codes:** 0 = clean (zero mismatches), 1 = mismatches found, 2 = operational error. **Load-bearing design call (D1):** the ingest hash covers the full `EvidenceRecord` proto **including `scope`**, but the wire `scope` is **not persisted** in the ledger (only the empty `scope_id` column) — so the original scope-inclusive ingest hash is provably **not reproducible** from a ledger row (confirmed empirically). The verify contract is therefore "the stored `hash` equals the canonical hash of the record **as reconstructable from the ledger**"; this faithfully detects any mutation of the persisted columns (payload, result, kind, …) — the dominant tamper surface. Aligning the ingest hash to be ledger-reproducible (persist canonical scope) is filed as a follow-up. **AC-3 proven on a live Postgres:** an integration test in the (coverage-excluded, already-shard-enrolled) `internal/evidence/ingest` package pushes a record via the real `Process`, asserts a clean walk reports zero mismatches, then **deliberately corrupts the `payload` column out-of-band** and asserts the walk detects the hash divergence (stored=`ee07b0c0…` recomputed=`cb2a577b…`); the CLI's four exit paths were exercised end-to-end against the live binary. **Secondary fix (AC-5):** `docs/SELF_HOSTING.md` step 5 documented `docker compose exec atlas atlas migrate up`, a subcommand the platform binary does **not** expose; corrected to the real `atlas-bootstrap` one-shot. **Docs (AC-4):** `docs-site/docs/backup-restore.md` drill now runs `atlas evidence verify --all-tenants` alongside the signed-OSCAL-export verify; the "not yet present" admonition is removed. **AC-6** (`docs/governance/business-continuity.md`, slice-373-owned) references now resolve to the real command — left to that doc's owner per the BCP/DR plan §11 annual-review reconciliation (not edited unilaterally). The verb adds **no** new floored package and **no** `scripts/integration-shards.txt` change. **Type: JUDGMENT** — decisions log `docs/audit-log/464-evidence-verify-cli-decisions.md`. Parent: slice 432. Slice doc: `docs/issues/464-atlas-evidence-verify-cli-ledger-integrity-walk.md`.

- **test(e2e): slice 470 — real header-overwriting reverse-proxy container in the self-host e2e harness, proving slice 466's `TRUSTED_PROXY_CIDRS` walk end-to-end.** Slice 466 replaced the blunt `TRUST_FORWARDED_HEADERS` boolean with a `TRUSTED_PROXY_CIDRS` allowlist + a right-to-left `X-Forwarded-For` walk, and covered it at the unit tier plus over a **real TCP connection** (`internal/api/auth/clientip_server_test.go`). What it could NOT prove — because the single-VM seed ships **no proxy container by design** (466 D4) — was the **multi-container topology**: a real header-overwriting proxy process in front of atlas, connecting from a container-network address inside `TRUSTED_PROXY_CIDRS`. This slice lands exactly that e2e. A `nginx:1.27-alpine` proxy (`deploy/docker/proxy/nginx.conf`) that **hard-overwrites** `X-Forwarded-For` with a fixed TEST-NET-3 client IP (`203.0.113.10`) is added in front of atlas via a compose overlay (`deploy/docker/docker-compose.proxy.yml`) on a **fixed-subnet** network (`10.123.0.0/24`, so `TRUSTED_PROXY_CIDRS` is deterministic, not a guess against docker's arbitrary default 172.x subnet). The existing self-host e2e harness (`deploy/docker/test-self-host-bundle.sh`) gains a third mode, `proxy`, with two assertions driving the production `/auth/local/login` → `clientIP(r)` → `sessions.ip_address` path, each pinned to the **exact** session row its login created (captured via the `atlas_session` cookie value = session id, read back as the postgres superuser): **AC-2** — a login THROUGH the proxy records the proxy-supplied client IP (`203.0.113.10`); **AC-3** — a DIRECT login forging `X-Forwarded-For: 198.51.100.66` (TEST-NET-2) records the direct container peer, **NOT** the forged value (the walk stops at the untrusted hop — spoofing rejected). The `proxy` leg is added to the CI `test-self-host-bundle` matrix (`mode: [bundled, external, proxy]`, AC-4); the failure-logs dump step is made overlay-aware so the proxy container's logs surface on a red run. **Does NOT touch the resolver** (`internal/api/auth/clientip.go` — slice 466 shipped + tested it; this slice is the e2e PROOF, not a reimplementation), does NOT change the shipped single-VM seed (no proxy there by design), and adds **no** Go integration package (so `scripts/integration-shards.txt` is untouched). **Validation:** the full proxy-mode harness was run **live** locally against a real multi-container bring-up — both AC-2 and AC-3 assertions passed; `nginx -t` confirms the proxy config syntax; `docker compose config` confirms the overlay merge (proxy service + `atlasnet` fixed subnet + `TRUSTED_PROXY_CIDRS` on atlas) and that the base `bundled`/`external` modes are unchanged. **Type: JUDGMENT** — decisions log `docs/audit-log/470-trusted-proxy-e2e-decisions.md` (placement, image, assertion mechanism, deterministic-CIDR, hard-overwrite, bundled-auth-reuse). Parent: slice 466 (466 D4 / R3). Slice doc: `docs/issues/470-trusted-proxy-e2e-seed-harness.md`.
- **feat(catalog): slice 438 — ISO 27001:2022 crosswalk loader (2nd framework — first empirical proof of UCF invariant #1).** Until now the control graph carried exactly one framework (SOC 2, slice 007), so the project's most load-bearing invariant — **#1, "one control, N framework satisfactions"** — was asserted but **unproven** (a single framework cannot show one SCF anchor satisfying two frameworks at once).
