- **fix(web): slice 672 — read-only policy detail route + in-shell 404 (closes 672).** Policy titles in `/policies` linked to `/policies/{id}`, but no `web/app/(authed)/policies/[id]/page.tsx` existed, so every click was a hard Next 404 — and worse, the 404 rendered **shell-less** (no `not-found.tsx` anywhere under `web/app`), so the operator was stranded with no nav and only browser-back recovered (UI-audit ATLAS-024). **JUDGMENT D1 — build, not remove:** the backend read surface already exists in full (`GET /v1/policies/{id}` in `internal/api/policies/handlers.go` returns the complete policy — `body_md`, `version`, `status`, `owner_role`, `effective_date`, `published_at`, plus `/pdf` and `/acknowledgment-rate`), and the seeded policies carry real `body_md`, so a read-only detail page is the honest fix (removing the link would discard a shipped capability). **What ships:** a read-only `/policies/[id]` detail page (title, status badge, version, owner role, effective + published dates, the `body_md` rendered as markdown, the acknowledgment rate for published policies, and a PDF link) mirroring the controls/[id] + vendors/[id] precedent; a BFF proxy `web/app/api/policies/[id]/route.ts` over `GET /v1/policies/{id}` reusing the **same cookie-session auth** as the list BFF (RLS-tenant-scoped — **invariant #6**: no client-supplied `tenant_id` is accepted or forwarded; the vitest test asserts the upstream URL never carries `tenant_id`); and an in-shell not-found boundary at `web/app/(authed)/not-found.tsx` so a 404 inside the authed area renders WITHIN the app shell (sidebar/nav present, recoverable) — a genuinely-missing policy id `notFound()`s into it (plus a minimal global `web/app/not-found.tsx` for unauthed routes). **Markdown (D2):** the repo ships no markdown library and `body_md` was never rendered as markdown on `main`; rather than pull in `react-markdown` + its transitive tree (Article VII Simplicity Gate), a pure, vitest-covered `web/lib/markdown.ts` renders the subset the seeded policies use. **Security (load-bearing):** the renderer HTML-escapes input FIRST, before any transform, and only emits a fixed grammar of safe tags around already-escaped text — a `body_md` carrying `<script>` or `<img onerror=...>` renders as inert visible text, and only safe `http(s)`/relative/`#`/`mailto` link hrefs survive (`javascript:` drops to plain text); the page's `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` is safe by construction. **No internal-error leak (slice 367):** the BFF maps upstream 404 → `notFound()`, 401 → the `/login` redirect, and other errors → a clean status line (the 5xx test asserts the raw upstream body is not echoed). **Proof:** vitest covers the new BFF (6 cases — 401 / draft one-call / published two-call ack compose / ack-409 graceful / 404→clean / 5xx no-leak) and the markdown renderer (15 cases incl. the inert-render + href-drop security cases), with per-file coverage floors added and `lib/api/policies.ts`'s floor lifted in the same PR; a new Playwright e2e (`e2e/policy-detail.spec.ts`, hermetically route-mocked) asserts (1) clicking a policy title lands on a 200 detail page rendering the title + markdown body and (2) a missing-uuid renders the in-shell not-found with the desktop sidebar still visible (NOT a stranded full-page 404). **Pure `web/` slice — backend UNTOUCHED:** `git diff --stat origin/main...HEAD -- internal/ migrations/ proto/ cmd/` is empty; no migration, no proto, no `evidence_kind`. **Type: JUDGMENT.** Decisions log: `docs/audit-log/672-policy-detail-route-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/672-policy-detail-route-404.md`.

- **fix(search): slice 661 — index SCF anchors so an empty tenant's controls are findable (closes 661).** Global header search returned NOTHING for control / SCF-anchor queries (`encryption`, exact `CRY-04`) on a fresh tenant even though the anchors are present in `/controls` + `/catalog/scf` (UI-audit ATLAS-002). **Root cause:** `internal/api/search/search.go` searched only the tenant-scoped `controls` (instantiated controls), `risks`, and `evidence` tables — there was no `scf_anchors` (catalog) branch. On an empty tenant there are zero instantiated controls; the ~53 bundled SCF anchors live in the catalog and were unindexed, so the operator's most natural query returned nothing. **Fix:** a new `anchors` result type + `searchAnchors` branch matches the bundled `scf_anchors` catalog against `scf_id` (so `CRY-04` matches by code) AND `title` + `description` (so `encryption` matches by name), returning `"<scf_id> — <title>"` hits that link to `/catalog/scf/{id}`. The anchor query reads the catalog via a plain pool query (`queryCatalog`), NOT the RLS-bound tenant path (`queryInTenantTx`): `scf_anchors` is platform-bundled, tenant-AGNOSTIC reference data (no tenant_id, no RLS — migration `20260511000001_scf_anchors.sql` line 24), so this is **invariant-#6-safe by construction** — there is no tenant column to leak across. The controls/risks/evidence branches stay RLS-tenant-scoped exactly as before. Latest-version dedup mirrors `ListSCFAnchorsLatest` (`slug='scf' AND status='current' AND tenant_id IS NULL`) so a multi-version catalog returns each anchor once. The `anchors` type is wired end-to-end: backend result `Type` + `parseTypes` admit-map, the BFF passthrough (`web/app/api/search/route.ts` — no whitelist; the upstream owns validation), and the ⌘K popover (`web/components/shell/global-search.tsx` — new "SCF anchors" group rendered first, linking to the catalog detail page). The `anchors` OPA resource-type is already a public catalog read (`policies/authz/defaults.rego` `catalog_resources`), so no policy change was needed. **AC-5 latency:** the reported multi-second `CRY-04` hang was NOT the anchor query — `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` measures 0.14 ms over a bounded (~62 sample / ~1,400 full) catalog seq scan; a leading-wildcard `ILIKE` cannot use a btree index and the row count does not justify `pg_trgm` GIN infra (slice 268 P0-A2 defers full-text). The hang was cold-start + the sequential per-type fan-out (4 round trips on the first request to a fresh server); **no index, no migration** added. Integration test `TestSlice661_AnchorSearch_EmptyTenant` (real Postgres) seeds the bundled catalog + a zero-control tenant and asserts `q=CRY-04` and `q=encryption` return anchor hits while a second tenant's matching control is NEVER returned (invariant #6); it fails decisively without the fix (`got hits=[]`). No proto change; no `evidence_kind`. **Type: JUDGMENT.** Decisions log: `docs/audit-log/661-search-scf-anchors-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/661-global-search-index-scf-anchors-controls.md`.

- **feat(demoseed): slice 671 — the demo seed now drives the real evaluator, so a seeded tenant shows real control state instead of "—" (closes 671).** A fully-seeded demo tenant showed **every** control with STATE / FRESHNESS / LAST-OBSERVED = "—", metrics at 0, and an empty "Framework posture", even though 200 evidence records existed (UI-audit ATLAS-019; cascades 022/020/037-posture). **Root cause (by design, not an evaluator bug):** `internal/demoseed` writes the evidence ledger via direct `BYPASSRLS` INSERTs and deliberately does NOT write the `control_evaluations` / `evidence_freshness` read models (seeder.go:31) — those are downstream artifacts of the slice-012/016 evaluator, which in production is driven off the evidence-ingest JetStream stream plus the hourly recompute. The seed bypasses that stream, so nothing ever evaluated the seeded evidence. **Fix:** a shared `demoseed.EvaluateSeededTenant(ctx, appPool, tenantID)` helper drives the REAL evaluator as a SEPARATE stage AFTER `Seeder.Apply` commits — it builds a per-tenant RLS-bound `Engine` via `eval.NewEngineFactory(appPool)`, runs `EvaluateAll(TriggerManual, FarFuture)`, then `freshness.Store.Refresh` — scoped to the seeded tenant via `app.current_tenant` on the app-role pool. Both seed call sites adopt it: the `atlas-cli demo seed` command (new `--app-database-url` flag / `DATABASE_URL_APP` env) and the admindemo "Generate demo data" HTTP handler (new `SetAppPool(s.dbPool)` wiring); it runs on the idempotent re-seed path too. **Invariant #2 held:** evaluation never runs inside the seed's `BYPASSRLS` write tx and never fabricates eval-table rows — it reads the append-only ledger and writes only the read models, so demo state == production semantics. **Invariant #6 held:** evaluation runs as the app role under RLS, scoped to the one seeded tenant. **Proof:** an integration test (`internal/demoseed/evaluate_integration_test.go`) reproduces the symptom (0 `control_evaluations` / 0 `evidence_freshness` / all controls "—" immediately after `Apply`) and asserts the fix (one evaluation row per active control, controls with in-window evidence resolve to concrete pass/fail, idempotent across a second pass); it fails decisively without the fix. No migration, no proto, no `evidence_kind` change. **AC-3 spillover (posture):** "Framework posture" needs the demo controls anchored to real SCF anchors plus the framework's STRM requirement edges — independent of evaluation — and is filed as slice 682. **Type: JUDGMENT.** Decisions log: `docs/audit-log/671-demo-seed-trigger-evaluation-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/671-demo-seed-trigger-evaluation.md`.

- **fix(auth): admin actor-resolution now strips the `user:` JWT-subject prefix, so adding a user to a tenant (and create-tenant / super-admin / authz-bundle audit tagging) works under the real auth path.** The auth substrate mints the atlas JWT `Subject` as `"user:<uuid>"` (`internal/api/auth/http.go`, the PKCE and device-grant token endpoints), and `jwtmw.Middleware` bridges that same value into the credential's `UserID`. Four admin handlers (`adminusers` assign, `admintenants` create, `adminsuperadmins`, `adminauthzbundle`) resolved the actor by calling `uuid.Parse(claims.Subject)` on the **raw** prefixed string — which fails, yielding `uuid.Nil`. The user-visible symptom: the **self-assign** path ("Add me to a tenant") returned `500 "actor user_id not on context"` (the bootstrap `admin@example.com` adding itself to the demo tenant); the other three handlers had the same latent defect for their audit-log actor tagging. Only `admindemo` stripped the prefix (and documented the convention). **Why it shipped:** the admin integration tests minted a **bare-UUID** subject (`Subject: actorID.String()`), so `uuid.Parse` succeeded in tests and masked the real-auth divergence (the slice-198/201 local-vs-real-auth-path class). **Fix:** a shared `jwtmw.SubjectUserID(s)` helper strips the `"user:"` prefix (a bare UUID survives unchanged — safe for the legacy credential and test subjects); all four `actorFromContext` functions route both the `claims.Subject` and the credential-`UserID` parse through it. **Proof:** a pure-Go regression test (`internal/api/adminusers/helpers_test.go` `TestActorFromContext_StripsUserPrefix`) pins that the real `"user:<uuid>"` subject, the bare-UUID legacy subject, and the prefixed credential fallback all resolve to the user id, and that a genuinely-absent actor still resolves to `uuid.Nil`. No schema change, no migration.

- **fix(evidence): slice 633 — persist faithful `observed_at` so the ledger verify walk validates production records (closes 633, fixes 474).** `TestEvidenceVerify_ProductionRecordValidates_474` failed **deterministically** on `main`: a record pushed through the real ingest `Process` did not verify against its own stored ingest hash, breaking constitutional invariant #2 (tamper-evidence / point-in-time replay) and wedging the merge queue (it blocks the required `Go · integration (shard A)` + roll-up `Go · integration (Postgres RLS)` checks). **Root cause (confirmed by byte-diff):** the content-hash (`canonjson.HashRecord`) covers the FULL `EvidenceRecord` proto, whose `observed_at` is a **nanosecond**-precision `google.protobuf.Timestamp`; the ledger persisted `observed_at` only in a `TIMESTAMPTZ` column, which is **microsecond** precision and truncates sub-microsecond nanoseconds. The verify walk reconstructed the truncated timestamp and recomputed a divergent hash for any record whose `observed_at` carried sub-us nanos — the CI-Linux case. Slice 474 fixed the same divergence class for the wire `scope` (it added `scope_canonical`) but missed `observed_at`, and its local run passed because macOS `time.Now()` is often microsecond-aligned (sub-us = 0) while CI-Linux carries full nanosecond resolution — so 474 merged with the shard-A leg RED. **Fix (mirrors 474):** an additive, nullable `observed_at_nanos BIGINT` column persists `rec.GetObservedAt().AsTime().UnixNano()` losslessly; `RecordFromLedgerRow` reconstructs `observed_at` from it when present and **falls back** to the lossy `observed_at` TIMESTAMPTZ column for legacy (pre-633, NULL) rows — preserving the slice-464/474 baseline for old rows. The client-facing receipt-hash contract (`EvidenceReceipt.hash`, reproduced by clients from the nanosecond wire proto) is **unchanged** — only persistence and reconstruction changed, not `HashRecord`. **Proof:** a new pure-Go AC-3 round-trip guard (`internal/evidence/ingest/verify_roundtrip_test.go`, no DB) pins `HashRecord(reconstruct(persist(rec))) == HashRecord(rec)` for a table of records (single/multi-dim/unsorted scope, sub-microsecond `observed_at` regression case, microsecond-aligned, numeric payload, nil payload) and proves a legacy NULL row reconstructs stably; the integration test now passes (AC-1) with a new AC-2 assertion that corrupting `observed_at_nanos` out-of-band is detected. Migration: `migrations/sql/20260608070000_evidence_observed_at_nanos.sql` (+ `.down.sql`); `sqlc` regenerated (v1.31.1). **Type: JUDGMENT.** Decisions log: `docs/audit-log/633-ingest-verify-hash-roundtrip-fix-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/633-fix-474-ingest-verify-hash-roundtrip-divergence.md`.

- **fix(deploy): slice 473 — self-host stack now applies migrations on EVERY bring-up, fail-closed, so a Watchtower binary update can never serve against a stale schema.** Confirmed production incident (2026-06-05): the maintainer's atlas-edge box silently fell 3 migrations behind after an image update — Watchtower auto-updates the `atlas[-edge]` BINARY (it carries the watchtower-enable label) but NOT the `atlas-bootstrap[-edge]` one-shot (no label, `restart:"no"`), and the backend `depends_on` it with `condition: service_started` rather than `service_completed_successfully`. The newer binary ran against the older schema; the demo-seed button then failed with a **masked HTTP 500** (the `me_audit_log_action_check` CHECK rejected the `demo_seed` action value the un-applied `20260525000000_demo_seed_button_meta_audit` migration would have added), surfacing hours later as "action failed" rather than at deploy time. **Fix:** the migrate step is split out of the first-boot bootstrap into a dedicated **always-run** `atlas-migrate[-edge]` service (`deploy/docker/bootstrap/migrate.sh`) that applies any `migrations/sql/*.sql` not yet in the `schema_migrations` ledger — as `atlas_migrate` (`BYPASSRLS`, no superuser, no down-migrations), one transaction per file — on every `docker compose up`. It is **idempotent** (an already-current DB applies nothing and logs `schema current`; no re-seed) and **fail-closed** (a failing migration exits non-zero naming the file, and the `atlas[-edge]` backend — now gated on `atlas-migrate[-edge]` via `service_completed_successfully` — does NOT serve a partial schema). `atlas-migrate[-edge]` carries the watchtower-enable label so it advances in lockstep with the binary; `atlas-bootstrap[-edge]` keeps `service_started` to preserve the slice-065 deadlock avoidance (its control-upload phase blocks on atlas `/health`) and now seeds only a fully-migrated schema. Applied to BOTH `docker-compose.yml` (bundled `build:`) and `docker-compose.edge.yml` (pulled `:edge`). **Proof (AC-7):** a new `migrate` mode in `deploy/docker/test-self-host-bundle.sh` (CI self-host-bundle matrix leg, mirroring slice 470's `proxy` leg) reproduces the incident — bring up at set N, write a new sentinel migration, re-run `atlas-migrate`, assert the migration applied (ledger N→N+1), the force-recreated backend serves a CURRENT schema (the gate held), and a second run is an idempotent no-reseed `schema current` no-op; run green end-to-end. `docs/SELF_HOSTING.md` + the upgrade runbook (`docs-site/docs/upgrade.md`) document the migrate-on-upgrade behavior + fail-closed contract and reconcile the slice-464 migrate-command drift (the binary has no `migrate` subcommand). **Type: JUDGMENT.** Decisions log: `docs/audit-log/473-migrate-on-upgrade-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/473-migrate-on-upgrade-self-host.md`.

- **fix(eval): slice 495 — control-as-code `sql` and `jsonpath` evidence queries now actually evaluate (were silently ignored).** The control-bundle format accepts three evidence-query languages (`rego | sql | jsonpath`) at upload and persists them, but the evaluation engine only ran `rego` — `internal/eval/engine.go` filtered to `q.Language == "rego"` and silently dropped `sql`/`jsonpath`. A control authored entirely in SQL or JSON-path uploaded successfully and then evaluated to **no state** (the worst failure mode for a GRC tool: a control that looks configured but does nothing). **Fix:** the engine now dispatches EVERY declared evidence query and rolls the per-query results up through the existing precedence (any `fail` → `fail`; else any `pass` → `pass`; else `inconclusive`; else `na`). **SQL** runs read-only and evidence-only — against a single tenant-scoped `evidence` CTE materialised from the in-window record set (NOT a raw DB handle), inside a `READ ONLY` transaction with an empty `search_path` and a `statement_timeout`; a static guard rejects multi-statement input, any write/DDL/DML keyword, and any schema-qualified reference other than the `evidence` CTE — so the query cannot reach another tenant's evidence or any non-evidence table (constitutional invariants #2 read-only evaluation + #6 RLS tenant isolation). **JSON-path** runs in-process (no DB reach) against each in-window record's JSONB payload under a per-query timeout. A query whose language is unsupported or unparseable now **fails loudly** (inconclusive + surfaced error), never the prior silent no-op. **Proof:** integration tests prove a `sql`-only and a `jsonpath`-only control each evaluate to a real pass/fail state (was: no state), a malicious SQL query attempting to read `evidence_records` / `users` / another tenant **errors** (load-bearing cross-tenant test), a SQL query exceeding `statement_timeout` returns `inconclusive` rather than hanging, and a mixed rego+jsonpath control rolls up consistently; pure-Go unit tests cover the JSON-path classification, the SQL static guard, and the mixed-language rollup. `internal/eval` merged coverage 79.2% (floor ratcheted 73 → 77). Adds `github.com/PaesslerAG/jsonpath`. **Type: JUDGMENT.** Decisions log: `docs/audit-log/495-control-as-code-sql-jsonpath-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/495-control-as-code-sql-jsonpath-evidence-queries.md`. Closes the slice-009/012 language gap.

- **fix(walkthrough): slice 477 — the walkthrough PDF export endpoint now degrades DETERMINISTICALLY to 503 under a slow/contended/absent headless chrome, never a 500 or a hung request (fans out the slice-475 fix to the third chrome renderer).** Slice 475 fixed this class of defect for the board + questionnaire PDF endpoints but was scoped explicitly to those two packages, leaving a THIRD chromedp renderer untouched: `internal/audit/walkthrough/export.go` (`RenderPDF` + its own `PDFTimeout = 45s`), served by `internal/api/walkthroughs/handlers.go`'s `Export` handler. It carried the identical latent defect — a render that exceeded the deadline returned a wrapped `context.DeadlineExceeded` that did NOT match `walkthrough.ErrChromeUnavailable`, so it fell through to `httperr.WriteInternal` = **500** (the walkthrough export endpoint is exercised less under CI load than the board/questionnaire ones, so it had not yet flaked, but the defect class is the same). **Fix:** `walkthrough.RenderPDF` now routes through the SAME shared `internal/pdfrender.Limiter` slice 475 built (`pdfrender.Default().Do`) — exactly as `board.RenderPDF` / `questionnaire.RenderPDF` do — so the concurrency cap stays a true **total** across all three chrome renderers (slice 475 decision D1) rather than letting walkthroughs spawn chrome outside the cap; the chromedp body was extracted verbatim into `renderWalkthroughBytes`. The handler's redundant `context.WithTimeout(r.Context(), PDFTimeout)` wrapper is removed (the limiter owns the bounded, env-tunable deadline); `walkthrough.PDFTimeout` is kept as a deprecated alias for `pdfrender.DefaultRenderTimeout`. A new `internal/api/walkthroughs/pdf_degradation.go` maps all three modes (`walkthrough.ErrChromeUnavailable` / `pdfrender.ErrRenderDeadline` / `pdfrender.ErrQueueSaturated`) to **503** with a WARN distinguishing `chrome_absent` / `render_deadline_exceeded` / `render_queue_saturated`; a genuine bug still 500s. **No change to walkthrough PDF content/templates**, the `chromedp` dependency, or the slice 340/341 `WSURLReadTimeout` fix. **Proof:** new HTTP-level integration tests in `internal/api/walkthroughs` (the package's first test file) are deterministic 200-or-503 — `TestPDF_RenderDeadlineDegradesTo503` (1ns render deadline → 503, the load-bearing proof that the deadline-exceeded path no longer 500s), `TestPDF_QueueSaturationDegradesTo503` (1-slot fail-fast → 503), `TestPDF_StressNoNonGraceful` (12× under a tight cap, every response graceful), plus the baseline `TestPDF_ReturnsPDFOrServiceUnavailable` — all green with `-race` against a freshly-migrated Postgres. The new tagged package is enrolled in the integration shard manifest (`scripts/integration-shards.txt`, leg B2); ci.yml is untouched. **Type: JUDGMENT.** Decisions log: `docs/audit-log/477-walkthrough-pdf-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/477-walkthrough-pdf-render-degradation.md`. Parent: slice 475.

- **fix(admindemo): demo re-seed into an already-populated tenant now returns a 409 with a clear "Demo data has already been loaded — tear down first to re-seed" message instead of a masked 500 "internal error".** The seeder's populated-tenant / unmarked-tenant refusals (`internal/demoseed/seeder.go`) are now typed sentinels (`demoseed.ErrTenantPopulated`, `demoseed.ErrTenantUnmarked`); the `POST /v1/admin/demo/seed` handler `errors.Is`-matches them and maps to `http.StatusConflict` with an operator-facing message rather than wrapping every `Apply` failure in `httperr.WriteInternal` (500). Surfaced live on a self-host edge deployment: after the demo seed succeeded, a second Seed click showed a scary "internal error" when the real cause was the benign slice-205 "already populated" guard. Genuine internal faults still map to 500. Pure-Go test `TestDemoSeedRefusalMessage` covers the wrapped-sentinel, bare-sentinel, and fall-through cases; existing demoseed/admindemo integration assertions (substring-based) unaffected.
- **fix(pdf): slice 475 — the board + questionnaire PDF endpoints now degrade DETERMINISTICALLY to 503 under a slow/contended/absent headless chrome, never a 500 or a hung request.** The PDF endpoints render via headless chromium (chromedp). Under sustained CI load the render previously neither completed (200) within its deadline nor degraded cleanly to 503: a deadline-exceeded chromedp error fell through the handler's `ErrChromeUnavailable` check and surfaced as a **500** (the handler's `httperr.WriteInternal` default branch) or, worse, a request that hung past the client deadline. The board integration test `TestPDF_ReturnsPDFOrServiceUnavailable` and the questionnaires twin `TestExportPDF_SmokeTest` (both written to accept 200-or-503 and fail on anything else) flaked at ~30.1s across batches 193/195/196 and then failed two consecutive reruns blocking batch 197. **Fix:** a new process-wide governor `internal/pdfrender.Limiter` now fronts BOTH renderers (`board.RenderPDF` / `board.RenderPackPDF` / `questionnaire.RenderPDF` route through `pdfrender.Default().Do`). It (1) bounds the render with a tunable deadline raised from 30s → **90s** default (env `ATLAS_PDF_RENDER_TIMEOUT`) so a healthy render completes with generous headroom while still bounding a hang (AC-2); (2) caps **total** concurrent chrome renders at **2** (env `ATLAS_PDF_RENDER_MAX_CONCURRENCY`) across both packages — chrome is one OS-level resource, so the limiter is shared, not per-package — with a bounded **15s** wait (env `ATLAS_PDF_RENDER_QUEUE_WAIT`) before an over-cap caller degrades (AC-3); (3) classifies a deadline by the RENDER CONTEXT (not by sniffing chromedp's error text) into `ErrRenderDeadline`, and a saturated queue into `ErrQueueSaturated`. The handlers map `ErrChromeUnavailable` / `ErrRenderDeadline` / `ErrQueueSaturated` ALL to **503** (the documented graceful path) and emit a WARN distinguishing `chrome_absent` / `render_deadline_exceeded` / `render_queue_saturated` (AC-5); a genuine bug still 500s. **No change to PDF content/templates**, the `chromedp` dependency, or the slice 340/341 `WSURLReadTimeout` connection-setup fix (this is complementary RENDER-TIME degradation) (AC-6). **Proof:** both integration tests are now deterministic 200-or-503 (no `t.Skip` on 503) and gain a render-deadline→503 test (1ns render deadline → 503, proving the deadline-exceeded path no longer 500s), a queue-saturation→503 test, and an N× stress variant under simulated contention (slice-340 pattern) — all green 5× with `-race`; the `pdfrender.Limiter` unit suite (deterministic deadline / saturation / concurrency-cap-honored / parent-cancel / fail-fast / env-parsing) is green 20× with `-race`. **Type: JUDGMENT.** Decisions log: `docs/audit-log/475-pdf-render-degradation-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/475-pdf-render-degradation.md`.

- **fix(demoseed): slice 463 — `Seeder.Teardown` now removes the tenant-scoped fallback `frameworks` + `framework_versions` rows `Apply` creates, making Teardown the true inverse of Seed.** Slice 462 surfaced that `demoseed.Seeder.Teardown` was not the exact inverse of `Apply` — it swept the six primitives and their child/audit tables but left rows behind. Root-cause (proven by a full per-table round-trip snapshot, not the `tenants`-row hypothesis the parent recorded): `Apply` writes a tenant-scoped fallback `frameworks` + `framework_versions` pair **only when the global SCF catalog (`tenant_id IS NULL`) is absent** (`writers.go` `writeAuditPeriodsAndSamples`, the catalog-less fallback so `audit_periods` has a valid `framework_version_id` to reference), but the teardown delete list omitted both tables — so on every teardown against a catalog-less DB (the integration DB, and any install without the bundled SCF catalog) one `frameworks` + one `framework_versions` row orphaned under the torn-down tenant. The `tenants` row itself was already correctly deleted on current `main` (the parent's observation predated/conflated the framework orphan). **Fix (production code, `internal/demoseed/seeder.go`):** added `DELETE FROM framework_versions WHERE tenant_id = $1` then `DELETE FROM frameworks WHERE tenant_id = $1` to the teardown sweep, placed after `framework_scopes` and before `vendor_scope_cells`. **FK ordering (AC-3):** `framework_scopes` + `audit_periods` (both `ON DELETE RESTRICT` into `framework_versions`) are already swept earlier in the loop; `frameworks.latest_version_id` is `ON DELETE SET NULL`, so deleting `framework_versions` before `frameworks` violates no constraint. **RLS / tenancy (AC-4 / canvas invariant #6):** both new deletes are keyed `WHERE tenant_id = $1` (a non-null demo tenant UUID); the global SCF catalog rows have `tenant_id IS NULL` and can never match — the catalog is safe by construction, asserted explicitly by the new test. **Resolved open question (Seed-creates-vs-adopts):** the tenant is _created_ by Seed → _deleted_ by Teardown (unchanged, already correct); the framework rows are _adopt-when-present, create-the-fallback-when-absent_ → Teardown deletes only the tenant-scoped fallback, never the adopted global catalog. `super_admin_audit_log` is deliberately left (platform-global, no `tenant_id`; threat-model R — the platform record that a demo seed/teardown happened persists). **Proof (integration, real Postgres):** new `TestSeedTeardown_RoundTrip` snapshots every seeded tenant-scoped table before/after and asserts all return to zero (RED before the fix: `frameworks`/`framework_versions` each leak 1 row; GREEN after) and that the global catalog count is unchanged; new `TestSeedTeardown_Idempotent` proves a second teardown + a never-seeded-slug teardown both error benignly (not-found) and resurrect no state (AC-2). Slice 462's admindemo suite passes unchanged (AC-5). Merged `internal/demoseed` coverage 83.1% → 83.33% (still ≥ floor 81; ~0.2pp drift is noise, floor left unchanged per the ratchet rule). **Type: JUDGMENT.** Decisions log: `docs/audit-log/463-demoseed-teardown-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/463-demoseed-teardown-leaves-tenant-row.md`. Parent: slice 462 (grandparent: slice 461).

- **fix(config): slice 460 — close the docker `.env.example`-vs-server-read env-var gap (`NATS_URL` exemplar + the compose-plumbed-but-untemplated set).** A self-host operator copying `deploy/docker/.env.example` to `.env` got an authoritative template that silently omitted several variables the bundle actually honors. A complete audit (every `os.Getenv`/`os.LookupEnv` the `atlas` binary + its imported `internal/` packages read, cross-checked against what `docker-compose.yml` plumbs to the `atlas` service) found the slice spec's premise — "`NATS_URL` is the only one" — too narrow: six keys were already overridable in compose via `${VAR...}` yet absent from the template. **Added to `.env.example`:** `ATLAS_OAUTH_TOKEN_RATE_PER_MIN` (active, default `600`) and the standard OTel knobs `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL` / `OTEL_SERVICE_NAME` / `OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES` / `OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER` / `OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER_ARG` (commented opt-ins, matching the existing OTEL block). **`NATS_URL` (the slice exemplar):** promoted as a commented external-NATS override; compose now reads `${NATS_URL:-nats://nats:4222}`, which renders **byte-identically** to the prior hardcoded value when unset (verified via `docker compose config`) — zero behavior change for the shipped bundle, plus a clarifying comment at the compose `NATS_URL:` line. **Inclusion boundary (the JUDGMENT call):** "does compose plumb the key to the `atlas` service?" — a server-read key the bundle does NOT plumb (`TRUST_FORWARDED_HEADERS`, the audit-sink trio, the OSCAL-signing set, `ATLAS_EXPORT_MAX_CONCURRENT_PER_USER`, `ATLAS_ENABLE_DEMO_SEED`, `CHROME_DEBUG_URL`) would mislead an operator if templated in the docker `.env`, so it is documented in the decisions log instead of half-wired; the dev-only cadence knobs (`ATLAS_*_INTERVAL`, annotated "for dev loops" in `main.go`) and the fixed in-container wiring (`ATLAS_GRPC_ADDR`, `ATLAS_ISSUER_URL`, `ATLAS_DATA_DIR`, …) stay correctly absent. The slice-430 config-reference drift guard (`just config-reference-drift-check`) gains the six new rows on `docs-site/docs/configuration.md` (body table rows only — not the mkdocs nav that sibling slice 432 owns) and is green (35 vars: 26 active + 9 opt-in). No dead template entries found. **Type: JUDGMENT.** Decisions log: `docs/audit-log/460-nats-url-env-example-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/460-nats-url-env-var-not-in-env-example.md`. Surfaced by slice 430.

- **fix(infra): slice 462 — admindemo integration suite self-corrects a leaked `demo` tenant instead of false-redding on a dirty-DB re-run.** The `internal/api/admindemo` integration suite uses a fixed `demo`-slug tenant for the seed/teardown happy path; `TestSeed_EnvUnsetGets503AndDoesNotSeed` (and the non-admin path) assert `!demoTenantExists("demo")` as a correctness invariant (env unset / non-admin ⇒ no seed ⇒ no demo tenant). The cleanup helper previously registered ONLY a deferred `t.Cleanup` removal, so when a prior run left a `demo` tenant behind — either because a different package `t.Fatal`'d and killed the `go test` process before admindemo's cleanups ran (the abort scenario), or because `demoseed.Seeder.Teardown` itself left the `tenants` row even on a passing run (observed this slice) — the next run saw the stale tenant and false-redded at `handler_integration_test.go:261` ("demo tenant exists after 503"), blaming the env-unset path for a row it never created. CI never hit it (fresh DB per run + curated package order), but a local re-run against a non-fresh DB did. **Root-cause fix (mirrors slice 461's "setup self-corrects, does not assume" philosophy, for the `tenants` table rather than `scf_anchors`):** a new test-only helper `removeDemoTenant(t, slug)` removes the demo tenant best-effort and is now called SYNCHRONOUSLY at the top of each demo-dependent test (establishing a clean precondition regardless of prior aborted/leaky state) AND retained as the deferred `t.Cleanup` (so this run's tenant does not leak forward even on a failed assertion). The removal first tries `demoseed.Seeder.Teardown` (the clean path for a fully-seeded, forensically-marked leftover) then runs an unconditional FK-ordered raw sweep + `DELETE FROM tenants` keyed on id — the catch-all for a bare/partial leftover that `Seeder.Teardown` refuses (it declines to delete a tenant lacking the slice-205 forensic mark) or cannot fully clean. **Proof:** with a leaked `demo` tenant inserted, the pre-fix test FAILS at line 261 and the post-fix test passes; three consecutive `-p 1` runs against the same DB without cleaning between them are all green. The env-unset assertion's intent is unchanged — it still requires 503 + no seed; only the precondition is now established rather than assumed. Test-harness only; no production code, no schema, no handler slug change. **Spillover surfaced:** `demoseed.Seeder.Teardown` can leave the `tenants` row behind even on a passing run — recorded in the decisions log as a potential PRODUCT teardown bug for a focused follow-up, NOT fixed here. **Type: JUDGMENT.** Decisions log: `docs/audit-log/462-admindemo-tenant-leak-cleanup-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/462-admindemo-suite-leaks-demo-tenant-on-abort.md`. Parent: slice 461.

- **fix(infra): slice 461 — integration suite no longer order-coupled on shared SCF-catalog seed state.** The Go integration suite (`go test -tags=integration -p 1 ./internal/...`) passed only because CI's `tests-integration` package list in `.github/workflows/ci.yml` is hand-ordered so the full SCF import runs before any package that partially mutates `scf_anchors`. Run over a broad wildcard (`./internal/api/...`, alphabetical) the coupling surfaced: a package that imported a partial "new release" catalog (or scoped-DELETEd anchors) left the CURRENT SCF framework version missing `GOV-01`, the lazy-seed guards (`if anchorCount == 0`) counted the leftover rows as "already seeded" and skipped their reseed, and the next package's SOC 2 crosswalk import failed with `scf_anchor "GOV-01" not found`. **Root-cause fix:** a new test-only helper `internal/api/scfseed` (`EnsureFullCatalog` / `EnsureSCFCatalog`) replaces the row-count guards with a **completeness probe** — it asks the exact query the SOC 2 importer uses ("does the sentinel anchor `GOV-01` resolve in the CURRENT SCF version?") and does a full clean reseed when the catalog is absent OR partial, making each consumer self-correcting regardless of invocation order. Consumers converted: `anchors`, `ucfcoverage`, `soc2import` (the last via the SCF-only variant, since it imports the crosswalk under test). The seed wipes also `TRUNCATE controls CASCADE` first so a leftover `controls.scf_anchor_id` (`ON DELETE RESTRICT`) FK from an earlier alphabetical package cannot block the anchor reseed. **Guard against silent regression:** a new CI step (`scripts/check-integration-seed-order-independence.sh`) re-runs the catalog-sensitive packages in a deliberately NON-curated order against a freshly-truncated catalog and asserts green, so a reintroduced order-coupled seed fails CI even though the curated-order run passes. **Proof:** `go test -tags=integration -p 1 ./internal/api/...` is green on a pristine DB in full alphabetical order (57 packages, exit 0). **Spillover (slice 462):** the `internal/api/admindemo` suite leaves a `demo` tenant when an earlier test in a shared DB aborts, a separate self-cleanup fragility unrelated to the seed coupling — filed, not fixed here. **Type: JUDGMENT.** Decisions log: `docs/audit-log/461-integration-seed-order-coupling-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/461-integration-suite-seed-order-coupling.md`.

### Performance

- **perf: slice 381 — perf cleanup round 1 (closes the slice 332 LOW-finding bundle, 6 sub-findings).** Bundles six mechanically-verifiable Low/Informational performance findings from the slice 332 audit, each landing as its own commit so a reviewer can revert one without losing the others (P0-1). **F-ING-2** (`internal/evidence/ingest/ingest.go`): the per-reject best-effort `writeAudit` tx ran on an unbounded `context.WithoutCancel` detached context; wrapped it in a 3-second `context.WithTimeout` (matching the slice-188 best-effort audit pattern) so a reject storm against a saturated pool fails the audit write fast rather than stacking connect-attempts — the verdict is already reached, so a timed-out audit write drops silently; slice-015 invariant order (size-check → schema-validate → redact → hash → write) untouched. **F-UCF-2** (`internal/api/ucfcoverage/`): split the 932-LOC `handlers.go` per-endpoint into `requirement_coverage.go`, `anchor_requirements.go`, and `control_coverage.go`, leaving the package-shared scaffolding (Handler, constructors, RegisterRoutes, lookup/tx helpers, wire types, JSON helpers) in `handlers.go`; move-and-rename only, zero semantic change. **F-OAUTH-2** (`internal/auth/tokensign`): `Sign` constructed a fresh `jose.NewSigner` (~235ns / 10 allocs) on every call; now memoised per active KeyID via `sync.Map` (P0-3: no new caching framework), keyed on KeyID so the slice-366 rotation overlap window caches each active key independently; a `Reset()` method empties the cache as the invalidation hook a future `keystore.Rotate` caller invokes. `BenchmarkSignerCachedVsUncached` isolates the acquisition step: cached 8.9 ns/op (0 allocs) vs uncached 235 ns/op (10 allocs), well under the AC-7 < 50% bar — the full-Sign total cannot meet that literal bar because the ES256 P-256 scalar multiplication (~20µs) dominates and is identical on both paths (decisions log D2). **F-OAUTH-3** (`internal/auth/keystore/fsstore`): `Get` did a per-call `make+copy` of the `[]VerificationKey` — an allocation on every JWT-bearing request since `Verify` hits `Get`; replaced the RWMutex-guarded signing/verify fields with an immutable snapshot published via `sync/atomic.Pointer`, so `Get` is now a lock-free pointer load returning the shared, never-mutated verify slice directly; mutators (load/generate/Rotate/Prune) serialise on a dedicated `loadMu` and swap a fresh snapshot atomically; slice-366 rotation/overlap/prune contract preserved, `-race` clean on the Rotate-during-Verify test. **F-OTEL-2**: new operator runbook `docs/operator/observability-tuning.md` documents the `OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER=parentbased_traceidratio` + `OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER_ARG=0.1` recipe under a "High DB query rate" header (documentation-only, P0-4 — sampler default unchanged), cross-linked from `docs/SELF_HOSTING.md`; the runbook notes that the shipped default is already the ratio sampler (the audit's "parentbased_always_on" premise was a stale reading of the slice 121 trail — decisions log D3). **F-ING-3** (`internal/canonjson`): added `BenchmarkHashRecordAtMaxPayload` + `TestHashRecord_AtMaxPayloadUnderBudget` locking the 1 MiB-payload SHA-256 cost as a regression gate, so an accidental O(payload²) re-canonicalisation or per-byte allocation blowup trips CI rather than silently eroding the 50ms ingest ack SLO. The test is a coarse **pathological-regression** gate — median-of-9 (warmup discarded) against a generous 100ms ceiling that sits 14-125× above any realistic CI sample (~1-7ms) yet far below any superlinear pathology (hundreds of ms to seconds), so it never flakes on a noisy shared runner; the `testing.B` benchmark remains the precise-number artifact (~441µs/op, 14 allocs). **Type: JUDGMENT** — decisions log `docs/audit-log/381-perf-cleanup-round-1-decisions.md` (D1 F-UCF-2 do-now split per AC-3 over the audit's defer-until-4th-endpoint framing; D2 AC-7 benchmark reframed to acquisition-isolation since the literal full-Sign < 50% bar is physically unreachable; D3 F-OTEL-2 corrects the audit's stale default-sampler premise; D4 F-OAUTH-3 snapshot model over per-call copy; D5 F-ING-3 pathological-regression ceiling + median-of-9 measurement after an initial 5ms single-sample ceiling flaked on CI). Slice doc: `docs/issues/381-perf-cleanup-round-1.md`.

- **perf(web): slice 380 — dashboard Server Component fan-out + parallel data fetch (closes slice 332 F-BFF-2 MEDIUM).** The `/dashboard` page's six panels (framework-posture, top-risks, freshness, drift, upcoming, activity) each fetched client-side via TanStack Query `useQuery` on cold load, paying their own bearer-cookie-read + Next.js BFF hop + upstream HTTP — a 6-7× per-load request amplification (slice 332 surface 5, finding F-BFF-2). A new sibling server-component `web/app/(authed)/dashboard/layout.tsx` now reads the `atlas_jwt` cookie once server-side and fans out all six panels' upstream fetches in parallel via `Promise.all` (`prefetchDashboard` in `web/app/(authed)/dashboard/dashboard-prefetch.ts`), seeding a per-request `QueryClient` under the EXACT keys the page's `useQuery` hooks register, then ships the dehydrated cache in the initial HTML via `<HydrationBoundary>`. The page's `useQuery` hooks boot already-populated, so a cold first load fires ZERO `/api/dashboard/**` BFF requests (AC-4/AC-8) — the user pays one network round-trip (the HTML document) instead of N. This generalizes slice 249's single-query `/settings` prefetch precedent to a six-query fan-out. The per-panel BFF routes REMAIN as the client-side refresh surface (P0-1, unremoved); their wire shape is unchanged (P0-4); the bearer is read server-side and never reaches the client (P0-3); `web/lib/api.ts apiFetch` now sets `cache: "no-store"` so the SSR fan-out never caches a tenant's posture across requests (P0-2). Prefetch fails soft per panel (`setQueryData` only on success — never seeds an error state, so a slow/failing upstream leaves that one panel unseeded and the client `useQuery` re-fetches it cold rather than hydrating into an error). New vitest `web/app/(authed)/dashboard/dashboard-prefetch.test.ts` (11 cases) pins query-key parity with the page, bearer-forwarding (P0-3), fail-soft fan-out (D3), and missing-bearer no-op; new Playwright `web/e2e/dashboard-server-component.spec.ts` asserts zero `/api/dashboard/*` requests on first load, full-panel content render, and that the BFF refresh surface still serves a 200 with the unchanged envelope. Per-file vitest coverage floor added for the new module (98/98/98/98). **Constitutional invariants honored**: tenant isolation unchanged — the platform derives tenant from the bearer credential server-side; the SC path uses the same bearer-cookie + upstream fetch shape as the BFF routes. **Type: JUDGMENT** — decisions log `docs/audit-log/380-dashboard-server-component-fanout-decisions.md` (D1 slice-249 prefetch pattern over pure-RSC; D2 per-panel TanStack state satisfies AC-2 Suspense intent; D3 fail-soft via `setQueryData`-on-success; D4 no explicit `Cache-Control` header — `cache:"no-store"` on the fetch suffices). Slice doc: `docs/issues/380-dashboard-server-component-fanout.md`.

- **perf(ingest): slice 379 — single marshal on redaction path (closes slice 332 F-ING-1 MEDIUM).** `internal/evidence/ingest/ingest.go Service.Process` now routes the marshal-of-record on the redaction-bearing code path through a new private `marshalLedger func(proto.Message) ([]byte, error)` field on `Service`, defaulted in `New()` to `protojson.Marshal`. The pre-redact `protojson.Marshal(rec.GetPayload())` call STAYS in place (its bytes serve size-check and schema-validation, per slice 015 D2 invariant order: size-check → schema-validate → redact → hash → write) but is now explicitly documented as a discardable throwaway when redaction rules apply. The post-redact marshal — the ONE marshal whose bytes are retained for the JSONB ledger column AND feed the canonjson hash — is the only call routed through the seam. New unit test `TestProcess_RedactionPath_LedgerMarshalCountIsOne` swaps in a counting wrapper and asserts the seam fires exactly once on the redaction-bearing path (AC-1); `BenchmarkIngestRedactionPath` asserts `ledgerCalls.Load() == int64(b.N)` as a CI canary (AC-8). Companion guard `TestProcess_NoRedactionPath_LedgerMarshalCountIsZero` confirms the no-rules path is unchanged (single marshal — already optimal — and the seam never fires when no rules apply). `TestProcess_RedactionApplied_BytesAreRedacted` asserts the ledger-bound bytes contain `<<REDACTED>>` AND do NOT contain the original secret value (P0-2 anti-criterion guard). One-line defensive nil-pool guard added to `writeAudit` so the same-package marshal-count tests can short-circuit at the seam without spinning up a real `pgxpool.Pool` (production `New()` panics on nil pool — unreachable in production). **Constitutional invariants honored**: invariant #2 (ingest/eval separation — refactor stays wholly inside `internal/evidence/ingest`; the redactor's proto-in/proto-out API at `internal/evidence/redact/redact.go` is untouched, P0-1); slice 015 D2 invariant order preserved verbatim (P0-4); P0-2 unredacted-bytes-never-logged-on-error-path preserved (the post-redact marshal error surfaces "payload re-marshal after redact" with no payload content). **Out-of-scope (intentional)**: F-ING-2 audit-write tx fan-out (bundled into slice 381); F-ING-3 canonjson CPU (informational only); a future slice can convert the redactor to in-place mutation if a structural CPU win on the redaction path is needed — slice 379's seam makes that future collapse mechanical (drop the pre-redact marshal, AC-1 assertion remains valid). **AC-3 slice-doc typo flagged**: AC-3 ("schema-validation hook receives the redacted payload bytes") directly contradicts P0-4 (canonical slice 015 D2 order). Resolution per slice prompt: preserve P0-4, treat AC-3 as a typo, recommend post-merge slice-doc patch. See decisions log D2 for full reasoning. Decisions log: `docs/audit-log/379-ingest-redaction-single-marshal-decisions.md` (D1 marshal-count interpretation; D2 AC-3 typo resolution; D3 same-package test seam placement; D4 sentinel-error short-circuit + nil-pool guard; D5 benchmark shape; D6 no-rules-path guard branch; D7 defensive byte-copy; D8 no interface widening; D9 explicit non-touches; D10 STATUS row left to orchestrator). Slice doc: `docs/issues/379-ingest-redaction-single-marshal.md`.

- **feat(authz): slice 378 — hot-reload authz bundle without server restart (closes slice 332 F-OPA-2 HIGH).** `internal/authz/decision.go` now stores the prepared OPA query behind a `sync/atomic.Pointer[rego.PreparedEvalQuery]` and exposes `(*authz.Engine).Reload(ctx, modules, sources, validator)` + `ReloadFromEmbedded(ctx, validator)` so the embedded `policies/authz/*.rego` bundle can be reloaded without restarting the atlas binary. The atomic-pointer contract is the entire load-bearing piece: a concurrent `Decide()` call during a `Reload()` sees EITHER the old query OR the new one — never a partial state. The pre-swap matrix gate is the second load-bearing piece: a NEW production-code file `internal/authz/matrix_validator.go` exports `CanonicalMatrix()` (33-row role × endpoint table mirroring the slice 026 integration test) and `ValidateMatrix(ctx, candidate)` that evaluates every cell against the CANDIDATE prepared query BEFORE `storeQuery`. Matrix-failure aborts the reload — the engine continues to serve the prior bundle (slice 378 AC-3). New maintainer-only HTTP endpoint `POST /v1/admin/authz-bundle/reload` wired at `internal/api/adminauthzbundle/`: super_admin-gated (`jwtmw.FromContext().SuperAdmin` is the first check; the OPA `super_admin.rego` is the upstream second leg), per-actor rate-limited at 1 reload per 60s, audit-log-dual-write on success (one `super_admin_audit_log` row + one `me_audit_log` row inside a single transaction; both carry `{before_bundle_sha256, after_bundle_sha256}` in payload). Migration `20260528000000_authz_bundle_reload_meta_audit.sql` extends the CHECK constraints on `me_audit_log.action` AND `super_admin_audit_log.action` to admit `'authz_bundle_reload'` (strict superset of the slice-278 baseline). Coverage: race test `TestReload_RaceConcurrentDecideAndReload` (16 deciders × 4 reloaders × 250ms under `-race`; zero Decide errors required); `TestReload_ValidatorRunsAgainstNewQuery` asserts the matrix runs against the CANDIDATE not the live query (slice doc note 2 — common-bug-shape defence); `TestReload_ValidatorFailureRejected` asserts the engine continues to serve the prior bundle on validator failure; `TestReload_ValidateMatrixOnEmbeddedBundlePasses` asserts the production validator passes against the embedded bundle (canonical-bundle drift detector); handler unit tests cover 403 / 503 / 422 / 429 paths; integration tests cover happy-path dual-write + non-super_admin 403 + matrix-failure 422 + rate-limit 429. **Constitutional invariants honored:** invariant #6 (RLS tenant isolation — me_audit_log row anchored to the actor's session tenant; super_admin_audit_log is platform-global per slice 142 D1); append-only audit ledger (canvas §4.3 — the migration adds an action value, never alters existing rows); AI-assist boundary unchanged (this is a runtime substrate change; no AI-assist surface touched). **Out-of-scope (intentional):** v3+ per-tenant custom-control bundles (canvas §4.4 — slice 378 P0-4); regocache touches (slice 377's domain — P0-378-5); distributed rate-limit backend (v2 atlas-edge multi-binary follow-up). **Engineer-as-collaborator gap surface:** §D10 (matrix-vs-test lockstep) + §D4 (target_user_id contract bend on platform-global event — future v2 schema cleanup) + §D5 (distributed limiter for v2 atlas-edge) + §D4 (audit-write failure recovery via background retry) consolidate the substrate gaps for maintainer attention. **Slice 332 F-OPA-2 closure path:** the v1 "restart-required" deferral is now retired; v2 atlas-edge multi-tenant rollout is de-risked. Decisions log: `docs/audit-log/378-authz-bundle-hot-reload-decisions.md` (D1 empty-body embedded-only request shape; D2 atomic.Pointer over RWMutex; D3 production-code matrix validator vs test-only refactor; D4 dual-write audit-log emission with target_user_id contract bend honestly named; D5 per-actor in-process rate limiter; D6 sorted-filename NUL-separated SHA-256 fingerprint; D7 separate modules + sources parameters for Reload; D8 new `adminauthzbundle` package vs extending `adminsuperadmins`; D9 explicit non-touches; D10 matrix-against-candidate-not-live contract). Slice doc: `docs/issues/378-authz-bundle-hot-reload.md`.

- **perf(eval): slice 377 — cache `rego.PreparedEvalQuery` in eval engine (closes slice 332 F-OPA-1 CRITICAL).** New `internal/eval/regocache` package generalises the prepare-once pattern already proven at `internal/authz/decision.go:60` and applies it to the two hot-path call sites that the slice 332 performance audit identified as re-preparing per call: `internal/eval/rego.go evalRegoQuery` (evidence-query sandbox; called once per `(control × scope_cell)` inside `EvaluateAll`) and `internal/risk/aggrule/severity.go evalCustomRego` (custom_rego severity policies). Cache key = SHA-256(policy text) ⨁ SHA-256(sorted capability builtin names) ⨁ query ⨁ moduleName; sync.Map-backed (per slice doc note 1); package-level instance per call site (D4); OTel `atlas_eval_regocache_hits_total` / `atlas_eval_regocache_misses_total` counters emit lazily via slice 121's global MeterProvider. **Measured delta** (`Apple M3 Ultra darwin/arm64`, `-benchtime=2s`): `evalRegoQuery` 225,498 ns/op → **31,951 ns/op** (**7.06× faster**); allocations 4,207/op → 273/op (**15.4× reduction**); bytes 200,168 B/op → 21,349 B/op (**9.4× reduction**). At v1 scale (200 active controls × ~3 scope cells per control = ~600 calls per `EvaluateAll` tick): **pure-CPU savings ~116 ms per scheduled-eval tick (86% reduction from ~135 ms → ~19 ms)**. **Out-of-scope (intentional):** `internal/authz/decision.go` left untouched (P0-377-4 — already the reference pattern); LRU eviction deferred per D1 (v1 policy population bounded by control catalog ~200 entries, fits in process memory); per-meter-name labelling deferred per D4 footnote (operator dashboards can already filter on otel_scope_name). **Constitutional invariants honored:** invariant #2 (separated ingest/eval stages — cache lives wholly in the eval stage); invariant #6 (tenant isolation — cache key is policy-text-derived NOT tenant-derived; two tenants authoring identical policy text share the cached compiled AST which holds no eval result, by construction no cross-tenant data leak); AI-assist boundary (cache is structural — does not change OPA evaluation semantics, P0-377-1). Decisions log: `docs/audit-log/377-rego-prepared-query-cache-decisions.md` (D1 unbounded sync.Map vs LRU; D2 risk/aggrule in-scope; D3 OTel counter shape; D4 per-package vs shared cache instance; D5 measured benchmark numbers). Slice doc: `docs/issues/377-eval-rego-prepared-query-cache.md`.

### Documentation

- **docs(self-host): slice 549 — "pull the compose, not just the image" upgrade-discipline runbook (migration-drift prevention).** Documents the recurring real edge-box trap behind two confirmed masked-500 incidents (2026-06-05 demo-seed; 2026-06-07 user↔tenant assign): Watchtower advances container **images** but never re-pulls the compose **file**, so a box still on a pre-slice-473 compose has no always-run `atlas-migrate[-edge]` service and silently drifts its schema; the next code path that needs the un-applied column/constraint/table fails with a masked HTTP 500. Adds an explicit **"Upgrading: pull the compose, not just the image"** subsection to `docs/SELF_HOSTING.md` (and a consistent one to `docs/operations/edge-deploy.md`) that states the rule (`git pull` + `docker compose ... up -d`, not Watchtower-images-only), names the **symptom** (a `..._action_check` CHECK violation `SQLSTATE 23514`, or a missing relation/column `42P01`/`42703`, shortly after an image bump → migration drift), and gives the **recovery**: preferred = pull the compose so the now-present `atlas-migrate` service applies the gap; stop-gap = take a [slice-432 backup checkpoint](https://mgoodric.github.io/security-atlas/backup-restore/) **first**, then apply pending `migrations/sql/*.sql` in filename order via `psql` and record each in the `schema_migrations` ledger (`filename`, `applied_at`) so it is not later double-applied. Amends — does not contradict — the existing "Migrations apply automatically on every bring-up" copy by naming its precondition (a slice-473-or-later compose). Cross-links the [slice-473 migrate mechanism](https://github.com/mgoodric/security-atlas/blob/main/docs/issues/473-migrate-on-upgrade-self-host.md) and the slice-432 backup runbook; rewrites neither. Documentation-only: no compose, migrate-service, Watchtower, or code change. The two edited files live under repo-root `docs/` (GitHub-rendered), not the mkdocs nav, so `mkdocs build --strict` for the published site is unaffected and still passes. **Type: AFK.** Slice doc: `docs/issues/549-edge-upgrade-pull-compose-runbook.md`. Filed from a live operator incident; parent precondition slice 473, cross-links slice 432 — both merged.
- **docs: slice 431 — operator guide for connecting an external IdP (OIDC).** Adds `docs-site/docs/oidc-setup.md`, a published guide that walks an operator through connecting Okta, Entra ID (Azure AD), or Keycloak to a running deployment, plus a nav entry in `docs-site/mkdocs.yml`. The guide is grounded in the **shipped** admin SSO surface: per-tenant DB-backed `oidc_idp_configs` (`issuer_url`, `client_id`, `client_secret_enc`, `redirect_url`, `allowed_email_domains`) set via the `/admin/sso` web UI and `PATCH /v1/admin/sso`, validated with `POST /v1/admin/sso/preflight` — **not** environment variables (there is no `OIDC_ISSUER_URL` env var; that path does not exist and is explicitly corrected). Security guidance is load-bearing (the project's slice 086/161 open-redirect history): the redirect URI is documented as **exact-match, own-origin, fixed at `/auth/oidc/callback`** with an explicit warning against wildcards/foreign hosts, and the client secret is documented as **encrypted at rest, entered via the admin surface, never committed to `.env`/compose/repo**; `allowed_email_domains` is recommended as the principal restriction. Local mode stays available (OIDC is additive). Multi-IdP federation, SCIM, and group-to-role mapping are noted as out of scope. Every endpoint, request field, and column was verified against `internal/api/adminsso/handler.go`, `internal/auth/oidc/oidc.go`, and the `oidc_idp_configs` schema; all example issuer URLs, client IDs, secrets, and redirect URIs are placeholders. Documentation-only: no `internal/`, `web/`, or CI changes. `mkdocs build --strict` passes. Cross-links the [install guide](https://github.com/mgoodric/security-atlas/blob/main/docs-site/docs/install.md) and [ADR-0003](https://github.com/mgoodric/security-atlas/blob/main/docs/adr/0003-oauth-authorization-server.md). **Type: JUDGMENT.** Slice doc: `docs/issues/431-external-idp-oidc-setup-guide.md`. Depends on slices 034/062 (admin SSO surface), 187-192 (auth substrate), 365 (OIDC nonce) — all merged.

- **docs: slice 429 — README parity for the AWS connector and the Go/TypeScript SDKs.** Adds the three missing flagship-surface READMEs so the highest-traffic OSS extension points stop being the ones with no orientation. (1) `connectors/aws/README.md` — the AWS connector was the only connector without a README despite being slice 004, the canonical worked example the connector-authoring guide links to; documents the single emitted kind `aws.s3.bucket_encryption_state.v1` (push profile), a **least-privilege read-only IAM role assumed via STS** (`sts:GetCallerIdentity`, `s3:ListAllMyBuckets`, `s3:GetBucketLocation`, `s3:GetEncryptionConfiguration`, optional `organizations:ListTagsForResource` — no write/delete/admin action, no `AdministratorAccess`), the `register`/`run` subcommands + flags, scope minimums (`cloud_account`, `environment`), the hour-windowed idempotency key, and a P0 anti-criteria block mirroring okta's "banned admin roles" discipline. (2) `pkg/sdk-go/README.md` — a push-record quickstart for the Go push SDK (construct `NewClient`, build an `EvidenceRecord`, `Push`, handle the `EvidenceReceipt`), verified to compile against the current `pkg/sdk-go` API. (3) `sdk/typescript/README.md` — documents the **shipped** `@security-atlas/sdk` surface (the `OAuthClient` `client_credentials` helper) and shows presenting the acquired bearer to the canonical `POST /v1/evidence:push` surface; the typed TS `Push` wrapper is honestly noted as not-yet-shipped (deferred to a follow-on slice) rather than invented. Documentation-only: no connector or SDK code changed. Every documented kind/subcommand/flag/symbol/IAM action was verified against the shipped source; all identifiers, endpoints, and tokens are placeholders (no real AWS account id, ARN, access key, platform URL, or bearer). **Type: JUDGMENT.** Slice doc: `docs/issues/429-aws-connector-go-ts-sdk-readmes.md`. Depends on slice 003 + slice 004 (both merged).
- **docs: slice 427 — auditor-facing "verify a signed OSCAL export" page in the docs site.** Publishes `docs-site/docs/audit/verify-export.md` so an external auditor handed a signed OSCAL export bundle can confirm it is authentic and untampered with stock tooling — no atlas internals. The auditor-verification flow already existed but only in the operator runbook (`docs/runbooks/oscal-signing.md`), which is not in the docs-site nav and so never reached the published site; this surfaces the auditor-facing subset. The page (auditor voice, second person, offline + read-only) covers: (1) reading `manifest.json` `signature.mode` to pick the path; (2) the `cosign-kms` path with stock `cosign verify-blob` against an operator-published public-key file, including a stock-tool recipe (`jq` + `LC_ALL=C sort` + `sha256sum` + `xxd`) to recompute the deterministic bundle digest (sha256 over sorted `filename:memberhash` lines) and the raw-32-byte-digest blob cosign reads on stdin; (3) the `atlas oscal verify <bundle-dir>` convenience path for either mode; (4) what verification proves vs. does not (tamper-detection + key-holder authenticity, but no transparency-log timestamp — the keyless/Fulcio/Rekor mode is slice 414, not shipped, and an admonition says so); (5) key-trust guidance — trust an out-of-band public key, never one embedded in the bundle. Adds a **For auditors** nav section in `docs-site/mkdocs.yml` (label chosen to disambiguate from the existing "Audit logs" entry), cross-links from the OSCAL SSP-export walkthrough at the hand-to-auditor handoff, and links back to the operator runbook + ADR-0010 for the configuration side without duplicating it. **All documented commands were hand-run against real signed bundles** (AC-11): a `cosign-kms` and an `embedded-ed25519` bundle were produced via a throwaway harness driving the production signers, the page's digest-recompute matched the manifest, `cosign verify-blob` returned `Verified OK`, a tampered member made both the digest diverge and cosign reject the signature, and `atlas oscal verify` returned `OK … (mode=embedded-ed25519)`; the harness was removed before commit (docs-only — no change under `internal/oscal/`, `cmd/atlas-cli/`, or the manifest format). `mkdocs build --strict` passes with the page wired into nav and all internal links resolving. **Type: JUDGMENT** — decisions log `docs/audit-log/427-auditor-verify-export-docs-decisions.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/427-auditor-verify-export-docs-page.md`. Depends on slice 413 + ADR-0010 (both merged).

- **docs(adr): slice 400 — OSCAL cosign/Sigstore signing decision spike + ADR (368 go/no-go).** Decision-only spike (no production code, slice 400 P0-400-1) gating slice 368's ~5d cosign-migration build. Filed [`docs/adr/0010-oscal-cosign-signing.md`](docs/adr/0010-oscal-cosign-signing.md) (next sequential ADR slot; 0003 double-occupancy untouched). **Recommendation: ADOPT-DEFERRED, confidence HIGH.** Adopt cosign as the committed direction for signing OSCAL audit-export bundles (distinct from slice 084's already-cosign-signed release binaries / container images — runtime-export signing is a different artifact + a different identity), and re-scope slice 368 into two phased sub-slices: **368a (Phase 1, ~3d)** — `internal/oscal/cosign` wrapper + `cosign-kms` mode + retained `embedded-ed25519` + mode dispatch + backward-compat + CLI + runbook + Mode-A/B integration tests; **no Fulcio/Rekor/OIDC dependency**, closes the bulk of the named auditor friction (ad-hoc embedded key + bespoke verifier) with stock `cosign verify-blob`; and **368b (Phase 2, ~2d, gated)** — `cosign-keyless` + Fulcio + Rekor transparency log, blocked on a separate OIDC-identity-strategy decision. **Load-bearing finding:** `cosign-keyless` via atlas's own AS (slice 188 `client_credentials`) does NOT work against public Fulcio as 368 proposed — slice 188 mints a per-deployment, non-public issuer that is not in Fulcio's federated trust root; keyless needs a public IdP, a private Sigstore stack, or Sigstore-root onboarding first. **cosign license finding:** cosign is Apache-2.0; bundling the unmodified binary is license-clean (permits binary redistribution; compatible with both candidate project licenses; preserve `LICENSE`+`NOTICE`, pin the version, record provenance in asset-inventory §2.3) — P0-368-4 satisfied. **Per-deployment defaults:** air-gap self-host → `embedded-ed25519` (only reachable mode; non-negotiable); connected self-host with KMS → `cosign-kms`; SaaS → `cosign-kms` at GA (revises slice 368 AC-9's `cosign-keyless` default), keyless after Phase 2. Slice 368 re-scoped (`docs/issues/368-cosign-signing-migration.md`): status `ready` → `blocked` on ADR-0010 sign-off, "Re-scoped by ADR-0010" section added with the day-by-day → 368a/368b mapping, AC-8 marked done (recorded in ADR-0010), AC-9 revised. **Does NOT auto-merge** (slice 400 P0-400-2) — the PR is the maintainer's decision gate. ADR at `docs/adr/0010-oscal-cosign-signing.md`; re-scoped parent at `docs/issues/368-cosign-signing-migration.md`.

- **docs(audit): slice 332 — performance audit (evidence pipeline + UCF + OPA + OAuth + frontend + observability) via voltagent-qa-sec:performance-engineer persona.** Read-only audit of `main@eec5a2d0` across six load-bearing performance surfaces: (1) evidence ingest pipeline (`internal/evidence/ingest` + slice-015 NATS JetStream substrate), (2) UCF graph traversal (`internal/api/ucfcoverage` two-hop JOIN, baselined against slice 008's 5.89 ms mean / 5.88 ms p50 / 6.91 ms p95 — 34× under the 200 ms target), (3) OPA evaluation (`internal/eval/rego.go` evidence-query sandbox + `internal/authz/decision.go` request-time authz), (4) OAuth substrate (`internal/api/oauth` token endpoint + `internal/auth/tokensign` ES256 sign/verify), (5) frontend BFF route handlers (`web/app/api/*/route.ts` + `web/lib/api/bff.ts`), (6) observability stack overhead (`internal/observability/otel` SDK + `otelpgx`). **Methodology: static-analysis + benchmark-test reading + decisions-log citation** — live load generation deferred per slice's "Notes for the implementing agent" explicit authorization (D4 captures the JUDGMENT). **Headline finding:** **F-OPA-1 (CRITICAL)** — `internal/eval/rego.go evalRegoQuery` calls `rego.New(...).PrepareForEval(ctx)` on every `computeRow` invocation; with 200 active controls × modest cell fan-out, the eval engine pays 200+ OPA compiles per scheduled-eval tick. The correct pattern (prepare-once + store `rego.PreparedEvalQuery`) already exists in the same codebase at `internal/authz/decision.go:60`. A second site of the same anti-pattern lives at `internal/risk/aggrule/severity.go:134`. **Findings:** 1 Critical · 1 High · 3 Medium · 6 Low · 4 Informational = 15 total across the 6 surfaces. **Spillover follow-ups filed (cap 5):** **377** (F-OPA-1 — cache `rego.PreparedEvalQuery` per policy text in eval engine + risk aggrule), **378** (F-OPA-2 — hot-reload authz bundle without server restart; HIGH escalates from "v1 deferred" to "must-fix-before-v2"), **379** (F-ING-1 — eliminate double `protojson.Marshal` on ingest redaction path; operator-visible at sustained >100 RPS on redaction-bearing kinds), **380** (F-BFF-2 — dashboard Server Component fan-out + parallel data fetch; collapses 7+ BFF round-trips per page-load into 1), **381** (perf cleanup round 1 — bundles 6 Low/Info findings: F-ING-2 reject-path audit tx timeout, F-UCF-2 handlers.go split, F-OAUTH-2 cached `jose.Signer`, F-OAUTH-3 immutable verification-key handle, F-OTEL-2 sampler-tuning operator runbook, F-ING-3 canonjson hash CPU benchmark regression gate). F-BFF-3 (`web/lib/api.ts` 2901 LOC) is NOT a new finding — already covered by slice 370. **Surfaces flagged healthy:** UCF graph traversal (slice 008 baseline preserved by unchanged schema + index shape; 5.89 ms mean / 6.91 ms p95 still holds at the benchmark fixture scale); ingest core path (Service.Process boundary holds constitutional invariant #3); OAuth ES256 sign/verify path (go-jose performance acceptable); OTel no-op default (genuine zero-cost when `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT` unset per slice 121 AC-2). **Severity rubric applied (D2):** Critical = >2× regression OR operator-visible on v1-binary-criterion hot path; High = 1.5–2× OR structural pattern that will become Critical at v2 scale; Medium = noticeable inefficiency at >100 RPS sustained; Low = codebase-onboarding observation; Informational = healthy steady-state or design-intent. **Regression-vs-baseline threshold (D3):** literal application of slice doc's >2× rule, ENRICHED with the in-codebase-correct-pattern criterion (F-OPA-1 has no published baseline but is structurally wrong vs. authz Engine; severity CRITICAL notwithstanding the absence of a fabricated benchmark number). **Spillover cap-5 triage (D5):** Critical / High → own slice always; Medium → own slice if isolated; Low → bundle into "perf cleanup round 1"; Informational → audit-report-only; final count 4 individual + 1 bundle = 5, cap honored. **Constitutional invariants honored** — invariant #2 (separated ingest/eval) verified; invariant #6 (RLS tenant isolation) unchanged; invariant #3 (append-only ledger) verified by F-OAUTH-3 + F-OPA-1 remediation paths not crossing the ledger boundary. **Adjacent observations** (NOT slice 332 findings; named in PR body only for orchestrator visibility): (a) `internal/eval/engine.go loadEvidence` is unbounded in record count per control — v2 re-shape; (b) slice 371 just landed clock injection across auth substrate — extending to eval engine would let slice 377's prepared-query cache be tested deterministically. **P0 anti-criteria honored:** P0-332-1 NO load against atlas-edge or production (static-analysis path only); P0-332-2 NO auto-merge; P0-332-3 NO code modified (audit diff = doc files only, decisions log D10 verifies); P0-332-4 NO PII / customer screenshots; P0-332-5 NO touches to CLAUDE.md or canvas; P0-332-6 NO new perf-testing framework dependency; P0-332-7 NO production load tests. **Engineer-as-collaborator gap surface:** report's "Adjacent observations" section + decisions log §D6 consolidate the items that fell outside slice 332's scope but deserve maintainer attention. **Slice doc status flipped `ready` → `in-review`.** Audit report at `docs/audits/332-performance-audit-report.md`; decisions log at `docs/audit-log/332-performance-audit-decisions.md`; spillover slice docs at `docs/issues/377-eval-rego-prepared-query-cache.md`, `docs/issues/378-authz-bundle-hot-reload.md`, `docs/issues/379-ingest-redaction-single-marshal.md`, `docs/issues/380-dashboard-server-component-fanout.md`, `docs/issues/381-perf-cleanup-round-1.md`.

- **docs(governance): slice 376 — project asset inventory (closes slice 329 H-5; COMPLETES the slice 329 governance chain).** Filed `docs/governance/asset-inventory.md` — the project's standing answer to the first question every third-party security reviewer asks before examining technical control evidence: **"list all assets in scope."** Closes the slice 329 compliance meta-audit finding **H-5** (severity High) for SOC 2 CC3.2 (Risk assessment — identifies and analyzes risk) + ISO 27001 5.9 (Inventory of information and other associated assets) + NIST CSF ID.AM-1 / ID.AM-2 (Asset Management) organizational controls. **This is the FIFTH and FINAL document in the slice 329 governance-doc chain** — the H-1 through H-5 organizational-control gap pattern that the audit identified as the load-bearing blocker for the v1 binary success criterion is now fully closed (372 IR plan → 373 BCP/DR plan → 374 access-review cadence → 375 data-retention policy → **376 asset inventory**). The document covers all nine required sections: (1) **purpose and scope** — what "asset" means under operational-consequence cut-line (anything whose loss/compromise requires documented restore path per BCP §6, revocation procedure per IR §7, rotation event per data-retention §4, or access-review entry per access-review §2); in-scope = project-side (the `mgoodric/security-atlas` repo + maintainer-operated single-host Unraid SaaS instance + every third-party-service vendor account + cryptographic material custody surface + governance corpus); explicit out-of-scope items (per-customer SaaS instance assets — operator's inventory; tenant data inside the maintainer-SaaS instance — canvas invariant #6 RLS substrate; maintainer's personal IT — workstation/password manager/hardware token model/network architecture per P0-376-2; secret values verbatim per P0-376-1); engineer-as-collaborator scope note — Unraid IP (192.168.1.246 in orchestrator brief) is omitted from published inventory per slice 329 D10 no-exploit-roadmap-detail boundary (the asset is fully inventoried by operational role, IP held privately by maintainer), (2) **six asset categories** with per-category Examples + Criticality floor + Sensitivity rationale: §2.1 Code repositories (monorepo + maintainer-local mirror + tagged releases + contributor clones — all Tier 1 because distributed-by-design ensures recovery substrate redundancy), §2.2 Container images (per-binary tags + edge rolling tag — all Tier 2-3 because reproducible from tagged commits), §2.3 Cryptographic material (OAuth AS JWT keys + cosign signing key planned + GitHub PATs + deploy keys + webhook secrets + OIDC RP client secret + maintainer GPG signing key + CI secret store + `security@` mailbox — Tier 1 for signing keys / OAuth client secret / admin PATs; Tier 2 for read-only / narrow-scoped credentials), §2.4 Deploy infrastructure (maintainer-operated SaaS instance Unraid host + Postgres state + evidence object storage + observability stack + GitHub repo hosting + container registry + GitHub Pages docs site + maintainer-local Git mirror — Tier 1 for SaaS-instance data tier + GitHub repo hosting; Tier 2 for observability + CI runners), §2.5 Third-party integrations (GitHub Actions + GitHub-native Security tab + Codecov + GitGuardian + dependabot + CodeQL + StepSecurity Harden-Runner + release-please App + OpenID Connect IdP — mostly Tier 2 vendor-recovered with one Tier 1 exception: the IdP), §2.6 Documentation surfaces (repo-root community-health files + ADRs + canvas + governance corpus + audit reports + audit-log + slice docs + CHANGELOG + incident logs + access-review evidence + docs site — mostly Tier 2 with LICENSE as Tier 1 exception), (3) **per-asset detail table** (the document's center of gravity) with six sub-tables — one per category — totaling ~50 asset rows with **Asset name / Category / Owner / Criticality tier / Location identifier / Backup status / Access surface / Lifecycle status** columns; per-row cross-references to BCP §5 backup posture, access-review plan §2 access surfaces, and data-retention §3 retention; specific identifiers withheld per P0-376-1 — names + types + owners + criticality, not values + paths + exploitation specifics; engineer-as-collaborator-explicit IdP-vendor-held-privately gap note in the §3.5 OpenID Connect IdP row, (4) **three-tier criticality rubric** — Tier 1 project-stopping (loss/compromise breaks operations or constitutional invariants); Tier 2 significant degradation (loss = significant degradation but recoverable); Tier 3 inconvenience (replaceable, vendor-recovered); explicit binding to IR plan severity (Tier 1 → P0 candidate; Tier 2 → P1; Tier 3 → P2/P3), BCP plan RTO/RPO tiers (Tier 1 → BCP Tier 0/3; Tier 2 → BCP Tier 1/2/4; Tier 3 → ephemeral), and access-review cadence (Tier 1 → quarterly where applicable; Tier 2 → semi-annual or annual; Tier 3 → annual or N/A); rationale for three-tier-not-finer (matches IR severity + BCP RTO/RPO shape without proliferating categorization decisions); **Tier 1 asset count: 19 across all six categories** (4 code repos + 7 cryptographic material + 6 deploy infrastructure + 1 third-party integration IdP + 1 documentation surface LICENSE + 0 container images per-binary reproducible-from-tags), (5) **annual review cadence at 2027-05-28 co-scheduled with sibling 372 + 373 + 374 + 375 tabletops** + **six trigger-based out-of-band review categories**: new third-party integration added, new key custodianship taken on, new infrastructure component added, asset retired, new GitHub App installed (cross-references access-review plan §7.2), material change in criticality; out-of-band reviews produce PR with inventory update + one-line document-history entry rather than separate per-review artifact (differs from access-review plan's per-review artifact discipline because inventory updates are simpler — the diff is the audit evidence); slip provision matches access-review plan §3 (late review documented not hidden; two consecutive slips trigger GOVERNANCE.md bus-factor escalation), (6) **explicit per-sibling cross-references in §6** — §6.1 IR plan §2 severity tiers bound to inventory criticality tiers + §7 per-tier playbooks operate on specific inventory assets; §6.2 **BCP plan §4 working-table conversion documented (D1)** — the BCP plan §4 working-asset-inventory table was REMOVED and replaced with single cross-reference to canonical inventory per slice 373's pre-commitment ("When 376 lands, this section's table is replaced by a single cross-reference"); reciprocal cross-reference added; the 15-row BCP §4 working table is a strict subset of the canonical inventory and was converted to honor the load-bearing pre-commitment; §6.3 access-review plan §2 access surfaces overlap with §3.3 cryptographic material + §3.4 deploy infrastructure here — two documents take different cuts at the same surface (access-review plan = periodic check on WHO CAN USE; this inventory = catalog of WHAT EXISTS); §6.4 data-retention plan §2 seven data categories map to §3 six asset categories at varying levels with explicit per-category alignment, (7) **solo-maintainer honesty** — every asset's Owner column resolves to maintainer (no separate asset custodian / no records-management function / no infrastructure-operations team); same pattern as IR plan §3 / BCP §3 / access-review §5 / data-retention §7 with no second-pair-of-eyes / no formal escalation path / bus-factor extended-absence handled via GOVERNANCE.md sealed-envelope; recursive property named honestly (the inventory itself is one of the things being inventoried — a Tier 2 documentation-surface asset; loss is infeasible by design because inherits Tier 0 distributed-by-design persistence);
