- **docs(governance): slice 375 — data retention + disposal policy (closes slice 329 H-4).** Filed `docs/governance/data-retention.md` — the project's standing answer to "what is your data retention policy?" and "how do you dispose of data when it ages out?", closing the SOC 2 C1.2 (Confidentiality — disposal of confidential information) / ISO 27001 8.10 (Information deletion) / GDPR Article 5(1)(e) (storage limitation) organizational-control gap surfaced by slice 329 compliance meta-audit finding **H-4** (severity High). Completes the slice 329 governance-doc chain (372 IR plan → 373 BCP plan → 374 access-review plan → **375 data-retention policy**); only H-5 (asset inventory, slice 376) remains for the full audit-driven governance suite. The document covers all nine required sections: (1) **purpose and scope** — what this policy covers (CI/CD artifacts + source code + governance corpus + audit-trail artifacts + maintainer-operated SaaS instance state + third-party-service state + issue-tracker state); explicit out-of-scope items (tenant data inside operator deployments per canvas invariants #3 + #6 + §4.6.5; GDPR Article 17 per-data-subject erasure tracked through privacy module slice 180; GDPR Article 33 breach notification deferred per OQ #10; platform-product audit-period freezing per canvas §8.4; maintainer's personal-IT retention); five disposal-method enumeration (hard delete; soft delete with tombstone; cryptographic erasure; aging out via rotation; ledger tombstone for append-only-with-supersede), (2) **seven data categories** (§2.1 source code + git history; §2.2 governance corpus; §2.3 audit-trail artifacts; §2.4 CI/CD artifacts; §2.5 maintainer-operated SaaS instance state; §2.6 third-party-service state; §2.7 issue-tracker state) with sensitivity + where-it-lives + canonical examples per category, (3) **retention-table anchored §3 with per-row Category / Retention duration / Disposal method / Framework floor / Enforcement mechanism columns** — the document's center of gravity; key durations: source code indefinite (Apache 2.0 community persistence underwrites), governance corpus indefinite (git history), public audit-trail artifacts indefinite (append-only via git), **unified audit log 7 years intent with §3.1 Gap 1 honestly named** (atlas_audit_log table is unbounded today — no scheduled purge; closing the gap is in §9 hardening), CI/CD GitHub Actions logs **90 days at GitHub default (project does not override)**, tagged release container images indefinite, edge-tag rolling images **30 days rolling via slice 207's `.github/workflows/edge-image-prune.yml`**, scanner finding history vendor-default, maintainer SaaS Postgres state indefinite-while-in-use, **Postgres nightly dumps 30 days rolling per BCP §5 Tier 3**, evidence ledger indefinite-append-only per canvas invariant #3, evidence object storage co-extensive with ledger, observability stack 30 days rolling, **Unraid offsite parity-aware backup 7 days rolling per BCP §5 Tier 3**, **OAuth AS signing keys co-extensive with key validity / §3.1 Gap 2 named: manual rotation today / tracked at slice 366**, third-party-service vendor-controlled, issue-tracker indefinite; **engineer-as-collaborator §3.1 enforcement-gap section** honestly names the two intent-vs-substrate divergences (unified audit log purge mechanism not yet implemented — over-retention is the safe failure mode; OAuth AS rotation manual — slice 366 closure path), (4) **per-disposal-method procedure detail** in §4.1-§4.5 with cross-references to access-review plan §4.1 quarterly procedure step 2 (stale CI secret deletion), IR plan §10 incident log (incident-driven deletions), BCP §5 Tier 3 (offsite lifecycle), and canvas invariant #3 (ledger tombstone constitutional); cryptographic erasure scoped honestly as fallback-not-primary; aging-out audit-trail is the configured substrate setting not per-event log, (5) **§5 audit-trail-of-disposal asymmetry** with what-is-logged table (access-review revocations + incident-driven rotations + yanked releases + retention-configuration changes + audit-log purge events when §3.1 Gap 1 closes + evidence-ledger tombstones + OAuth AS key rotation) vs what-is-not-logged enumeration (routine substrate rotation: GHA log expiry / artifact expiry / edge-image prune / Postgres-dump lifecycle / observability rolling / Unraid offsite); rationale documented (human-decision disposals deserve forensic record; substrate behavior does not generate signal), (6) **§6 legal-hold override** with broad-by-default trigger ("any received subpoena, court order, or written regulatory request that names retained artifacts as relevant to an investigation automatically triggers a legal hold across all categories" — sole-maintainer reality: defaults to safety/preservation without forcing scope interpretation under stress); narrow-scope for security-incident + maintainer-initiated holds (scope named in incident log or dedicated hold-tracking doc); hold-scope explicit (rotation suspended via window-extension; lifecycle deletions suspended via rule-disable; hard deletes suspended; ledger tombstones continue per canvas invariant #3); **release process with 30-day cooling-off period** between triggering condition concluding and disposal resumption (calibrated against litigation-resurfaces pattern); hold tracking at `docs/governance/legal-holds.md` created on first use (matches slice 374 D8 pre-cadence-baseline-honesty pattern — file's absence is explicable); solo-maintainer legal-hold authority named (no separate legal counsel on retainer; ad-hoc outside counsel at maintainer expense when warranted), (7) **§7 solo-maintainer honesty** with three-document sibling-parity pattern (the retention officer is the maintainer; no second-pair-of-eyes review; no formal escalation path); **cross-infrastructure-migration commitment** load-bears on BCP plan §5 Tier 3 + §6 Scenario A operational substrate (the 7-year audit log retention must survive an Unraid chassis swap or future migration); **the accumulation problem** named honestly (indefinite retention for governance corpus + audit-trail artifacts + source code + issue-tracker = monotonically-growing storage footprint, correct for OSS-on-public-Git; re-evaluated at GOVERNANCE.md trigger 2028-05-20 OR 100 deployed self-hosts), (8) **§8 cross-references** — constitutional: canvas invariant #3 (append-only evidence ledger → ledger tombstone disposal method is the compatible disposal posture; hard-deleting evidence records is forbidden), canvas invariant #6 (RLS at DB layer → cross-tenant disposal is scoped by RLS; migration `20260521010000_tenants_rename.sql:195` flags tenant-removal retention as separate slice), **canvas §4.6.5 AI-assist boundary + §4.6.7 board-narrative AI-assist** (schema-level enforcement unchanged; full prompt + full response audit trail for board-narrative drafts unchanged; `prompt_version` + `model_name` + `model_version` + `model_provider` columns unchanged; snapshot-at-generation indefinite retention unchanged — per D6, this slice acknowledges and cross-references constitutional commitments without modifying them); operational: slice 327 audit verified-positive controls (encryption at rest enables cryptographic-erasure disposal posture; sha256-per-record integrity in evidence ledger); sibling slice 372 IR plan §6 + §10 (incident logs); sibling slice 373 BCP §5 + §11 (backup retention windows reproduced in §3); sibling slice 374 access-review plan §6 + §4 (per-review artifacts inheriting audit-trail-artifact category retention; CHANGELOG discipline composed); slice 329 finding H-4 binding, (9) **§9 maintenance** — annual review co-scheduled with sibling 372 + 373 + 374 tabletops at 2027-05-28; cadence-reducing changes (e.g., 7-year → 3-year on unified audit log) require an ADR (asymmetric: reducing retention is the easier compliance violation to make accidentally); ISO 27001 5.36 monitoring-of-policies closed at capability level; **seven named hardening items "not committed today"**: unified audit log purge mechanism (closes §3.1 Gap 1), OAuth AS key rotation automation (tracked at slice 366, closes §3.1 Gap 2), legal-hold tracking document (created on first use), retention-configuration drift detection at annual review, cross-infrastructure-migration retention verification, PostgreSQL WAL archival (also in BCP §11), off-GitHub repository mirror (also in BCP §11). - **docs(governance): slice 374 — GitHub access review cadence (closes slice 329 H-3).** Filed `docs/governance/access-review.md` — the project's standing answer to "do you review access at least annually?", the load-bearing first-question diligence reviewers ask before examining technical controls (slice 329 compliance meta-audit finding **H-3**, severity High; completes the governance-doc chain after slice 372 IR plan and slice 373 BCP/DR plan that closes the SOC 2 CC6.2 + CC6.3 + CC6.5 / ISO 27001 5.18 + 8.2 / NIST CSF PR.AC-1 organizational-access-control gap). The document covers all nine sections required by the work-order: (1) **purpose and scope** — what "access review" means here (periodic maintainer-driven walk-through with continue/revoke/reduce-scope decision per grant); in-scope (repo collaborators + installed Apps + OAuth Apps + CI secrets + ghcr.io tokens + webhooks + deploy keys + PATs + GPG/cosign signing keys); out-of-scope (per-PR review which is its own discipline, operator-hosted deployments, platform tenant access reviews, maintainer personal-IT identity surfaces); **engineer-as-collaborator finding documented inline** — `mgoodric/security-atlas` is owned by an individual GitHub account (verified via `gh api orgs/mgoodric` → 404; `gh api repos/mgoodric/security-atlas/collaborators` → 1 entry login=mgoodric role=admin), NOT a GitHub organization as the work-order title suggested; document honestly addresses the user-vs-org reality and names "Repository-to-organization migration" as a §8 hardening item dependent on GOVERNANCE.md re-evaluation trigger, (2) **inventory of eight access-surface categories** (repository ownership + collaborators; installed GitHub Apps; authorized OAuth Apps; CI secret store with categorical naming of the six secrets per P0-374-6 no-verbatim-secret-names; container registry tokens; webhook receivers + deploy keys; Personal Access Tokens including ADR-0005's BRANCH_PROTECTION_READ_TOKEN; signing keys GPG + cosign-future), (3) **three-tier cadence table with realistic first-review dates**: **Quarterly (90 days)** covering collaborators + CI secrets — first review **2026-08-28**, **Semi-annual (180 days)** covering GitHub Apps + OAuth Apps — first review **2026-11-28**, **Annual (365 days)** covering PATs + webhooks + deploy keys + signing keys + container registry tokens — first review **2027-05-28 co-scheduled with sibling slice 372 IR plan tabletop + slice 373 BCP plan tabletop** so three governance cadence activities happen in a single week; slip provision documented (late review = documented event with rationale, not silent failure); pre-cadence baseline honesty — 2026-08-28 explicitly named as the FIRST formal review under this plan with no retroactive artifacts filed for pre-cadence informal checks, (4) **per-tier review procedure** with concrete copy-paste `gh api` commands — quarterly procedure uses `gh api repos/mgoodric/security-atlas/collaborators --paginate` + `gh secret list --repo mgoodric/security-atlas`; semi-annual procedure uses `gh api repos/mgoodric/security-atlas/installation` + manual `https://github.com/settings/applications` walk; annual procedure uses manual `https://github.com/settings/tokens` walk + `gh api .../hooks` + `gh api .../keys` + GPG/cosign-fingerprint verification; outcomes documented in `docs/governance/access-reviews/YYYY-<period>.md` per template, (5) **solo-maintainer considerations** — the load-bearing "the reviewer is the reviewed" structural condition explicitly named (no separation-of-duties at single-maintainer size) with three honest substitutes documented (public artifacts as transparency-vs-separation-of-duties; documented cadence with visible-slip provision; IR plan post-incident review as retrospective scrutiny); same engineer-as-collaborator honesty pattern as slices 372 + 373; bus-factor extended-absence trigger cross-references GOVERNANCE.md sealed-envelope mechanism and names the first review under new-maintainership as an out-of-band §7 trigger, (6) **audit trail** — `docs/governance/access-reviews/` directory established with naming convention (Quarterly YYYY-Q<N>.md / Semi-annual YYYY-H<1|2>.md / Annual YYYY-annual.md / Trigger-based YYYY-MM-DD-<slug>.md); per-artifact TOML-frontmatter + Markdown template with `review_id`, `review_type`, `scheduled_date`, `performed_date`, `reviewer`, `trigger` fields supporting future automation; CHANGELOG `### Security` discipline (file bullet only on revocations or scope-reductions; empty-result reviews stay in-artifact); public-by-default confidentiality with `[redacted — see private archive]` placeholder per IR plan precedent; IR plan composition — unauthorized grants surfaced by review promote to incidents at minimum P2 severity, (7) **seven trigger-based review categories** for out-of-band reviews: collaborator/contributor departure (artifact within 7 days); GitHub App rotation/replacement (within 7 days); CVE published against installed App or dependency or signing-key surface (composes with IR plan §7.3); suspected credential compromise (artifact within 24 hours regardless of subsequent confirmation/disproof, composes with IR plan §7.2); disclosure-triggered out-of-band review (composes with SECURITY.md); **explicit non-trigger** of "onboarding a new collaborator" (the addition is the access decision itself; next scheduled quarterly review covers it); major branch-protection ruleset change (the PR is the access decision; annual review confirms still warranted), (8) **tooling and automation** — what's automated TODAY is honestly nothing (Dependabot/GitGuardian/CodeQL/govulncheck only ADJACENT to access review as detection controls); **seven automation candidates named as §8 hardening items not committed today**: scheduled-review reminder action, diff-against-previous-review automation, inactive-collaborator surfacing, stale-secret detection, PAT-expiration tracking, repository-to-organization migration, GitHub-native audit-log subscription (last two are governance-decision-dependent); rationale for deferral — "automation amplifies a working manual process; it does not substitute for one" — first three quarterly reviews establish manual baseline; 2027-05-28 annual review is the prioritization decision point, (9) **annual maintenance** of THIS document co-scheduled with the sibling 372 + 373 tabletops at 2027-05-28; cadence-loosening changes require an ADR; ISO 27001 5.36 monitoring-of-policies closed at capability level; canvas invariant #6 (RLS) named as the runtime-tenant-isolation analogue that this document complements at the GitHub-repository organizational boundary. Companion artifacts shipped in the same slice: `docs/governance/access-reviews/.gitkeep` + `docs/governance/access-reviews/README.md` (establishes the per-review evidence directory with naming convention + TOML template cross-reference + first-review-baseline note explaining the directory's emptiness at filing); `docs/governance/README.md` (slice 374 row moved from "Planned" to "Current documents"; remaining 375 + 376 planned rows unchanged); `docs/audit-log/374-github-access-review-cadence-decisions.md` (D1 filename `access-review.md` matching sibling convention not `access-review-cadence.md` per planned-row text; D2 user-owned-not-org reality documented inline with verifiable `gh api` reproducibility; D3 three-tier quarterly/semi-annual/annual cadence shape with surface allocation by privilege-tier and rate-of-change; D4 first-review dates 2026-08-28 / 2026-11-28 / 2027-05-28 calibrated to strict-interval not calendar-boundary; D5 seven automation candidates deferred as named-not-committed hardening items per sibling slice 373 D5 pattern; D6 the-reviewer-is-the-reviewed structural condition with three honest separation-of-duties substitutes documented; D7 length budget ~530 lines within work-order 400-700 target and shorter than sibling slice 373 ~1100 lines naturally because surface is genuinely smaller; D8 pre-cadence baseline honesty — no retroactive artifact filed; D9 seven out-of-band triggers including explicit non-trigger of new-collaborator onboarding). The document covers all eleven sections required by the work-order: (1) purpose and scope — operator-side BCP/DR for the project itself, explicitly out-of-scope items enumerated (operator-hosted deployments, regional natural disasters, customer-side compliance program continuity, 24/7 uptime SLAs), (2) **five-tier RTO/RPO target table** with sole-maintainer honest targets: Tier 0 OSS continuity RTO 24h / RPO 0 (git is distributed), Tier 1 distribution artifacts RTO 7d / RPO N/A (reproducible from tagged commits), Tier 2 docs / communication RTO 24h / RPO 0, **Tier 3 maintainer-operated single-host Unraid SaaS instance RTO 4h best-effort during waking hours / RPO 24h matching pg_dump nightly cadence** — no fiction about 5-minute RTOs or 99.99% SaaS uptime, Tier 4 supporting services + signing keys RTO 48h / RPO N/A (rotation-based), (3) **roles and responsibilities** — five standard BCP roles (BCP Coordinator / Recovery Lead / Comms Lead / Backup Custodian / Key Custodian) with explicit solo-maintainer role devolution mirroring slice 372's IR plan §3 pattern; GOVERNANCE.md advisory-council trigger named as the re-evaluation point; no 24/7 on-call promise; no dedicated DR site, (4) **asset and dependency inventory cross-reference** — BCP working subset of fourteen load-bearing assets with tier / restore priority / restore-procedure pointer / notes; canonical inventory deferred to slice 376, (5) **backup strategy per tier** with what-is-backed-up / how / frequency / retention / encryption / integrity-check / gap-noted columns for every tier; **explicitly named gaps**: PostgreSQL WAL archival not configured (RPO 24h holds without it), off-GitHub repository mirror not committed (Tier 0 RPO 0 holds via maintainer-local mirror, but single-substrate risk acknowledged), JWT signing key rotation not automated (slice 327 audit M-1, tracked at slice 366), (6) **five restore scenarios** each with detect / contain / restore / verify / resume operations sub-steps: **Scenario A** Unraid hardware failure (disk-failure parity-rebuild path + chassis-failure spare-hardware swap path + offsite-restore fallback), **Scenario B** PostgreSQL corruption (24h-window pg_dump restore + point-in-time-recovery gap honestly named since WAL archival not configured), **Scenario C** object storage loss (canvas invariant #3 append-only evidence ledger replay path — full-bucket loss is recoverable because the ledger preserves sha256 + observed_at per evidence record), **Scenario D** ransomware on SaaS instance (composes with IR plan §5.2 containment; no-pay-ransom posture explicit; restore to fresh chassis from offsite-only backup; audit-period freeze integrity assessment per canvas §8.4; slice 327 verified-positive controls re-verified), **Scenario E** GitHub organization compromise (Path 1 account-recoverable composes with IR plan §7.2 auth-compromise playbook; **Path 2 full GitHub-loss recovery via maintainer-local mirror push to replacement hosting surface** — Codeberg/Gitea/GitLab; all-keys rotation; audit-trail review), each verify step cross-references canvas invariant #6 (RLS — tenant_id integrity preservation), (7) **continuity of the OSS project** — operationalizes GOVERNANCE.md bus-factor & succession without modifying GOVERNANCE.md content; Apache-2.0 fork-ability ultimate fallback named; maintainer's 2027-05-20 co-maintainer recruitment commitment reproduced; extended-absence operational sequence (weeks 1-2 CI continues / week 3 trusted-contact issue / months 2-3 sealed-envelope org-transfer or fork-fallback); contributor-during-absence actions (fork, continue running, document publicly), (8) **annual tabletop co-scheduled with IR plan tabletop at 2027-05-28** with rotating two-scenario coverage per year (Year 1 A+B / Year 2 C+E-Path-1 / Year 3 D+E-Path-2 / Year 4+ cycle); chaos experiments 5 (Postgres failover) and 7 (object-storage outage) named as operational test substrate per slice 335 backlog; quarterly audit cycle as passive testing, (9) **communications during continuity events** — narrow channel list (GitHub issue/discussion / CHANGELOG / SECURITY-INCIDENTS.md for reputation-affecting events / GitHub Security Advisory for P0-P1 / security@ email / direct-operator-outreach for ransomware-or-key-compromise only); **public status page explicitly deferred** per IR plan §6 pattern — operating a status page requires hosting outside the failing infrastructure (Statuspage.io or equivalent dependency); no social-media announcements; no phone/in-person comms; tone discipline cross-references slice 337 board-narrative anti-pattern list, (10) **documentation and audit trail** — continuity-event logs share IR plan's `docs/incidents/YYYY-MM-DD-<slug>.md` public-by-default directory + IR plan §10 template; CHANGELOG `### Fixed` or `### Security` per event character; `[redacted — see private archive]` placeholder convention for attack-vector detail per IR plan precedent, (11) **maintenance** — annual review co-scheduled with IR plan tabletop; output is PR + annual-review note at `docs/audit-log/business-continuity-review-YYYY.md`; ISO 27001 5.36 monitoring-of-policies closed at capability level; **seven named hardening items "not committed today"**: off-GitHub repository mirror / PostgreSQL WAL archival / JWT signing key rotation automation (tracked at slice 366) / cosign image signing (tracked at slice 368) / public status page / operator mailing list / spare hardware verification (verified at every annual review starting 2027-05-28). Companion artifacts shipped in the same slice: `docs/governance/README.md` (governance directory index updated — slice 373 row moved from "Planned" to "Current documents"; remaining planned governance documents 374/375/376 untouched), `docs/audit-log/373-business-continuity-plan-decisions.md` (D1 GOVERNANCE.md disposition — exists; cross-reference without modification per P0-373-3; D2 five-tier RTO/RPO shape with solo-maintainer honest Tier-3 4h-waking-hours / 24h-RPO targets per P0-373-1; D3 exactly five restore scenarios A-E with detect-contain-restore-verify-resume substructure covering hardware × Postgres × object-storage × ransomware × GitHub-loss cross-product; D4 annual tabletop co-scheduled with IR plan tabletop with rotating two-scenario coverage per year; D5 off-GitHub mirroring named as hardening item not committed — engineer-as-collaborator gap-acknowledgment pattern; D6 spare-hardware commitment named for the first time with annual-review verification; D7 length ~1,100 lines per work-order's 800-1500 target overriding slice doc's 150-300 target; D8 out-of-scope content explicitly enumerated). Slice doc status flipped `ready` → `in-review`. **Anti-criteria honored:** P0-373-1 no unrealistic RTO/RPO for solo-maintainer single-host (Tier 3 RTO 4h explicitly best-effort during waking hours; RPO 24h matches actual pg_dump cadence; gaps named not hidden); P0-373-2 zero new dependencies; P0-373-3 zero CLAUDE.md / canvas changes (GOVERNANCE.md also untouched — referenced bidirectionally but not modified); P0-373-4 zero code changes; P0-373-5 no backup-retention compliance claims to specific frameworks without documenting verification path (Tier 3 retention windows stated; ISO 27001 5.36 monitoring-of-policies named at capability level not certification level); P0-373-6 GOVERNANCE.md not bundled inline — exists at repo root, cross-referenced (D1). **Engineer-as-collaborator gap surface for maintainer attention:** §11 hardening-items table is the consolidated visibility on personal-infrastructure dependencies the BCP plan acknowledges (spare hardware, off-GitHub mirror, WAL archival, status page, operator mailing list) — none are committed in this slice, all are surfaced for the annual-review prioritization conversation. Pairs with slice 372 to close the slice 329 Respond+Recover gap pair; H-3 / H-4 / H-5 remain open for slices 374 / 375 / 376. Slice doc at `docs/issues/373-business-continuity-plan.md`; decisions log at `docs/audit-log/373-business-continuity-plan-decisions.md`; companion governance index at `docs/governance/README.md`.

- **docs(governance): slice 372 — incident response plan (closes slice 329 H-1).** Filed `docs/governance/incident-response.md` — the project's standing answer to "what is your IR plan?", the load-bearing question for the v1 binary success criterion per the slice 329 compliance meta-audit (finding **H-1**, severity High; flagged as the **most-load-bearing** spillover for v1). The document covers all twelve sections expected of a SOC 2 CC7.3 + CC7.4 / ISO 27001 5.24 + 5.25 + 5.26 / NIST SP 800-61r3 / HIPAA 164.308(a)(6) incident-response surface: (1) purpose and scope, (2) **P0 / P1 / P2 / P3 severity tiering** with ack / containment / fix targets per tier (P0 24h ack best-effort outside business hours; P1 5 business days ack aligned with SECURITY.md; P2 10 business days; P3 20 business days), (3) **NIST roles inventory + solo-maintainer role devolution** explicitly named — Incident Commander / Tech Lead / Comms Lead / Scribe / On-call all devolve to the single maintainer, single-person on-call honest answer, no 24/7 promise the project cannot keep, GOVERNANCE.md advisory-council trigger named as the re-evaluation point, (4) detection sources (GitHub PVR, email, CI scanners — CodeQL + Dependabot + Trivy + GitGuardian + govulncheck + StepSecurity Harden-Runner, build pipeline failures, quarterly audit cycle, chaos experiments per slice 335, maintainer self-discovery) with the slice 327 "what to monitor for" inventory inlined, (5) NIST SP 800-61r3 lifecycle workflow (detect → triage → contain → eradicate → recover → review) with per-stage detail, (6) **communications playbook** restricted to existing channels only (GitHub PVR / advisory / CVE assignment / CHANGELOG `### Security` / release notes / `SECURITY-ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS.md` / `SECURITY-INCIDENTS.md`) — no operator mailing list / Discord / status page committed; tone discipline cross-referenced to slice 337's board-narrative anti-pattern list, (7) **per-tier playbooks for four common incident shapes** — data leak (secret committed to repo), auth compromise (maintainer GitHub PAT in third-party hands), dependency vulnerability (high-CVSS CVE in transitive dep), deploy break (release pipeline compromised or producing bad artifacts) — each with containment / eradication / recovery, (8) documentation and audit trail (`docs/incidents/YYYY-MM-DD-<slug>.md` public-by-default with `[redacted]` placeholder for attack-vector detail; CHANGELOG `### Security` for public-facing audit trail), (9) **blameless post-incident review** with template (mandatory for P0/P1, optional for P2/P3); action items become slices via `/idea-to-slice`; quarterly governance-checkin review of open action items, (10) **incident log template** with TOML frontmatter (`+++` markers matching CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md convention) carrying incident_id + severity + status + timestamps + reporter + IC for future automation, (11) **annual tabletop exercise commitment** (first due 2027-05-28) + chaos-experiment substrate from slice 335 + quarterly audit-cycle passive testing — three-redundant testing surface, (12) **annual review cadence** owned by maintainer, ISO 27001 5.36 monitoring-of-policies clause closed at capability level. Companion artifacts shipped in the same slice: `docs/governance/README.md` (governance directory index naming this IR plan + the four other planned governance documents 373/374/375/376), `docs/incidents/README.md` + `.gitkeep` (establishes the public-by-default incident-log directory with file-naming convention and confidentiality posture), `docs/audit-log/incident-NNN-template.md` (per AC-4 standalone post-incident review template for future incident reviews), `docs/audit-log/372-incident-response-plan-decisions.md` (D1 severity-tier shape P0/P1/P2/P3 chosen over Critical/High/Medium/Low to avoid CVSS-rating semantic collision; D2 solo-maintainer role devolution explicitly named with anti-pattern "24/7 on-call rotation" rejected per P0-372-1; D3 communications channels existing only — no operator mailing list / Discord / status page commitments; D4 annual tabletop cadence chosen over six-monthly with chaos backlog as supplementary substrate; D5 incident logs public-by-default with explicit redaction convention matching project transparency posture; D6 TOML frontmatter `+++` markers matching CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md precedent; D7 SECURITY.md one-line cross-reference scope per P0-372-2; D8 out-of-scope governance content explicitly enumerated). One-line `SECURITY.md` cross-reference added at end of "Disclosure policy" section pointing intake at the new IR plan (engineer-as-collaborator scope per slice doc — P0-372-2 honored, intake process unchanged). **Anti-criteria honored:** P0-372-1 no 24/7 commitments security-atlas can't keep (solo-maintainer best-effort explicitly named throughout); P0-372-2 SECURITY.md intake process unchanged (one-line cross-reference only); P0-372-3 zero code changes; P0-372-4 zero CLAUDE.md / canvas changes (operational doc, not architectural); P0-372-5 zero new dependencies; P0-372-6 no SOC 2 certification claims (document describes capabilities, not certifications). Closes slice 329 finding H-1 and unblocks the v1 binary criterion's third-party-diligence first-question conversation. Slice doc at `docs/issues/372-incident-response-plan.md`; decisions log at `docs/audit-log/372-incident-response-plan-decisions.md`; companion governance index at `docs/governance/README.md`.

- **audit(compliance): slice 329 — compliance meta-audit via voltagent-qa-sec:compliance-auditor persona.** Read-only operator-side gap analysis of v1-complete `main` at HEAD `bb9d0adc` (post-slice-371 reconcile) against SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria (Security + Availability + Confidentiality), ISO 27001:2022 Annex A (5.x organizational + 8.x technological controls), NIST CSF 2.0 (six functions: Govern + Identify + Protect + Detect + Respond + Recover), GDPR Articles 25/30/32 (Art 33 deferred per OQ #10), HIPAA Security Rule (164.308 administrative + 164.312 technical safeguards; 164.310 physical N/A SaaS), and PCI DSS v4.0 non-CDE scope (req 6/8/10/11/12 only). **This is a gap analysis, not a certification claim** — security-atlas is not SOC 2 certified, not ISO 27001 certified, not HIPAA-attested; the audit examines whether the project's evidence could pass those audits today. **Headline finding:** technical controls (auth substrate, RLS tenant isolation, CI scanner coverage, change management discipline) are exceptionally strong; the operator-side gaps are **organizational and operational** — the policy artifacts a third-party reviewer expects to see before examining technical evidence. **No Critical findings.** 5 High (H-1 no documented Incident Response plan — most-load-bearing for v1 binary success criterion; H-2 no documented Business Continuity / Disaster Recovery plan; H-3 no documented GitHub-org access review cadence; H-4 no documented data retention + disposal policy; H-5 no project-level asset inventory document), 6 Medium (M-1 project-level monitoring SLOs; M-2 change-mgmt policy consolidation; M-3 vendor mgmt register; M-4 personnel security policy stub deferred per solo-maintainer stage; M-5 promotion policy formalization; M-6 project risk register), 4 Low (L-1 CoC reporting placeholder; L-2 SECURITY-ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS.md forward reference; L-3 project-side observability gap; L-4 CHANGELOG audit-trail formalization), 8 Informational (strong slice-process audit trail, branch-protection ruleset, CI scanner coverage, DCO discipline; plus correctly-deferred items: GDPR Art 33 per OQ #10, privacy v0 per OQ #7, HIPAA per-customer-scope to FrameworkScope, PCI DSS N/A no CDE). **Spillover follow-ups filed (cap 5):** **372** (H-1 — Incident Response plan), **373** (H-2 — Business Continuity / DR plan), **374** (H-3 — GitHub org access review cadence), **375** (H-4 — Data retention + disposal policy), **376** (H-5 — Project asset inventory). All 6 Mediums kept audit-report-only per JUDGMENT D3 — they share the structural cause "no consolidated compliance evidence index" that the 5 Highs collectively close. Zero findings duplicate slices 327 (security audit) or 328 (code review); cross-references recorded for ISO 27001 8.24 cryptography (slice 366 key rotation) and 8.31 supply-chain integrity (slice 368 cosign migration). Per-framework readiness verdicts: SOC 2 + ISO 27001 NO-pre-readiness with strong technical controls; NIST CSF PARTIAL (Identify + Respond + Recover gaps); GDPR PARTIAL Art 32 only; HIPAA + PCI DSS N/A. Audit report at `docs/audits/329-compliance-meta-audit-report.md`; decisions log at `docs/audit-log/329-compliance-meta-audit-decisions.md`. Read-only audit — zero code changes in this slice's diff.

### Quality

- **test(demo): slice 476 — verify the demo-data operator-reachability journey end-to-end + add a CLI reach-it hint.** Slice 476 was filed from live maintainer use: after the demo seed succeeded on a self-host edge box, the operator had **no way to reach the demo data** (the seed creates a SEPARATE `demo` tenant; the membership-bounded switcher (P0-192-5) hides a tenant the operator is not a member of). The mechanism that closes the gap was subsequently shipped by **slice 478** (the `POST /v1/admin/users/assign` self-assign API, incl. the load-bearing local-auth synthetic-key path) and **slice 479** (the `/admin/users` self-assign UI), so slice 476 **reduces to verification + a small seed hint** — building it from scratch would re-implement 478/479. **The gap no prior slice tests:** 205 (seed), 478 (assign), and 192 (resolver) are each covered in isolation, but the COMPOSED journey — seed the REAL demo dataset, self-assign the seeding operator to that REAL demo tenant, prove it is now reachable WITH the 50/20/200 dataset present — was never asserted. **New integration test** `internal/api/adminusers/demo_reachable_integration_test.go` (reuses the slice-478 harness in the same `adminusers_test` package + the real `internal/demoseed.Seeder` + the slice-192 `oauth.DBUserResolver`): (1) `TestDemoReachable_IdPOperator` (AC-1/AC-5) — seed → assert the demo tenant is NOT reachable pre-assign → self-assign → assert it IS in `available_tenants`, `current_tenant` UNCHANGED (no auto-switch), and the demo tenant carries ≥50 controls / ≥20 risks / ≥200 evidence; (2) `TestDemoReachable_LocalAuthOperator` (AC-2 — the load-bearing self-host case) — an empty-IdP operator self-assigns, reaches the demo tenant via the `urn:atlas:local` synthetic key, and `available_tenants` is EXACTLY `{session, demo}` (no over-match); (3) `TestDemoReachable_NormalUserCannotSeeDemoTenant` (AC-3 / P0-476-1) — a normal user who never self-assigned cannot see the demo tenant, preserving the P0-192-5 bound. The seeded demo tenant uses a unique neutral `test-demo-*` slug (never the literal `demo`) so a shared CI database is never polluted, and `t.Cleanup` tears it down via the seeder's own `Teardown` (exercising AC-6 seed/teardown interplay). The package is already enrolled in the integration shard manifest (`scripts/integration-shards.txt` B1) — no manifest change. **Seed hint (the one rough edge):** the `atlas-cli demo seed` success output printed the new tenant id + counts but never told the operator the data lives in a SEPARATE tenant they must self-assign to — a dead end. Added a three-line "Reach the demo data" block pointing at `/admin/users` → "Add me to a tenant" → re-auth → switch. No new surface (479 owns the UI, 478 the API) — pure operator guidance. **Type: JUDGMENT** — decisions log `docs/audit-log/476-demo-reachable-decisions.md` (D1 integration-over-e2e surface choice; D2 reduced-to-verification outcome; D3 seed-hint scope; D4 unique-slug + teardown-cleanup; detection-tier classification). Slice doc: `docs/issues/476-demo-data-reachable-for-operator.md`.

- **test(e2e): slice 423 — OSCAL signed-export download chain e2e.** Closes the v1-binary gap that the highest-stakes audit artifact — the OSCAL bundle the auditor receives — had no full-stack e2e behind it. The Go side (`internal/api/oscalexport/handler.go`, 98 floor) has unit + integration coverage, but the browser-driven export chain was never exercised end-to-end; slice 413 made the bundle a signed artifact (ADR-0010 — embedded-ed25519 default, cosign-kms opt-in), raising the stakes. **New spec** `web/e2e/oscal-export-e2e.spec.ts` drives an authenticated in-page `fetch` POST to `POST /v1/audit-periods/{id}/oscal-export` (the `evidence-push-e2e.spec.ts` / board-pack stage-1 precedent — a same-origin POST flows through the page network and is intercepted by `context.route`), then asserts: the export chain completes deterministically (AC-2 adapted to the real surface); `Content-Type: application/json` — the content type the handler sets at `handler.go:144` (AC-3); the envelope carries the four canonical OSCAL members SSP/AP/AR/POA&M (invariant #8 wire format); and the slice-413 signed-bundle manifest is present with `signature.mode === "embedded-ed25519"` (the KMS-free default reachable in the harness), a non-empty `ed25519` algorithm + lowercase-hex digest + signature + public_key (AC-4). **Scoping note (load-bearing):** the slice doc phrased AC-1/AC-2/AC-3 as a browser DOWNLOAD chain (`/audits` → per-period detail → click → `page.waitForEvent("download")` with a `Content-Disposition` attachment, mirroring the board-pack PDF), but that surface does not exist in v1 — the handler returns a JSON ENVELOPE not an attachment (no `download` event), there is no BFF route forwarding to the export endpoint, and there is no per-period detail page (`/audits/[id]` is a future slice per the slice-184 banner; the `/audits` toolbar carries the slice-217 disclosure `<span>`, not a trigger). Building that surface is a multi-slice FEATURE the slice's scope discipline forbids ("does NOT add a new export feature"); per AC-5 / P0-423-3 the missing download surface is a precondition gap filed as spillover **#457** rather than faked (a fabricated `download` event the product does not emit is the dishonest-test anti-pattern this project rejects). **P0 compliance:** no in-browser cosign verification (P0-423-1 — Go tier, slice 425); no cross-tenant negative case (P0-423-2 — Go RLS tier); no relaxed precondition (P0-423-3 — spillover #457); the slice-217 disclosure spec in `audits-list.spec.ts` is left intact (P0-423-4 / AC-7); `_STATUS.md` untouched (P0-423-5). All ids + signature values are neutral synthetic strings (no JWT/vendor-shaped literals — GitGuardian). **Type: AFK.** Spillover: `docs/issues/457-oscal-export-browser-download-surface.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/423-oscal-signed-export-download-e2e.md`. Mirrors slice 388; builds on slice 413; preserves slice 217.

- **test(e2e): slice 394 — load `/e2e/` `route.fulfill` mocks from contract goldens (closes slice 392 D5 deferral; resolves slice 334 P-1 for the golden-covered routes).** The Playwright e2e suite hand-wrote upstream response bodies in `route.fulfill` calls (slice 334 finding P-1), which can silently drift from the provider's recorded wire shape — the slice-210 / slice-276 class of bug ADR-0007's contract tier exists to catch. Slices 349/392 + 409 recorded the real Go handler bodies into `web/lib/contracts/*.golden.json` for nine endpoints; this slice teaches the e2e mocks for those routes to load from the goldens. **New helper** `web/e2e/test-utils/fulfill-from-golden.ts`: `fulfillFromGolden(route, endpoint, variant, { status?, override? })` reads a golden and serves the recorded body via `route.fulfill`. `endpoint` is a typed `GoldenEndpoint` union of the nine covered routes (`me`, `version`, `install-state`, `demo-status`, `framework-posture`, `activity`, `upcoming`, `freshness`, `drift`) — a typo cannot fall through to a non-golden path; an unknown `variant` throws (listing the available keys). The caller keeps `page.route(pattern, …)` + any method-guard; the helper owns only the fulfill. **Specs migrated** (every spec hand-writing a body for a golden-covered route): `first-time-login.spec.ts` (two install-state mocks → `fresh_install_without_tenant` / `post_first_install`), `dashboard.spec.ts` slice-229 subtitle tests (freshness `populated`+override for the deterministic 87% + two `empty`-variant cases), `settings-profile-credential-bearer.spec.ts` + `settings-notifications-credential-bearer.spec.ts` (`/api/me` → `synthetic_admin` + `display_name` override). **Left hand-mocked by design:** error/abort paths (no recorded body — the AC-3 escape hatch, e.g. the install-state 503 fallback + the dashboard `abort()` degrade tests), and routes with no golden (`/v1/risks`, `/v1/controls/*`, `/v1/board`, `/v1/policies` — goldens tracked as #410 / #411); the typed union mechanically prevents passing an uncovered endpoint. **Escape hatch (AC-3)** documented in `web/e2e/README.md` "Golden-backed route mocks" and tested two ways: the credential-bearer + freshness specs exercise the `override` deep-merge (golden stays the shape-complete base; only the assertion-load-bearing value changes), and the install-state spec keeps its hand-written 503 path beside the golden path. **Helper self-test** `web/lib/contracts/fulfill-from-golden.test.ts` pins the pure logic (endpoint→file map, variant lookup, unknown-variant throw, override merge) on the existing vitest surface (the helper's only Playwright import is `import type { Route }`, erased at runtime). **Zero new tooling, zero new CI job, zero new gate** (AC-4) — rides the existing `Frontend · Playwright e2e` + `Frontend · vitest` surfaces; `.github/workflows/ci.yml` untouched. **Type: JUDGMENT** — decisions log `docs/audit-log/394-e2e-fulfill-from-golden-decisions.md` (D1 helper shape + location; D2 migration inventory; D3 escape hatch; D4 zero-new-gate; D5 the discourage-new-hand-mocks note). ADR: `docs/adr/0007-contract-test-tier.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/394-e2e-mocks-load-from-contract-goldens.md`.

- **test(contract): slice 409 — contract-tier goldens for the dashboard panel routes (unblocks slice 394).** Extends the slice-349/392 golden-file contract tier (ADR-0007 option 1: provider records, consumer asserts) to the five high-traffic dashboard panel routes the `/e2e/` suite traverses — the precondition slice 394 (teach the `route.fulfill` mocks to load from goldens) was `blocked` on. **The load-bearing per-endpoint design call (JUDGMENT):** the dashboard handlers read tenant data through a `*Store` holding a pgx pool, exactly the case slice 392 hit with `/v1/metrics` and deferred for lack of an interface seam. Resolved all five via **Option A — a bounded, unexported, injectable read seam** (`reader` in `internal/api/dashboard`; `freshnessLister` + `driftReporter` in `internal/api/freshnessdrift`) that `*Store` satisfies unchanged, so the recorder injects a fixed-row stub and records the real row→wire transformation on the plain `go test ./...` **unit surface** — no Postgres, no integration tag (P0-409-1 honored). The seams + their `newHandlerWith*` constructors stay unexported and `New(*Store)` is byte-for-byte unchanged (P0-409-2 honored); `httpserver.go` wiring untouched. **Endpoints covered:** `GET /v1/frameworks/posture`, `GET /v1/activity`, `GET /v1/upcoming` (provider `internal/api/dashboard/handler_contract_test.go`), `GET /v1/evidence/freshness`, `GET /v1/controls/drift` (provider `internal/api/freshnessdrift/handler_contract_test.go`); each records `populated` + `empty` variants (the empty-set variant pins the load-bearing array-vs-null contract a fresh-tenant e2e run hits; the activity null-summary row pins `summary: null`; the freshness class-less row pins the `unclassified` bucket). Consumer asserts under `web/lib/contracts/*.contract.test.ts` drive the real BFF (`web/app/api/dashboard/**/route.ts`) against each recorded body (verbatim passthrough) on the existing vitest surface. **Goldens (neutral fixtures, synthetic UUIDs — slice 314 / GitGuardian):** `web/lib/contracts/{framework-posture,activity,upcoming,freshness,drift}.golden.json`. **Zero new tooling, zero new CI job, zero new gate** (AC-4; `.github/workflows/ci.yml` untouched). **Drift sensitivity proven empirically** (AC-3): renaming the freshness golden's per-bucket `stale` → `staleCount` failed **both** halves (provider golden-mismatch + consumer "stale must be number"); golden restored, both green. **AC-5: slice 394 reassessed and flipped `blocked` → `ready`** — every dashboard route the e2e suite mocks now has a golden (9 endpoints total with slice 392's four), so a `fulfillFromGolden` helper is no longer premature; `docs/issues/394-*.md` Status + Dependencies updated (the slice doc, NOT `_STATUS.md`). **Option-C deferrals** (handlers needing a wider seam than recording justifies): `GET /v1/risks` dashboard panel (wide multi-method risks handler + non-passthrough BFF → spillover **#410**, `ready`) and the controls-detail / audit-workspace route families (large pool-backed surfaces → spillover **#411**, `blocked` on appetite). **Type: JUDGMENT** — decisions log `docs/audit-log/409-contract-tier-rollout-dashboard-decisions.md` (D1 per-endpoint A/B/C + P0 compliance; D2 Option A over B; D3 variant shape; D4 drift proof; D5 the 394 reassessment; D6 spillovers; detection-tier classification). ADR: `docs/adr/0007-contract-test-tier.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/409-contract-tier-dashboard-routes.md`.

- **ci(e2e): slice 387 — production-build standalone CI harness for the prod-build specs (closes slice 351 spillover).** Slice 351's audit (AC-4, disposition (b)) re-quarantined `web/e2e/bff-cookie-production-build.spec.ts` (slice 146 — the `NODE_ENV`-coupled cookie attribute that turned BFF panel JSON into login HTML under standalone) and `web/e2e/logo-render-production-build.spec.ts` (slice 153 — the `output: "standalone"` tracer not copying `web/public/`, so the logo + OG/Twitter assets 404'd in the deployed image) behind `test.skip(!process.env.ATLAS_PROD_BUILD, …)` because **no CI job built + booted the Next.js standalone server** (`grep ATLAS_PROD_BUILD .github/` was empty). Both specs assert regressions that ONLY manifest under `node .next/standalone/web/server.js`, not under the `npm start` dev server the existing Playwright job runs against — forcing them green there would have green-washed the standalone-only path. This slice adds the missing harness. **New CI job** `frontend-playwright-prod-build` (check name `Frontend · Playwright e2e (prod-build standalone)`, with a slice-061 stub sibling under the same name) mirrors the dev-server Playwright leg's bring-up verbatim (Postgres service container + NATS + MinIO via `docker run` + atlas build/boot + slice-201 runtime JWT mint via global-setup), then swaps three things: builds with `npm run build:standalone` (`next build && cp -r public .next/standalone/web/public` — the `cp` reproduces the web.Dockerfile runtime-stage copy the standalone tracer omits, exactly the slice-153 fix), boots `node .next/standalone/web/server.js` instead of `npm start`, and sets `ATLAS_PROD_BUILD=1` so the prod-build specs un-skip. **AC-1:** the job builds + boots the standalone server (verified in CI — all build/boot steps green). **AC-2:** the slice-153 `logo-render-production-build.spec.ts` executes (not skipped) and PASSES against the standalone server (it serves `/logo-light.svg`, `/logo-dark.svg`, `/og-image.png`, `/twitter-card.png`, `/favicon.ico` all 200 with correct content-types and `/login` references `/logo-light.svg`), so the slice-153 `output: "standalone"` public-assets regression is now gated. The companion slice-146 `bff-cookie-production-build.spec.ts` is **held back to spillover slice 399**: its first real run exposed two dev-server-shaped body assumptions (it asserts the browser fires `/api/dashboard/` BFF calls, but under the prod build the panels are server-rendered RSC so zero client-side calls fire — the page renders fully authenticated, so this is NOT the slice-146 cookie regression; and it calls `addCookies` with a `domain` derived from `authedPage.url()` before navigation, which is `about:blank` → empty hostname). Fixing those is a spec-BODY change the slice 387 brief forbids, so the job ships **ADVISORY** (`continue-on-error: true`) running only the logo spec until slice 399 re-shapes the cookie spec's assertions and flips the job to blocking. **AC-3:** the dev-server `Frontend · Playwright e2e` leg is untouched — it sets no `ATLAS_PROD_BUILD`, so both prod-build specs stay `test.skip`-guarded there (no-op, as before); that leg passed green in the same run. The `test.skip(!ATLAS_PROD_BUILD)` guards are RETAINED (the mechanism that scopes each spec to the standalone leg); only the quarantine comments + skip-reason strings were updated. **AC-4:** decisions log records the new-job-vs-matrix-leg choice (D1) + wall-clock cost (D6) + the cookie-spec deferral (D9). Ships NON-required + advisory (`.github/branch-protection.json` unchanged); promoted to a blocking required check once slice 399 lands and both specs are green. **Type: JUDGMENT** — decisions log `docs/audit-log/387-e2e-prod-build-ci-harness-decisions.md` (D1 separate job over matrix leg; D2 dev-server leg untouched; D3 guard retained not removed; D4 standalone boot/build mechanics; D5 named-specs-only; D6 wall-clock; D7 sudo exception; D8 actionlint; D9 cookie-spec held to slice 399 + advisory). Spillover: `docs/issues/399-bff-cookie-prod-build-spec-body-fix.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/387-e2e-prod-build-standalone-ci-harness.md`.

- **test(contract): slice 392 — roll out the golden-file contract tier to three more BFF↔atlas endpoint pairs (closes slice 349 spillover).** Extends the slice-349 golden-file contract tier (ADR-0007 option 1: provider records, consumer asserts) from the install-state pilot to three additional high-value endpoint pairs, following the pilot pattern exactly (a Go unit-surface recorder writes a committed golden under `web/lib/contracts/`; a vitest consumer asserts the Next.js BFF against it; `-update` regenerates the golden in the same PR as an intentional shape change). **Endpoints covered:** (1) `GET /v1/version` — provider `internal/api/version_contract_test.go` (`TestContract_Version`) records the slice-072 four-field shape (`version`/`commit`/`build_time`/`go_version`) from the real `VersionHandler` (pure unit surface, `fieldsFn` callback, no DB), consumer `web/lib/contracts/version.contract.test.ts` asserts verbatim passthrough + the `public, max-age=300` cache header; (2) `GET /v1/me` — provider `internal/api/me/profile_contract_test.go` (`TestContract_Me`) records the slice-108/130 identity shape via the **no-DB synthetic-profile path** (`NewProfile(nil,nil,nil)` + a non-UUID credential id so `buildProfile` never reaches the users store and `resolveRoles` returns `[]`), consumer `web/lib/contracts/me.contract.test.ts` asserts `user_id`/`tenant_id`/`is_admin`/`tenant_role` always present, `roles` always an array, `owner_roles` array-or-null, `time_zone` nullable; (3) `GET /v1/admin/demo/status` — provider `internal/api/admindemo/status_contract_test.go` (`TestContract_DemoStatus`) records the slice-278 `{enabled: bool}` shape (admin-credential context, injected gate, no DB), consumer `web/lib/contracts/demo-status.contract.test.ts` asserts the boolean passthrough + the 401-on-missing-cookie guard. The slice-349 pilot's inlined plumbing is factored into a shared `internal/api/contractrecord_test.go` helper (canonicalize/read-golden/diff/`-update` + a symmetric dropped-variant check); the `me`/`admindemo` packages carry sibling copies because Go test files cannot cross a package boundary. The pilot file is left untouched (reference). **`GET /v1/metrics` deliberately NOT covered** — its `ListCatalog` handler reads through `dbx.New(h.pool)` with no interface seam, so recording it would require the Go integration surface (DB bring-up coupled to golden regeneration), violating ADR-0007's "rides the Go-unit surface" intent; deferred with rationale (decisions log D1). **Zero new tooling, zero new CI job, zero new gate** (anti-criteria P0-1/P0-2) — the tier rides the existing Go-unit + vitest surfaces; `.github/workflows/ci.yml` untouched. **Drift sensitivity proven empirically** (AC-2): renaming the version golden's `build_time` → `built_at` failed **both** halves (provider golden-mismatch + consumer "must carry string build_time"); golden restored, both green. **AC-3 (e2e mocks load from goldens): DEFERRED with rationale** (decisions log D5) — teaching the 57 `route.fulfill` mocks to load from goldens does not materially reduce slice-334 P-1's fragility until golden coverage spans the ~30+ routes the e2e suite traverses (3 goldens vs ~30 routes), and the per-test error/pagination/empty-set variations the mocks need would still require a hand-written escape hatch beside any golden-backed helper; filed as a spillover to land once golden coverage reaches the high-traffic dashboard routes. **Type: JUDGMENT** — decisions log `docs/audit-log/392-contract-test-tier-rollout-decisions.md` (D1 endpoint selection + metrics deferral on the unit-surface constraint; D2 variants-not-single-body; D3 shared recorder helper + per-package copies; D4 `-update` flag composition; D5 e2e-mock-loading defer; D6 drift proof on version). ADR: `docs/adr/0007-contract-test-tier.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/392-contract-test-tier-rollout.md`.

- **docs(qa): slice 349 — contract-test-tier evaluation + golden-file PILOT (closes slice 333 Q-1 / slice 334 P-1).** Evaluation slice resolving slice 333 finding Q-1: no test surface pins the HTTP response-body shape between the Next.js BFF (consumer) and the Go atlas API (provider). The four-surface gate exercises the provider in isolation (Go unit/integration) and the consumer with _hand-written upstream mocks_ (vitest `route.test.ts` literals, 57 Playwright `route.fulfill` calls) — nothing binds the consumer's invented shape to the provider's real one. The slice-140 `openapi-drift-check` guard does NOT close it: it verifies route presence + auth tier + structural shape but explicitly NOT response bodies (`RouteSpec` carries no body field; `validator.go` "What it does NOT check"). The gap is concrete and has already bitten — slice 210's BE/FE contract bug (install-state gained a `tenant_id` field the login form needed). **Decision (ADR-0007): ADOPT via a tight golden-file pilot; broad rollout deferred to slice 392.** Four options scored on (a) build cost, (b) maintenance cost, (c) drift sensitivity, (d) slice-069 fit: Option 1 (golden-file: provider records, consumer asserts) wins as the only option closing the gap with zero new tooling, zero new CI job, zero new gate — riding the existing Go-unit + vitest surfaces. Option 2 (schemathesis) rejected — depends on complete OpenAPI body schemas that do not exist + a fifth surface. Option 3 (`buf breaking`) rejected — gRPC-only, cannot see the HTTP wire shape (slice doc P0-2). Pact rejected as cargo-cult broker infra for a monorepo where provider and consumer ship in one PR. **Pilot shipped** for `GET /v1/install-state` (chosen for its slice-210 bug history + non-trivial conditional `tenant_id` shape): provider recorder `internal/api/install_state_contract_test.go` (`TestContract_InstallState`, runs on the plain `go test ./...` unit surface via the `PlatformStatus` interface — no DB) records three variants to the shared golden `web/lib/contracts/install-state.golden.json`; consumer `web/lib/contracts/install-state.contract.test.ts` reads the golden and asserts the BFF's `first_install` assumption + verbatim passthrough on the vitest surface. `-update` flag regenerates the golden in the same PR as an intentional shape change. **Drift sensitivity proven empirically** (not asserted): injecting `first_install` → `firstInstall` failed both halves; golden restored. Rollout follow-on filed at `docs/issues/392-contract-test-tier-rollout.md` (P0: no new tooling, no new CI job). **Type: JUDGMENT** — decisions log `docs/audit-log/349-contract-test-tier-evaluation-decisions.md` (D1 pilot-then-adopt over no-tier; D2 Option 1 over 2/3/Pact; D3 install-state-only over the doc's pair suggestion; D4 unit-surface recorder; D5 golden under `web/lib/contracts`; D6 shape- not byte-equivalence; D7 empirical drift proof; D8 follow-on filed). ADR: `docs/adr/0007-contract-test-tier.md`. Slice doc: `docs/issues/349-contract-test-tier-evaluation.md`.

- **ci: slice 391 — wire `duphelper-lint` into CI as a hard-failure step (closes slice 369 spillover).** Slice 369 created the `cmd/scripts/duphelper-lint` analyzer (golang.org/x/tools/go/analysis; mirror of slice 367's `errleak-lint`) — the H-1 helper-consolidation regression guard that rejects new package-local `writeJSON`/`writeError`/`writeServerErr` free-function declarations in `internal/api/*` — and wired it into the `justfile` `lint` target (`just lint-duphelper`), but could not touch `.github/workflows/ci.yml` because batch-164 reserved that file for slice 345 to avoid a merge collision (slice 369 AC-4 deferred to a spillover by the batch directive). This slice adds the deferred CI step: a new `Go · duphelper (slice 369 H-1 guard)` step in the `lint-go` job, immediately after the slice-367 errleak step, invoking `go run ./cmd/scripts/duphelper-lint ./internal/api/...` on the same `setup-go` cache. A hit exits 3 and fails the CI check, identical to the errleak step's contract — the guard is now enforced as a hard failure on every PR. Pure CI wiring; the analyzer logic is unchanged (P0-391-1). Closes slice 369 AC-4.

- **audit(quality): slice 328 — comprehensive code-review audit via voltagent-qa-sec:code-reviewer persona.** Read-only audit of v1-complete `main` at HEAD `1e1a78a6` across all three language surfaces (Go backend ~779 files, TypeScript frontend ~506 files, Python OSCAL bridge 7 files). **No Critical findings.** 2 High (H-1 `writeJSON`/`writeError`/`writeServerErr` duplicated across 50+ `internal/api/*` packages as 101 declarations; H-2 `web/lib/api.ts` god-file at 2901 LOC + 219 exports violating the established `web/lib/api/*.ts` split convention), 4 Medium (M-1 `internal/api/httpserver.go` 1397-LOC god-file with 184 route registrations — audit-report-only; M-2 auth substrate clock-injection drift across sessions/apikeystore/jwtmw; M-3 `SESSION_COOKIE` symbol misnamed — audit-report-only; M-4 Python OSCAL bridge gRPC INTERNAL status reflects `str(exc)` from compliance-trestle — cross-reference to slice 327 M-2 thread for maintainer dedupe), 3 Low (writeLog near-duplicates; apikeystore goroutine shutdown coordination; json encode error discards — all audit-report-only), 4 Informational (exceptional doc density; zero TODO debt; zero package-boundary violations; comment-vs-code drift sample). Spillover follow-ups filed: **369** (H-1 — consolidate writeJSON/writeError into `internal/api/httpresp` mirror of slice 367's `httperr`), **370** (H-2 — split `web/lib/api.ts` into per-domain files), **371** (M-2 — auth-substrate clock injection across 3 packages). Mediums M-1, M-3, M-4 documented in the audit report only per JUDGMENT (see decisions log §D3-D5). Zero findings duplicate slice 327's security audit per §D6. Audit report at `docs/audits/328-code-review-comprehensive-report.md`; decisions log at `docs/audit-log/328-code-review-comprehensive-decisions.md`. Read-only audit — zero code changes in this slice's diff.

### Security

- **fix(security): slice 466 — replace the `TRUST_FORWARDED_HEADERS` boolean with a `TRUSTED_PROXY_CIDRS` allowlist + right-to-left `X-Forwarded-For` walk.** Closes the client-IP-spoofing vector left open by slice 465's blunt boolean (which trusted the left-most `X-Forwarded-For` IP unconditionally, so a deployment not actually behind a header-scrubbing proxy let any client forge its source IP — poisoning the slice-162 session-row client-IP capture that feeds the audit log and the admin-demo per-IP rate limiter). The structurally-correct design ships: `internal/api/auth/clientip.go` now parses `TRUSTED_PROXY_CIDRS` (comma-separated CIDRs) into a validated set **once at boot** (`InitTrustedProxiesFromEnv`, called from `cmd/atlas/main.go`; a malformed CIDR fails loud with `os.Exit(1)` rather than silently per-request — AC-1), then resolves the client IP by walking `X-Forwarded-For` **right-to-left**, accepting a hop only while the immediately-connecting peer is inside an allowed CIDR and stopping at the first untrusted hop (the real client — AC-2), so a client-forged XFF prefix is never reached. Empty/unset `TRUSTED_PROXY_CIDRS` ⇒ direct TCP peer (`r.RemoteAddr`), byte-identical to today's unset behavior (AC-3, proven via `docker compose config` diff). `TRUST_FORWARDED_HEADERS=1` is retained as a **documented deprecated alias** that maps to trust-any-proxy (`0.0.0.0/0` + `::/0`) with a boot-time deprecation warning, superseded when `TRUSTED_PROXY_CIDRS` is set (AC-4). The `internal/api/admindemo/handler.go` rate-limiter `clientIP` was de-duplicated to delegate to the new exported `auth.ClientIP`, so both IP-capture surfaces share ONE implementation (no drift). A per-request hop cap (`maxForwardedHops=50`) bounds the walk against a pathological-header CPU sink (threat-model D). Plumbed through `deploy/docker/.env.example` (commented-off with the spoofing warning) + `docker-compose.yml` (`TRUSTED_PROXY_CIDRS: ${TRUSTED_PROXY_CIDRS:-}`, empty default) + the slice-430 config reference (`docs-site/docs/configuration.md` row + security-critical bullet; `config-reference-drift-check` green). Load-bearing unit tests (AC-5) cover forged-prefix-rejected (direct + prepended-through-proxy), single/multiple trusted hops, empty allowlist = direct peer, malformed header, malformed-CIDR-fails-at-boot, and the hop cap; two wire-level tests drive the resolver over a real TCP connection (`internal/api/auth/clientip_server_test.go`). AC-6's full multi-container proxy e2e is filed as **slice 470** (the self-host seed ships no reverse-proxy container) — the assertion was not relaxed; the walk is covered at the unit + real-TCP tier in the interim. JUDGMENT decisions log at `docs/audit-log/466-trusted-proxy-cidrs-decisions.md` (D1 back-compat mapping · D2 hop cap · D3 keep both vars · D4 e2e spillover · D5 single shared impl · D6 boot-time parse).

- **feat(deploy): slice 465 — plumb `TRUST_FORWARDED_HEADERS` through the self-host bundle (default OFF).** The server already reads `TRUST_FORWARDED_HEADERS` (`internal/api/auth/clientip.go`, `internal/api/admindemo/handler.go`) to opt session-row client-IP capture into parsing `X-Forwarded-For` instead of `r.RemoteAddr`, but slice 460 found the key was never plumbed through `deploy/docker/`, so an operator behind a trusted reverse proxy had no templated way to enable correct client-IP capture (sessions recorded the proxy IP, degrading audit/forensic value). This slice plumbs it: `docker-compose.yml`'s `atlas` service now passes `TRUST_FORWARDED_HEADERS: ${TRUST_FORWARDED_HEADERS:-}` (empty default — unset maps to off, so `docker compose config` is byte-identical to today when the var is unset), and `deploy/docker/.env.example` documents it as a **commented-out, default-off** opt-in carrying a prominent client-IP-spoofing warning: it may be enabled **only** behind a trusted proxy that overwrites the inbound header, otherwise any client can forge the recorded IP and poison IP-based rate-limit / audit decisions. The slice-430 config-reference page (`docs-site/docs/configuration.md`) gains the matching row + a `security-critical` posture-toggle bullet, keeping `just config-reference-drift-check` green. No reading code changed — this is a plumb-and-template slice. JUDGMENT decisions log at `docs/audit-log/465-trust-forwarded-headers-decisions.md` (D1 empty-default vs `false` literal · D2 single boolean vs trusted-proxy CIDR allowlist, deferred to a v2 follow-on · D3 warning phrasing · D4 SELF_HOSTING.md left untouched).

- **feat(auth): slice 366 — JWT signing key rotation end-to-end.** Closes slice 327 security-audit finding **M-1** (MEDIUM — long-lived static ES256 signing key; compromise blast radius unbounded; NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 §5.3.6 cryptoperiod guidance unmet). The slice-187 keystore shipped multi-key data structures + multi-key JWKS but `fsstore.Store.Rotate` was a stub returning `ErrRotateUnsupported`. This slice lights up the orchestration on top of the existing primitives. **(1) `fsstore.Store.Rotate(ctx)`** generates a fresh ES256 keypair, writes it at mode 0600 via the existing atomic temp+rename `writePrivateKey` path, and reloads so the new (newer-timestamp) KeyID becomes the active signer while every prior key remains in the verification set for the overlap window; a same-second collision bumps the new KeyID to the next second so the chronological-lexicographic "alphabetically-last is active" invariant always holds. **(2) `fsstore.Store.Prune(ctx, cutoff)`** removes keypair files at or before the cutoff EXCEPT the active signer (P0-366-2 — never pruned regardless of age), reloading so pruned keys drop from JWKS; the cutoff is `now − AccessTokenLifetime − RotationOverlap` (`fsstore.PruneCutoff`), structurally guaranteeing no in-flight token is invalidated mid-validation (P0-366-3). **(3) `fsstore.Store.List(ctx)`** returns a minimal `KeyInfo{KeyID, Age, Signing}` per key — no field can carry private material (P0-366-1 defense-in-depth). **(4) `atlas-cli keys list / rotate / prune`** operate directly on the keystore directory (no DATABASE_URL); `prune` is dry-run by default and requires `--confirm` to delete. **(5) scheduled rotation** in `cmd/atlas` rotates on a configurable cadence (annual default via `ATLAS_KEY_ROTATION_INTERVAL`) and prunes after each rotation; every rotation writes a structured platform-level audit line `audit_event=key_rotation` carrying the previous + new signing KeyIDs (never key bytes — P0-366-1 / P0-187-6). **(6)** the JWKS endpoint continues to publish the full verification set; a new test asserts both keys are advertised across the overlap window. ADR-0003 § Key rotation strategy updated to "implemented" (and the cadence-default discrepancy — annual per spec vs the draft's 90d — reconciled in-doc); operational runbook at `docs/runbooks/jwt-key-rotation.md` covers routine + emergency (suspected-compromise) + recovery + JWKS-impact. Integration tests (`//go:build integration`, `-race`) cover: pre-rotation token still verifies during overlap (AC-3), pruned-key token rejected past overlap (AC-4), and rotate-under-concurrent-verify with no data race. JUDGMENT decisions log at `docs/audit-log/366-jwt-key-rotation-decisions.md` (D1 overlap 24h · D2 annual cadence · D3 slog-vs-table audit trail · D4 collision bump · D5 prune dry-run default · D6 minimal KeyInfo surface). KMS/HSM-backed rotation remains a deferred v3 revisit.

- **fix(api): slice 367 — generic 5xx error responses + custom errleak lint rule (CWE-209 closure).** Closes slice 327 security-audit finding **M-2** (MEDIUM, CWE-209 Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information). The `internal/api/` HTTP surface previously reflected raw `err.Error()` text — pgx column/table/constraint names, filesystem paths, library-internal state — into the JSON body of 5xx responses across ~119 sites. Three load-bearing changes ship together: **(1) shared helper** `internal/api/httperr.WriteInternal(w, r, op, err)` (plus `WriteStatus` for 502/503/504) emits a generic `{"error":"internal error","request_id":"<uuid>"}` body to clients, an `X-Request-Id` response header, and a `slog.Error` log line that carries the full `err.Error()` keyed by `request_id`, `op`, `method`, `path`, `status` server-side (P0-367-2 — server-side logging never weakened); **(2) chi request-ID middleware** at `internal/api/requestidmw` mounted second in the root chain (after `securityheaders`, before `corsMiddleware`/JWT) — reuses an inbound `X-Request-Id` only when it parses as a canonical UUID (rejects hostile garbage / log-injection / 4 KB blobs and mints a fresh UUIDv4 instead), then plumbs the ID through context and response header; **(3) custom static analyzer** at `cmd/scripts/errleak-lint` (golang.org/x/tools/go/analysis) — rejects any `writeJSON`/`writeError` call at a 5xx status (constant OR numeric literal 500-599) whose body arg transitively contains an `err.Error()` call; wired into the `Go · lint` CI job and `just lint-errleak`. All 119 sites across 36 files were migrated to the helper (or to the per-package `writeServerErr`/`writeStoreErr`/`writePackError`/`writeBundleError` helpers which themselves now delegate to `httperr.WriteInternal`); 4xx behavior is out of scope per slice 367 D1 and remains unchanged. Two existing test fixtures were rewired to assert the new generic shape and explicitly the absence of leaked detail (`internal/api/dashboardexport/dashboardexport_test.go::TestSlice269_ExportDashboard_SourceErrReturns500`, `internal/api/mcpwriteproposals/handlers_test.go::TestWriteServerErr_GenericInternalError`). Decisions log at `docs/audit-log/367-error-detail-leakage-audit-decisions.md` records D1 (5xx-only scope), D2 (`"internal error" + request_id` body wording), D3 (no pre-existing request-ID middleware — slice 367 adds the chi-mounted one), and D4 (hard CI failure on new violations).

- **feat(auth): slice 365 — OIDC ID-token nonce generation + validation.** Closes slice 327 security-audit finding **H-1** (HIGH, CWE-294 Authentication Bypass by Capture-Replay). The OIDC Relying Party at `internal/auth/oidc/oidc.go` now generates a fresh 16-byte `crypto/rand`-backed nonce per flow alongside the existing `state` + PKCE `code_verifier`; the value is persisted in a new `atlas_oidc_nonce` flow cookie (HttpOnly + SameSite=Lax + Path=/auth/oidc + 10-minute MaxAge, mirroring the other flow cookies) and sent on the authorize URL via `coreos.Nonce(...)`. On callback, `HandleCallback` reads the cookie and — after `verifier.Verify` parses the ID token — manually compares `idTok.Nonce` against the cookie value (go-oidc v3.18.0 `verify.go:189` explicitly does NOT verify the nonce claim; it's the caller's responsibility). Mismatch or missing cookie returns a new sentinel `ErrNonceMismatch`, distinct from `ErrStateMismatch` so audit-log review can tell a CSRF attempt apart from an ID-token-replay attempt. `ClearFlowCookies` extended to clear the nonce cookie alongside state/verifier/idp. **Additive only:** state + PKCE validation paths are unchanged (locked in by `TestStateStillEnforcedAlongsideNonce` + `TestBeginLoginPreservesStateAndPKCECookies`). Closes the OIDC Core §3.1.2.1 + RFC 9700 §4.5.3 gap that an external auditor will flag. Decisions log at `docs/audit-log/365-oidc-nonce-validation-decisions.md`.

- **audit(security): slice 327 — read-only security audit of v1-complete `main` via voltagent-qa-sec:security-auditor persona.** Comprehensive seven-surface read-only audit (OIDC RP, OAuth Authorization Server, RLS tenant isolation, evidence integrity, AI-assist boundary, OWASP top-10, cross-cutting). **No Critical findings.** 1 High (H-1 OIDC ID-token nonce missing — `internal/auth/oidc/oidc.go`), 3 Medium (M-1 JWT signing key rotation not implemented; M-2 verbose error reflection in 36 `internal/api/` handler sites; M-3 OSCAL signing uses in-process ed25519 not canvas-mandated cosign), 3 Low + 3 Informational. Spillover follow-ups filed: **365** (H-1 OIDC nonce), **366** (M-1 key rotation), **367** (M-2 error-detail leakage cleanup + lint rule), **368** (M-3 cosign migration). Audit report at `docs/audits/327-security-audit-security-auditor-report.md`; decisions log at `docs/audit-log/327-security-audit-security-auditor-decisions.md`. Read-only audit — zero code changes in this slice's diff.

### Added

- **feat(oscal): slice 413 — cosign-kms OSCAL bundle signing + retained embedded-ed25519 (368 Phase 1).** Implements Phase 1 of the [ADR-0010](docs/adr/0010-oscal-cosign-signing.md) ADOPT-DEFERRED plan (maintainer-approved 2026-06-03): the **reachable** cosign value with **no Fulcio/Rekor/OIDC dependency**, keeping the in-process ed25519 path as the air-gap default. Keyless (Fulcio + Rekor) stays deferred to slice 414. **New package `internal/oscal/cosign`** — a conservative wrapper around the `cosign sign-blob`/`verify-blob` binary: an injectable `runner` exec boundary (the cosign binary is a swappable, unit-testable dependency — every wrapper branch is covered with no binary, the real-binary path via the integration tier), explicit context timeouts, a **curated env allowlist** (the cosign subprocess inherits ONLY `PATH`/`HOME` + cloud-KMS credential families `AWS_*`/`GOOGLE_*`/`AZURE_*`/`VAULT_*` — never the atlas DSN or OAuth keys; deny-by-default, `WithExtraEnvKeys` for non-standard vars), no shell interpolation (argv slice, never `sh -c`), and structured error mapping (`ErrCosignNotFound`/`ErrSignFailed`/`ErrVerifyFailed`/`ErrTimeout`/`ErrBadConfig`). **`Mode` discriminator** on `oscal.Signature` (`embedded-ed25519` | `cosign-kms`; `cosign-keyless` reserved-not-implemented per P0-413-1) recorded in the export manifest with `omitempty`; **verification dispatches** on it. **`KMSSigner`** signs the same deterministic bundle digest via cosign + a cloud-KMS key reference (`ATLAS_COSIGN_KMS_REF`), recording the `key_ref` in the manifest so an auditor verifies with **stock `cosign verify-blob`** — closing the bulk of the slice-327 M-3 / canvas-§9 auditor friction. **Backward-compat (P0-413-4):** a pre-413 manifest with no `mode` field defaults to `embedded-ed25519` and still verifies (directly tested via `TestBackwardCompat_OldManifestVerifies`, which strips the `mode` key to simulate the old format). `VerifyBundle` (no cosign dep) handles embedded and **fails closed** (`ErrCosignVerifierRequired`) on a cosign bundle rather than silently passing; `VerifyBundleWithCosign` is the cosign-aware superset the CLI uses; both recompute the digest from on-disk member bytes (a member-tamper is rejected before cosign even runs). **Offline guarantee (P0-413-1):** the sign argv pairs `--use-signing-config=false` + `--tlog-upload=false` — empirically load-bearing (cosign v3.0.6 still uploads a Rekor entry with only the former); `TestIntegration_NoTlogEntry` asserts no `tlogEntries` are produced. **CLI** `atlas oscal sign|verify|config-check` (new `oscal` parent; the existing `oscal-export` command is unchanged) with `config-check --probe` running a live KMS test-sign. **Mode resolution** centralized in `ResolveSigningConfig` (`ATLAS_OSCAL_SIGNING_MODE` explicit, a set KMS ref infers `cosign-kms`, else the air-gap-safe embedded default — P0-413-2; `cosign-keyless` rejected). **`ReadBundle`** loads a written bundle for verification (path-traversal-guarded). **Defaults per ADR-0010:** docker-compose self-host → `embedded-ed25519` (unchanged); connected self-host with KMS → `cosign-kms` (opt-in); SaaS/Helm at GA → `cosign-kms` (revised AC-9). **CI:** the `tests-integration` job now installs cosign (`sigstore/cosign-installer@v4.1.2`, v3.0.6 — same pin as release signing) so the Mode-A integration tests run; `internal/oscal/cosign` enrolled in the integration package list (slice-345 guard green) with a hard coverage floor of 88 (merged ~91.5%). The Mode-A integration tests use a locally-generated throwaway cosign key pair as a clearly-marked cloud-KMS stand-in (no real key material — GitGuardian-safe). cosign binary provenance (v3.0.6, Apache-2.0, bundle-clean) recorded in the asset inventory §2.3 + §3.3 per AC-10. Operator runbook at `docs/runbooks/oscal-signing.md`. **Server export wiring stays embedded** (the CLI carries the cosign-kms capability now; threading mode-aware signing through `Exporter` is the top revisit item — D9). **Type: JUDGMENT** — decisions log `docs/audit-log/413-cosign-kms-signing-decisions.md`. Slice doc `docs/issues/413-cosign-kms-oscal-signing-phase-1.md`; parent `docs/issues/368-cosign-signing-migration.md`.

- **test(e2e): slice 351 — critical multi-tenant flow gap fill (closes slice 333 Q-9).** Audits the merged Playwright gate against the v1 binary success test and fills the P0 gaps. **Coverage matrix** at `docs/audits/351-e2e-critical-flow-coverage-matrix.md` grades 14 critical flows (spec status × priority) and reconciles two stale rows in the slice 333 spot-check (`questionnaires.spec.ts` was never skipped — it is a live mocked spec; `control-detail-tabs.spec.ts` was un-quarantined by slice 276). **New specs:** `web/e2e/tenant-switch.spec.ts` (AC-2 — the security-critical multi-tenant flow that had zero coverage: switcher render for an N-tenant user, the switch carrying real tenant scope through the RFC 8693 token-exchange, and the single-tenant hide rule that is itself the UI-edge isolation guarantee) and `web/e2e/evidence-push-e2e.spec.ts` (AC-3 — the CLI push → ingest → BFF read → UI display chain, asserting the push Receipt's content hash is the hash the UI renders). Both follow the established `route.fulfill` mock convention (anti-criterion P0-4). **Quarantine triage (AC-4)** of the 6 active `test.skip()` guards: `auth-open-redirect.spec.ts` un-skipped (the `!HAS_BEARER` guard was vestigial post-slice-201 `globalSetup`; the spec body was already fixed by slice 161); `risks-create.spec.ts`, `risks-create-control-link.spec.ts`, `audits-create.spec.ts` un-skipped and rewritten from their slice-082-era commented-contract bodies into live mocked specs — the rewrite corrects stale assertions (empty-title is now gated client-side by native `required`, not a server bounce; `mitigate` requires a linked control client-side) rather than green-washing the obsolete shapes; `bff-cookie-production-build.spec.ts` + `logo-render-production-build.spec.ts` re-quarantined with a documented justification (they assert standalone-build-only regressions and no CI job boots the standalone server — forcing them green against the dev server would assert nothing), blocked on new slice 387. **Type: AFK** (with quarantine JUDGMENT); decisions log `docs/audit-log/351-e2e-critical-flow-gap-audit-decisions.md`. Spillovers filed: 387 (production-build standalone CI harness), 388 (board-pack export e2e), 389 (multi-tenant JWT harness + real-RLS cross-tenant-leak spec). Slice doc: `docs/issues/351-e2e-critical-flow-gap-audit.md`.

- **feat(a11y): slice 363 — admin form Checkbox primitive + error-association pattern (WCAG 2.4.7 + 3.3.1 + 3.3.2).** Two related a11y gaps in admin form pages closed under one slice. **Gap 1 (WCAG 2.4.7 Focus Visible):** the raw `<input type="checkbox">` at `web/app/admin/tenants/page.tsx` was replaced with a new shadcn-themed `<Checkbox>` primitive (`web/components/ui/checkbox.tsx`) wrapping `@base-ui/react/checkbox` (the existing dep family — no new top-level dep per slice 277-style discipline). The primitive carries the same `focus-visible:ring-3 focus-visible:ring-ring/50` ring shape as the `<Input>` and `<Button>` primitives, restoring the focus indicator that Tailwind 4 preflight strips from raw inputs. **Gap 2 (WCAG 3.3.1 + 3.3.2):** every admin form (`web/app/admin/tenants/page.tsx`, `web/app/admin/super-admins/page.tsx`, `web/app/admin/api-keys/page.tsx`, `web/app/login/page.tsx`) now wires `aria-describedby` on each input to the submit-error `<Alert>`'s stable id WHEN the Alert is mounted (idiom: `const errorId = error ?
